chapters.summary: Council staff calls the room to order and reminds attendees to silence devices and not approach the dais.
chapters.title: Staff calls hearing to order

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Quiet down.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Quiet down, please.

chapters.summary: Council staff introduces the joint executive budget hearing of the Committees on Finance, Higher Education, and Education.
chapters.title: Staff introduces joint budget hearing

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to today's New York City Council hearing, executive budget hearing from the committees on finance jointly with the committees on higher education and the committee on education.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this point, I'd like to remind everyone to please silence their electronic devices and at no point is anyone to approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chairs, we're ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee welcomes attendees to the ninth day of FY27 Executive Budget Hearings, introducing the CUNY portion and co-chair Rita Joseph, and noting council members in attendance.
chapters.title: Chair Lee welcomes attendees to FY27 CUNY budget hearing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, good morning everyone and welcome to the ninth day of the FY27 Executive Budget Hearings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Linda Lee and today's hearing will begin with City University of New York followed by Department of Education and I'm very pleased to be joined by my colleague and chair of the Committee on Higher Education, Councilmember Rita Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we have been joined by Council Members Wong, Lewis, Marano, and Marte.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And welcome, Chancellor Felix Metros Rodriguez and your team.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you all for joining us today to answer our questions.

chapters.summary: Lee presents CUNY's proposed FY27 budget of $1.57 billion, noting the $21.1 million increase from the preliminary plan and the $15 million in restored operating support.
chapters.title: Chair Lee outlines CUNY FY27 executive budget

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On May 12, 2026, the admin released the executive financial plan for FY26 to 2030 with a proposed FY27 budget of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The City University of New York's proposed FY27 budget of $1.57 billion represents

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 1.3% of the administration's proposed budget in the executive plan, and this is an increase of $21.1 million, or 1.4%, from the $1.55 billion originally budgeted in the FY27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And this increase is mainly the result of $15 million in city funding for operating support that was restored and baselined starting in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of April 2026, CUNY's pedagogical headcount was the same as their fiscal 26 budgeted headcount, and their non-pedagogical headcount was four positions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: than their FY26 budgeted headcount.

chapters.summary: Lee calls on the Mayor to add and baseline $22.5 million for CUNY retention programs including ACE, ASAP, and Reconnect, expressing disappointment these were not included in the executive plan.
chapters.title: Chair Lee urges baselining of CUNY ACE, ASAP, and Reconnect

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In the Council's preliminary budget response, we called on the Mayor to add and baseline a total of $22.5 million to CUNY's budget to invest in essential programs that ensure student retention, such as CUNY ACE, CUNY ASAP, and CUNY Reconnect,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: that they remain in place.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: While we so often look at recruitment to CUNY colleges, programs that encourage people to return and to stay at a higher education, returning to stay in higher education must be fully funded and baselined.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We were disappointed to see that these programs were not included in the plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And while we are happy to see the administration baseline operating support for CUNY, we urge the administration to go further.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: CUNY is New York's gateway to affordable higher education, but this opportunity is only as strong as the supports that allow students to reach the finish line.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The evidence-based programs at stake have demonstrated clear, measurable impact on retention and graduation rates, and students are counting on them.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I look forward to hearing more from the Chancellor today about where that funding stands and what is needed to protect it.

chapters.summary: Chair Rita Joseph delivers opening remarks welcoming attendees to the CUNY FY27 executive budget hearing and outlining the budget totals including personal service and OTPS spending.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph opens CUNY budget hearing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And at this time, I want to now turn it over to my co-chair for this hearing, Chair Councilmember Joseph, for her opening statement.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Rita Joseph, Chair of the Committee on Higher Education.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Welcome to today's hearing on the City's Fiscal 2027 Executive Budget for the City University of New York.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: CUNY fiscal 2027 executive budget totals $1.57 billion, including $1.12 billion in personal service to support 4,289

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: pedagogical positions and 1,735 non-pedagogical positions.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The university's budget includes $455 million in other personnel service spending in this executive plan.

chapters.summary: Joseph discusses the $21.5 million in one-time savings from community college supply reductions and the $15 million in baseline funding for academic advisement and wellness programs, expressing disappointment that ACE, ASAP, and Reconnect were not restored.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on CUNY savings and missing program restorations

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: One-time saving of $21.5 million was added in the fiscal 2027

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: from spending reductions on supplies and other materials at community colleges, of which CUNY is still determining exactly how to allocate these savings across community colleges.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Although the executive plan also includes baseline funding, $15 million to support academic advisement, mental health and wellness,

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: career readiness, and workforce preparation program, it is disappointing that the plan did not restore any of the essential programs like CUNY Reconnect, ACE, and ASAP.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: There is no doubt that these programs have proven to be effective in bringing individuals back to pursue a degree as well as providing financial and academic support to unlock higher education.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: It is unacceptable that we have to fight every year for the administration to restore funding for these programs.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We are eager to hear why CUNY, Reconnect, ACE, and ASAP are not funded beyond fiscal 2026 and whether CUNY has included them in their fiscal 2027 budget priorities to OMB.

chapters.summary: Joseph emphasizes the barriers students face including food and housing insecurity, childcare, and transportation costs, and calls for investment in programs that increase graduation rates and address disparities between campuses.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on student barriers and graduation pathways

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: As we're moving closer to

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: adopting the fiscal 2027 budget, we must remember that many students continue to face barriers such as food and housing insecurities, childcare needs, and the cost of transportation.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I said it at our preliminary budget hearing and I will emphasize it again.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We must invest in programs that clear pathways to increase graduation rates.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We must also find disparities that exist in programs between campuses and work to address them.

chapters.summary: Joseph raises concerns about CUNY building maintenance including leaking roofs and non-working elevators, and asks about plans to address critical capital repairs at community colleges with the additional $50 million in FY27.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on CUNY community college capital repairs

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The executive capital commitment plan include an additional $50 million in fiscal 2027 to help bring CUNY community colleges back in a state of good repair.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We are interested to learn about the university's plan to address critical capital repairs at community colleges.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: It is well recorded that many CUNY buildings are in desperate need of maintenance and repair for leaking roofs, non-working elevators.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: It is my hope that CUNY prioritize capital projects to ensure equity.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: As there are so many needed repairs across the CUNY system.

chapters.summary: Joseph criticizes CUNY's internal process for responding to committee budget follow-up questions, noting staff waited over three weeks for answers and demanding improved timelines.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on CUNY response timeline for budget inquiries

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Lastly, it was brought to my attention that CUNY needs to improve its internal process to respond in a timely manner to the committee's budget-related follow-up questions.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I should say that it is unacceptable that staff has to wait over three weeks to receive CUNY's answers on budget inquiries.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Information is key in keeping this committee informed, and this timeline to respond to follow-up questions must improve, as we do want to remain good partners.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Before I begin, I'd like to thank the finance staff, Ali Stowe.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Florentine Cabaret, and Caitlin O'Hagan for preparing this hearing, as well as committee staff Julia Goldsmith-Pinkham and Regina Paul for their support.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Finally, I'd like to thank my staff, including Chief of Staff Giovanni Piquan and my Legislative and Budget Director Benjamin Atkinson.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I will now turn it back over to Chair Lee.

chapters.summary: Lee announces that public testimonies for all hearings will be held on one day, Wednesday June 10th at 9:30 AM, and encourages sign-ups.
chapters.title: Chair Lee announces public testimony date

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just before we begin, another reminder, as I've been saying this at all the hearings, that we are going to have all of our public testimonies on one day, June 10th, Wednesday, this coming Wednesday, starting at 9.30.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So if you know folks that would like to testify for the public hearing portion, please make sure that they sign up, and we look forward to hearing from the public on their feedback.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So again, June 10th, Wednesday, 9.30 a.m.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel Brian Sarfo swears in Chancellor Matos Rodriguez and Vice Chancellors Alvaro, Batista, and Gardner before testimony begins.
chapters.title: Swearing in of CUNY witnesses

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with that, I will turn it over to Brian Sarfo, our committee counsel, to swear in the witnesses.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to Council Member questions, Chancellor Matos Rodriguez?

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Vice Chancellor Alvaro, Vice Chancellor Batista, and Vice Chancellor Gottner.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Chancellor Matos Rodriguez delivers opening testimony, thanking the Council and highlighting that 72% of CUNY students attend tuition-free and 77% graduate without debt, with enrollment growing nearly 4% in the fall.
chapters.title: Chancellor Matos Rodriguez on CUNY enrollment growth and affordability

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair Lee and Chair Joseph and the members of the City Council Committees on Finance and Higher Education.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I am Felix Matos Rodriguez, the Chancellor of the City University of New York, and I'm joined by the CUNY Dream Team of University Provost Alvaro, COO Batista, and CFO Gardner.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I want to thank the City Council for your steadfast commitment to CUNY.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Your investment sustained what has long defined this university, brought access to affordable,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: high-quality education for one of the most diverse student bodies in the nation, and a pathway to social and economic mobility for generations of New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Your support has been essential to delivering strong returns for the more than 400,000 degree-seeking and continuing education students we serve,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: allowing 72% of CUNY students to attend tuition-free and 77% to graduate without debt.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I just want to give you a warning that I'm going to be reading a shorter version of the submitted testimony that you have a copy of, and I assume that you'll be happy about that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Our affordable tuition and diverse offerings and support systems have helped fuel enrollment gains.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: This fall, enrollment grew nearly 4% over last fall, the third consecutive year of growth.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Since the fall of 2022, enrollment has jumped 9%, nearly 21,000 additional students.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: This increase is most evident in our community colleges.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Enrollment at CUNY's seven community colleges has rebounded from the pandemic, with an additional 9,500 students welcomed over the last three years, an 18% increase.

chapters.summary: The Chancellor details community college enrollment rebounding 18% over three years with 9,500 additional students, driven by TAP expansion and the Reconnect initiative, along with personalized recruitment letters to graduating seniors.
chapters.title: Chancellor on community college enrollment and recruitment strategies

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Notably, during the same period, part-time enrollment surged 37% at our community colleges.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: This growth is driven by the expansion of the state's TAP and by the Reconnect initiative launched to bring adult learners back to college.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Community colleges have also seen the highest retention rate increase, 7%, since we have began tracking this in 1990.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: This growth has also been fueled by our ongoing efforts to actively recruit the next generation of CUNY students from the New York City public schools.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: For the past three years, we have sent personalized letters

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: to all graduating seniors, and more recently to their caregivers offering admission and waiving application fees.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Once students earn their degree, we are committed to making sure it opens doors to a career.

chapters.summary: The Chancellor describes CUNY Beyond, a career readiness program aiming to triple paid internships and increase employer recruitment by 20%, currently at four campuses with plans to expand to five more.
chapters.title: Chancellor on CUNY Beyond career readiness program

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Last fall, we launched CUNY Beyond, an ambitious plan

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: that invests career readiness into every aspect of the undergraduate experience and smooths the transition from classroom to the workplace.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The program aims to triple the number of students in paid internships and increase employer recruitment by 20%, ultimately driving a projected $3.3 billion in future wage growth

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: that translates into increased tax revenue and greater economic mobility.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We started CUNY Beyond with four campuses.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: With $6.8 million in state support in next year's budget, we are expanding it to five more this summer.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We are looking to the council for support for two more community colleges

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: in the next phase.

chapters.summary: The Chancellor highlights CUNY's role as New York's primary talent engine, preparing one-third of new NYC public school teachers with a 17% higher five-year retention rate, and its early childhood education pipeline with 3,600 students across 50 programs.
chapters.title: Chancellor on teacher pipeline and early childhood education

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: These efforts build on one of CUNY's redefining roles, serving as New York's primary talent engine for many of the essential sectors that keep the city running.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We prepare more than one-third of all new New York City public school teachers each year, and more than 40% of the students of color in New York State.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I am proud that city teachers from CUNY have a 17% higher

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: five-year retention rate than those from other institutions.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: This CUNY teacher pipeline is essential to addressing the statewide teacher shortage, and this year, for example, we tripled the number of CUNY students in the Teaching Fellows program.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: More than 15,000 students have participated in the program since its inception, including the current school's chancellor, Kamar Samuels.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: A critical priority for Governor Hochul, Mayor Mandani, Speaker Menin, and this council is the shared goals of universal child care.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: CUNY is uniquely positioned to meet the moment.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The university already enrolls more than 3,600 early childhood education students across 50 academic programs, making us the primary engine for training the educators needed

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: to make this effort successful.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We're expanding that pipeline through the statewide New York Early Childhood Professional Development Institute, and we're also launching new programs in early childhood leadership and administration, addressing a critical shortage in the field.

chapters.summary: The Chancellor discusses CUNY research impacts including an AI model for breast cancer detection at City College, the NYC Urban Heat Portal at Hunter, and the FloodNet flood monitoring partnership with NYU.
chapters.title: Chancellor on CUNY research and climate initiatives

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Turning to another vital area, CUNY's research enterprise does not simply advance knowledge.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: It has a real impact on the quality of life for millions of New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: For example, faculty researchers at City College, working with Memorial Sloan Kettering, have developed a groundbreaking AI model that can

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: detect breast cancer earlier and more accurately.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And projects like the New York City Urban Heat Portal at Hunter College and our partnership with the city and NYU on the FloodNet real-time flood monitoring program are helping address climate emergencies.

chapters.summary: The Chancellor describes the Constructive Dialogue Institute collaboration funded by the City Council, the largest deployment of CDI's model at any university system, and the Center for Inclusivity and Equal Opportunity.
chapters.title: Chancellor on Constructive Dialogue Institute and inclusion efforts

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: At a time of increasing polarization and division, CUNY is helping members of our community develop the skills to engage across differences with empathy and respect.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Through our trailblazing collaboration with the Constructive Dialogue Institute, largely funded by this city council, we are building a culture of constructive engagement.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: This ambitious partnership represents the largest deployment of the CDI's model and the first initiative of its kind at a university system.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Complementing this work is our Center for Inclusivity and Equal Opportunity, which ensures that reports of discrimination and harassment are addressed promptly and fairly, and that all members of our community have access to the support that they need.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: That commitment to safety, dignity, and inclusion extends beyond our campuses and into the communities that CUNY serves.

chapters.summary: The Chancellor highlights ACE program outcomes doubling three-year completion rates, CUNY Accommodate increasing disability services enrollment 25% to 13,800 students, and CUNY CARES in the Bronx helping over 1,000 students enroll in SNAP.
chapters.title: Chancellor on student success programs and food security

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: At a time of heightened uncertainty for immigrant communities, the Council's support for CUNY citizenship now is more important than ever so that we can continue serving the thousands of New Yorkers who depend on us.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Our proven models of student success

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: continue to set national standards.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The ACE program more than doubles three-year completion rates for associate degree students, while ACE participants have achieved four-year graduation rates 12.4 higher than non-participant peers.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We're also leveraging technology to improve accessibility.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: CUNY Accommodate has streamlined disability services, leading to a 25% increase in enrollment for these services, now reaching a record 13,800 students.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The CUNY CARES model in the Bronx, in its first two years, has helped over 1,000 Bronx students enroll in SNAP.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Collectively bringing an estimated $2.96 million into their household budgets to combat food insecurity.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: This work is complemented by CUNY's system-wide network of campus food pantries, supported in part by the City Council's Food Insecurity Program.

chapters.summary: The Chancellor reports 42 capital projects completed and $555 million spent on completed and ongoing projects, then turns to the FY27 executive budget thanking the Mayor for fully funding the PSC-CUNY labor contract and baseline funding $50 million.
chapters.title: Chancellor on capital projects and FY27 executive budget

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Over the past year, CUNY brought 42 capital projects to total completion.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We have also spent a total of $555 million towards both completed and ongoing projects,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: representing meaningful progress in the ongoing effort to modernize and preserve critical infrastructure across the university.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: In my submitted testimony, you will find a list of notable projects brought to completion this year.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I will now turn to the

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Mayor's Executive Budget for Fiscal Year 2027.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Let me begin by thanking Mayor Mandani for fully funding the cost of the PSC-CUNY labor contract and completing the funding for health insurance in the preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Additionally, I'd like to thank the Mayor for providing baseline funding for the $50 million originally included in last year's executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: On the capital side, we're thankful for the $50.8 million toward needed state of good repair work, in addition to the $7.1 million from the borough presidents.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We ask that you support the inclusion of the priorities listed in the submitted testimony and in our green sheet.

chapters.summary: The Chancellor details the $1.7 billion capital request including $384 million for community colleges, asks for Council support for the remaining $100 million in community college capital funds, and concludes that CUNY's success is NYC's success.
chapters.title: Chancellor on community college capital needs and conclusion

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: On the capital side, community colleges continue to face significant infrastructural challenges that directly impact student learning, environments, operational continuity, and sustainability.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The fiscal year 26-27 capital request reflected the urgency of these needs and the necessity of sustained investments in aging facilities across the system.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The $1.7 billion request in the 26-27 capital plan

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: included approximately $384 million for community colleges to support critical asset preservation initiatives and strategic growth and modernization projects.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: These investments are essential to maintaining facilities.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Capable of meeting the evolving needs

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: of students, faculty, and workforce programs.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We ask for your support with the remaining amount from our $100 million asked for capital funds for community colleges.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: To conclude, CUNY's success is New York City's success.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We have seen the impact that targeted investments has on enrollment, student success,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: and career readiness.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: With your continued support, CUNY will remain the nation's greatest engine of social mobility and a national model for public higher education.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you for the chance to testify, and I look forward to your questions.

chapters.summary: Lee begins Q&A asking how the $15 million in restored operating support was allocated across community college campuses in FY26 and whether the same split will apply in FY27. COO Batista provides the breakdown by campus.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions $15M restoration campus allocation

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much, and definitely appreciate the abbreviated testimony.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I love it, and we have it all here, which is good, too.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've been joined by Councilmember Brewer and Epstein, as well as Areola on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So really quickly, I'm going to try to go through these so I can pass it on to the chair and our members.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For the FY27 executive budget, there's a restoration and baselining, as we mentioned, of $15 million starting in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The funding is a restoration from FY26.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How was the funding used in FY26, and what was the college campus allocation breakdown, if you have that?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And will the funding be split between campuses in the same way for FY27?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So let me defer to our CFO here for the answer.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Ken Gardner
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Ken Gardner
utterances.text: The money, the money,

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: $15 million was spread out among the community colleges.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: You want me to read out the numbers to you?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If you can, yes.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: No, no problem.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: BMCC, 4.6 million.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Bronx Community College, 1.4.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Gutman, about 270,000.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Hostos, a million.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Kingsborough, 2.5.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: LaGuardia, 3 million.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: And Queensborough, 2 million.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect, okay.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: I'm rounding those numbers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry?

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: I'm rounding those numbers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, okay, got it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect, thank you.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how baselining the $15 million allows CUNY to expand programs and free up funding. The Chancellor explains baselining provides predictability for personnel investments and procurement contracts beyond one year.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on baselining benefits for CUNY planning

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then, is this funding and the baseline, how will it allow you to actually expand maybe perhaps on other programs and free up that funding?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, I mean, one of the main things is, and this is a recurring theme you'll hear from us throughout the hearing, baselining allows predictability and to make investments, particularly on the personnel side, that are not just one year off, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Right, so many.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So for many programs, and we'll be talking about this as you talked about Reconnect and ASAP and all those programs, our capacity to bring in individuals who are going to be able to be there for the long term allows us to plan better and to be more effective in the services.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Also, if we need to procure contracts, you know, you can now do that just with money that's going to be there for one year.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So they'd be extremely helpful to us as we move forward.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right, so more planning purposes, which hopefully will help you plan better and then also figure out staffing, all of the other operational needs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $3.4 million for the city governance program with the Campaign Finance Board, including how many fellows will be selected and the selection process. The Chancellor says they will follow up with numbers.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on city governance fellowship program

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: FY27 executive budget also includes $3.4 million only in intra-city funding as a new need for the city governance program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: With the Campaign Finance Board, and the program will support fellows gaining work experience in civic engagement, which is great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How many fellows will be selected, and what will be the selection process for that?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Who has the numbers for that, Ken, or?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we need to get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I mean, the great thing about that program, too, is that it's consistent with our approach to give students a lot more paid and earned opportunities, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: It's money in their pockets, and they're also advancing their education while serving the city, but we can get you the numbers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And for the host organizations, do you know approximately how many you're going to be partnering with and if you're going to also anticipate extra funding in the out years for this program?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Again, the more funding we have, the more students that we can include, and the more that we can partner with other groups, I can get you a list of the organizations to be precise.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I don't know if you break it down this way, but if you could sort of give us an average of like how much the cost would be per student because, you know, and let us know what your, I guess, how much you would want to expand it to, which would also include the budget.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $25 million tuition adjustment reduction and CUNY's projected vs actual enrollment for FY25 and FY26. Batista reports enrollment trending upward with 179,000 FTE and 239,000 total enrollment in FY26.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on tuition adjustment and enrollment trends

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: There's also a $25 million reduction in FY26 only for tuition adjustment related to student enrollment.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What was CUNY's projected student enrollment versus the actual for FY25, which I know is going back a little bit, and FY26?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Can you have those?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then also just whether you anticipate an increase or decrease in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: So the 26 enrollment on FTE basis is at the senior colleges 128,000, at the junior colleges, at the community colleges, pardon me, 51,000 for a total of 179.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: That's about 4,000 in total.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Above the 4400 above the FY25 level.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: That's on the FTE basis.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: On a total basis, those numbers are 26, 164 at the senior colleges, 75 at the community colleges.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: For a total of 239, which is 1,700 higher than the enrollment in 25.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the trends are increasing, which is good to see.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: The enrollment trend is ticking upward.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes, that's good.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Amen.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether any particular CUNY schools are struggling more with recruitment and enrollment. The Chancellor notes all schools experienced growth except Queens College, which has developed a new enrollment strategy.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on schools struggling with enrollment

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are there any particular CUNY schools that are, in comparison, struggling more than others in terms of recruitment, enrollment, and are there resources that are needed for those schools?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So, I mean, I'm going from last year.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We're still looking at how the full 26 is going to shape.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: But the main enrollment growth was driven by the community colleges, right, which is

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Good for us because a lot of those students are going to end up transferring to the four-year schools, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So that is a good trend now.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: It's going to be a good trend moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Going from memory, I think last fall, all of the schools experienced enrollment growth, maybe with the exception of Queens College.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And I know that they put together a strategy to advance enrollment.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We've also, from the central office, have been working with some of the schools that had enrollment challenges

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: and tighter fiscal scenarios to support them with investment because clearly enrollment is the main driver of fiscal health for all of the campuses, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $21.5 million one-time reduction for revenue re-estimate and whether it was part of the chief savings officer exercise. Batista confirms it was designed to not impact campuses and was offset by additional state resources.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on $21.5M savings plan reductions

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And FY27 executive budget includes a one-time reduction of $21.5 million for a revenue re-estimate.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Was this part of the exercise that you went through as part of the chief savings officer exercise?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Was that?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how was the amount determined, if that was the case?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: For reduction questions, we go to Ken.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I get all the good news.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: The

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: The $21 million in savings relates to the savings program that we were asked to identify.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: And it's the way that CUNY is...

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Savings is structured.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: All of those things take place in 27.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Is there, I'm sorry, was there more to your question?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, just how it was determined, how the reductions were determined and how, like what you used, if you could give us categories of where those reductions are.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: We are working through the details on how this will play out, but the

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: additional state resources that we expect to receive.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: This reduction was very purposely designed so that we would not impact the campuses.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I mean, yes, that is the main goal that we definitely wanted to push on the council side was that no cuts or services just to make sure.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know some of them probably came from vacancies, but...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Were there any proposals that you submitted to OMB for the savings plan that were rejected or not accepted?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about current enrollment in CUNY ASAP and ACE and retention rates. Provost Alvero reports 39,299 students with a 65.6% one-year retention rate, approximately twice as high as non-ASAP students.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on ACE and ASAP enrollment and retention

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, moving to ACE and ASAP.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So CUNY, the ASAP's program and the ACE program is great, and I'm glad you included more details in the testimony.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How many students are currently enrolled in CUNY ASAP and ACE, and what is the retention rate?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I'm going to let our provost answer that.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: So the finalized numbers for academic year 24-25 is worth 39,299 students, and the one-year retention rate was 65.6%.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how does the retention rate differ for students not enrolled in CUNY ASAP or ACE?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: That's about twice as high compared to non-ASAP students.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, wow.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And graduation rate versus similar students not enrolled?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: The two-year graduation rate is 16.5%, and the three-year graduation rate, which is the main metric, is 34.6%.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know that this hasn't been baselined yet, which obviously, as we mentioned, impacts the ability to sort of plan and for a new cohort of students.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so when do you usually recruit?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For that program to enroll in terms of CUNY ACE?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: We are usually recruiting right at the very point of admission.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: So students apply in the fall and get decisions throughout the spring semester.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And as they are receiving admissions letters, that's when the recruitment starts.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And to your point, Chair, the recurrent funds is not just the obvious personnel.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: You're making a commitment to bring students in for two, three years, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we need to make sure that we have the funding to be able to support them at that point when they come in, too.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: That's another reason why the baselining on this case and also on ACE is so important.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, no, definitely.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes, planning purposes wise, for sure.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about CUNY Beyond funding levels and sources. Batista reports $2.2 million in FY26 and $12.5 million in FY27, combining state, city, and philanthropic funding following the ASAP model.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on CUNY Beyond funding levels

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For CUNY Beyond, what is the current funding level for CUNY Beyond in FY26 and 27?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what are the funding sources for the program?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I know I saw that.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: I can jump in.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: The budget level for CUNY Beyond is about 2.2 in 26 and about 12.5 in 27.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, 12.5 in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And that is just a combination, I mean, of...

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: State and city funding, we also, similarly to the strategy that we have with ASAP, if you remember the history of ASAP, we use a lot of philanthropic

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: dollars to establish the model, establish the success of the model, and then make the case to you and to the state that it was a worthy investment.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we're doing similarly, we're aggressively recruiting funds philanthropically to be able to support that work, particularly some of the work that we do at the central office,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: hoping that we can prove that this is the right thing to do for our students and then hopefully make that case.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we're sort of following a similar strategy of how ASAP was supported, structured, and validated.

chapters.summary: Lee asks which schools are included in CUNY Beyond and how many students are impacted. The Chancellor lists the first four schools and five expansion schools, providing student projection numbers for each campus.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on CUNY Beyond expansion schools and student counts

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how many, because I know that you said there was an extra 6.8 million in FY27 for CUNY Beyond, which would allow you to expand to five schools.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So can you list which schools are included in CUNY Beyond?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So the first four were Lehman, BMCC, Hunter, and LaGuardia.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We are expanding to...

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: City College, and I think Queensborough, Kingsborough, and York, and those other ones, and Baruch.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And Baruch.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And Baruch, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So the new ones are City, Baruch, York, QCC, and QCC.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Queensborough and Kingsborough.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And we would love additional support to be able to bring in two more community colleges from the council.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That would be great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We gotta look at that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and how many students does the program currently impact?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then I guess with the additional five, what does it expand to?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So, we have the numbers for BMCC is 15,126,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: at Hunter 12,194, at LaGuardia 8,936, Lehman 7,990, and the projections for

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Baruch is 4,000.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: 1,090 for CCNY, 3,259 for Queensborough, 1,969 and Queensborough, 2,270.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Oh, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Kingsborough is 1,969 and Queensborough 2,270.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And then York is in the next page.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: York has 1,062.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about vacancy rates by college and how enrollment changes have impacted headcount. The Chancellor says the most immediate impact is on full-time faculty and adjunct hiring, and promises to provide specific vacancy data by campus.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on CUNY headcount and vacancy rates

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then just lastly, headcount and vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The vacancy rate as of April is 0.7% for pedagogical positions and 16.4% for non-pedagogical positions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Which colleges are facing the highest vacancy rates?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just while you're looking for that, if you could let us know how the changes in student enrollment have impacted the headcount as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That'd be good to know.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The headcount of faculty positions and...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right, the vacancy rate, yeah, in the positions.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I mean, the most immediate impact tends to be initially the employment of either full-time faculty or adjuncts, right, depending on the growth of opening more sections, and that would be the most immediate case of that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes, if you could let us know which colleges are facing the highest vacancy rates, and then also just in terms of the current vacancies and how they're impacting, or if at all, if they're impacting course offerings for the students, that would be great if you could let us know as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Council Members Wilson and Jay Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with that, I'm going to pass it off to Chair Joseph.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks why there is a $25 million tuition adjustment reduction if enrollment is increasing. CFO Gardner explains it is to match actuals to budget levels.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on tuition adjustment vs enrollment growth

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Good to see you, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Same here.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: In your testimony, you mentioned that enrollment grew nearly 4% over the last fall, mostly in community colleges.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Basically, post-COVID enrollment jumped up to 9%.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Can you further explain the tuition adjustment of the reduced $25 million if enrollment is increasing?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: You want to take that?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I mean, because that's a, those are the projections the city makes, right, based on enrollment.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So, you want to go?

utterances.speaker: Ken Gardner
utterances.text: No, it's fine, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Ken Gardner
utterances.text: I mean, this is just to match the actuals to the budget levels, right?

utterances.speaker: Ken Gardner
utterances.text: So we have to adjust the budget for the actual expenditure on that category.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about the $50 million for community college state of good repair, how CUNY assessed campuses needing repairs, and which colleges will receive funding. COO Batista explains the prioritization process including facility assessments and distribution across schools.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on $50M community college repair allocation

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The fiscal 2026 to 2023 capital commitment plan include $50 million in fiscal 2027, only to bring community college to the state of good repair.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The $50 million would be matched by the state for a total of $100 million.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We spoke at the preliminary budget hearing about how CUNY assessed the campuses that need immediate repairs.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Now that the funding has been allocated, will you be performing any additional assessments?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And what colleges will be allocated funding for repairs?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: In April, CUNY students and staff rallied for the needs for repairs, citing issues such as broken elevators, mold, flooding, among others.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How will CUNY ensure these problems are addressed quickly?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: You want to take that, Dr.?

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Yeah, sure.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Let me first start out by saying, obviously, we're very happy with the $50 million.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: It matches to $100.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: As you recall, we asked for $100 million.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: As the Chancellor alluded in his testimony, you know, our community college is struggling from an issue of deferred maintenance and the state of good repair.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: As I stated at our hearing, there are multiple ways.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: We look at how to prioritize first the campus size and physical infrastructure, the age and condition of the facilities, the state of good repair and deferred maintenance, and then campus needs and strategic priorities.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: And all of that, it is really grounded around a couple of areas.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Number one is grounded around the fact that we have done what we call a facility assessment of our facilities, and we know exactly what

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: systems are going to come offline at a certain time, you know, in six months or a year.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: And based on those, knowing those things, then we allocate resources to make sure that we don't have any problems.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: In the winter months by, you know, system going offline because it's not, we didn't repair it at the time that we needed to do that.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Furthermore, what we also try to do is really try to really focus on tackling what we call

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: bathrooms and a whole approach of just dealing with bathrooms and making sure that we have or dealing with

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: trying to package everything together so we could get the whole issue of economy of scale and trying to do a lot more with less.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: And then lastly, I think it's really focused on which other vital systems that we know need our most immediate attention, and those are the ones that we prioritize.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: And then we try to distribute those funds across all the schools, so every school at least gets something.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: But some schools get more than others based on the age of their infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: When you do do that assessment, would you be able to share with the committee what that looks like and what's the timeline?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I know that, for example, City Tech, their library ceiling fell.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Is that one of your priorities?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: It was on the news.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So when you do decide on and assess those needs and how you plan on addressing those issues, would you share with the committee what your timeline is and what schools did you prioritize and put first?

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: I'm not sure what was on the news was actually what actually happened, but let's...

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Too bad I can't play the clip for you, but...

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Yeah, no, I understand that.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: But not everything we hear in the news is actually factual.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: But the fact is that we...

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: We will definitely be able to provide you that information.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So the news is lying on CUNY?

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Excuse me?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Is the news lying on you?

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: It hasn't in the first time.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Oh.

chapters.summary: Joseph raises concerns about elevator repairs and accessibility at CUNY campuses, asking how CUNY ensures accessibility in older buildings and historic landmarks. The Chancellor says accessibility is prioritized and campuses work around limitations through class scheduling.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on elevator repairs and campus accessibility

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We've also heard that many students about the needs for elevator repairs at CUNY's campuses.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Elevators are essential for accessibility.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: As it's updating the adaptive technology, how does CUNY ensure accessibility as campuses, especially in older buildings?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: If a building cannot be made accessible due to historic landmark status, what steps do CUNY take to ensure students can?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Require accessibility modification and you know take full advantage of coming to your university because of mobility issues

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, first of all, we prioritize all that has to do with accessibility and compliance, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: It's one of the criterias that the CEO, Batista, mentioned in his comments.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So that's one of the things that we prioritize in determining what critical maintenance might be required.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And then the second part, then we work with the campuses, particularly in the ones that might be older, right, in which it might be unfeasible to do that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And then they work with the campuses to make sure that the classes that are taught for students that might require that might not need to be part of that building if you have staff that need those things.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I mean, in our vision of the world, we'd love to have all the buildings to be fully accessible.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: But I think the campuses work very hard to make sure that if we do have some that are not, that clearly nobody's missing out because of that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And then the scheduling of classes and the scheduling of offices, you know, and I can tell you having been president of two campuses is one of the things that you figure out while you work on the things where you can actually.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Change the conditions and build the ramps and do the changes that are needed.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks how CUNY leverages adaptive technology for accessibility. Provost Alvero describes glasses with interpreters, VR headsets, and the CUNY Accommodate system meeting accommodation requests within two weeks.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on adaptive technology for accessibility

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How do CUNY leverage adaptive technology to ensure accessibility as well?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, I'm going to turn it over to our provost.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: One of the big items that you'll see in our green sheet is additional support for all the work that we're doing with students with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So go to the heart of that question.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: But I'll let her talk about CUNY Accommodation and some of the things that we've been doing.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: We leverage and are exploring and are actually having quite a lot of fun with the adaptive technologies.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: The new technologies are pretty sophisticated now.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: We have each of the campuses, there are glasses that have an interpreter for those who are hard of hearing and we're exploring virtual reality and VR headsets.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: But we have, we constantly meet the needs of every single student through accommodate, as we've mentioned before.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: We have been able to expedite from the moment of request to accommodation granted.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Our goal was two weeks, and we met the goal and exceed that within two weeks.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Because we have access to some adaptive technology, we are able to provide that for our students.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And if we don't, we immediately meet the needs and find resources that are available.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So the wait is two weeks, and you can borrow among the university system across?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: In case you don't have it?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks if CUNY has a building accessibility rating system similar to NYC Public Schools. The Chancellor says CUNY does not but would be interested in learning about the DOE model.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on building accessibility rating system

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: New York City Public Schools developed a building accessibility rating system to determine how accessible a building is and where they could improve.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Does CUNY have a similar model to determine accessibility needs?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I don't think we do.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: You don't.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So love to learn more.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: But you would eventually probably develop one?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I mean, we'd love to look at the model of the deal if it makes sense for us.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: It includes an assessment and how do you plan it.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: It's really good.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about the early childhood apprenticeship program budget, enrollment, and outreach. The Chancellor reports 30 apprentices currently enrolled, with recruitment through the statewide network, campus students, and child care centers.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on early childhood apprenticeship program

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Let's talk about our favorite topic, early childhood education.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: That's everybody's favorite topic.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: CUNY is requesting an additional $6.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Million dollars to fund early childhood education program including the apprenticeship program the pro the child development associate the CDA program

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: the CDA completion pro project and b2 teacher certification support CUNY is requesting three million over three years to expand the early childhood

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: apprentices program to recruit 200 new professionals what is the current budget of the program for fiscal 2026 and 2027

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And how many apprentices do you have currently enrolled?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The first, which program?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The apprenticeship program.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The apprenticeship program for your CDA.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: This is...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know we have this number.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So, I mean, the cost for year one is a million in what we have projected.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So I assume that we're using that criteria to the numbers that we have now.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And how many apprentices are currently enrolled in your program?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And how do apprentices apply and what type of outreach does CUNY perform for this program?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, so we use a number of things.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: First, we have the statewide network, which is sort of the Cadillac of working with all the individuals that are working in early childhood.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: That's a key method for us to be able to recruit.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We use our own network of the students on the campuses.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I think I mentioned the testimony.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We have about 3,600 undergrad students studying that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We have great relationships also with a lot of the nonprofits and some of the providers that work with us.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And, you know, we have also our 17 child care centers that are hubs of talented people spreading the word, too.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So plenty of ways to getting good people in.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: That's wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How long is the apprenticeship?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How long is the program?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I need to get back on that.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And while the students are in your program, what type of support do you have?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: That was 30 students.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How many?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: That I know.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I need to get back to you on the duration of the program.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about the CDA credit-bearing program cost per student and whether funds allow free certification. Provost Alvero reports $9,000 per student including tuition, fees, and career advisement, with 105 students enrolled and 110 graduates last year.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on CDA bilingual program costs and outcomes

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay, okay.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And what support do you provide once a student is enrolled in that program?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, one of the great things that the program gets to do is that they're immediately paired with a provider, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So you're building up first networks for the students in terms of employability, getting a sense of what that experience in the workplace is.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And then, you know, I think that we are becoming the go-to as all the scaling up happens for people to come seeking talent.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And we provide specific pre-service job training, and we assist with job matches as well.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The program was launched in 2025.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How is CUNY assessing the success of the program, and can you share any preliminary outcomes?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So I don't have an answer for that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And I think the best thing to know is that there is wide demand for the program.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And I'll get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So one-year program.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So I have to sort of find out more about the evaluation component of it.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Absolutely, because that's how we're going to measure success if we should expand and how the investment is going to work.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And the part of the program includes the Child Development Associate, the CDA certificate, and it's fully funded.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And all of the apprentices enroll.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And so we can get you the completion data as a metric because all of them enroll in that credential.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And all of the credits in the program articulate to associates or bachelor's degrees if they then choose to go beyond for teaching.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And do you provide placements for students who are in the program once they're done?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Some of them are already working in centers.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Some of them are already working, but we do do job matches and job placements.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: CUNY is also requesting $2 million over two years for the Child Development Associate Credit Bearing Program, which is a bilingual credit-bearing CDA program that provides 12 credits to participants.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: What is the cost for a student who enrolls in this program?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Would the funds allow certification to be provided for free?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The answer to the second part of that question is yes.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: That I know.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: In the first part?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: That is the idea of the funding of the program, to be able to have them participate and get those credentials.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: It's run through the School of Professional Studies, and there's been wide demand for it.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: So it's $9,000 per student.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: That includes tuition fees, the credential exam fees as well, the career advisement.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And that is the cost is also dependent on the number of students that are enrolled.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: There were 105 students in fiscal year, 26 in the program.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And I do want to say that last year, 110 students graduated from the CDA certificate program, and of that, 19 were from the Spanish language CDA that we recently offered.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And that cohort was just started in fall 25, and already 19 graduated in that program.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The credit transfers to the six associate and the bachelor degree programs, what are the six programs?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: So we have seven CUNY programs, Borough of Manhattan Community College, so BMCC, Hostos, Kingsborough, LaGuardia, Medgar.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: CUNY SPS, and Bronx Community.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
