utterances.text: College.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The fiscal 2027 executive budget did not include a restoration of 10.1 million for CUNY, accelerated, complete, and engaged the ACE program.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about ACE program funding, cost per student, and whether the $10.1 million supports all eligible students. Provost Alvero reports 5,603 students in AY24-25, and the Chancellor later provides the cost per student of $3,440 for both ASAP and ACE.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on ACE program funding and cost per student

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: CUNY ACE provides bachelor degree seeking students with additional financial and academic support to ensure higher education is accessible.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: In the fiscal 2026 funding level, 10.1 million able to support all eligible students, if not how much additional funding is needed and what's the average cost per student in the CUNY ACE program.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I'm going to defer to Provost Alvaro, who's looking for that information.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Cost per student is the thing that I am trying to get for you.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Can't seem to find the cost per student for ACE.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: But I know I have it.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: I will tell you there are 5,603 students in academic year 24-25.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And I can get you the cost per student that I know I will find at some point.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: No worries.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Are there any supports not currently offered through CUNY ACE that students have requested other than changes to the program CUNY is considering?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Based on feedback from your student, are you considering any changes to the program?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: What we are changing, not necessarily feedback from students, is with the utilization and acceleration of technology and AI,

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: embedding that into some of the tools and technologies for the career advisement piece.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: That's something that we're doing holistically, as well as into the identifying the right major and the match for the major and the more strategic

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: advisement, the tools and technologies nowadays to alleviate some of the burden and the workload of the advisors and provide more personalized advisement.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And so that's something that we're implementing, not necessarily based off of student feedback.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: They really appreciate having a dedicated advisor and a high touch, but that is a change that we are exploring.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about ASAP funding levels for FY26 and FY27 and the impact of not restoring $4.6 million. Provost Alvero reports the additional funding allowed 2,000 more students to enroll, utilizing 98% of the allocation.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on ASAP funding and restoration impact

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: In the executive budget, you also did not include restoration for 4.6 for CUNY ASAP.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: CUNY ASAP provides associate degree seeking students with intensive academic support as well as financial support such as free transportation and textbook.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: What are the current funding level in fiscal 2026 and 2027 for CUNY ASAP?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So I think we have the funding.

utterances.speaker: Ken Gardner
utterances.text: For fiscal year 26, it's about $92 million.

utterances.speaker: Ken Gardner
utterances.text: As of now, without the recurring funding, it would drop to 88.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And what is the $4.6 million supporting in fiscal 2026?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How would it impact programming if funding is not restored?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: So it impacts, we were able to increase enrollment.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: We actually utilized upwards of 98% of our allocation.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: We were able to increase enrollment by 2,000 students as a result of the additional funding.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So we know the program works.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And I knew we had this here.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The approximate cost per student in both ASAP and ACE is $3,440.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I knew it was in some place.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you so much for being so detailed today.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks whether CUNY has emphasized the importance of evidence-based programs to OMB and whether there is a rationale for not baselining them. The Chancellor says CUNY does not know the rationale and that ASAP has been replicated in nine states.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on OMB rationale for not baselining programs

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Has CUNY emphasized the importance of funding evidence-based programs that dramatically improve student outcomes such as ACE, ASAP, and CONVERGENCE?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Conversations with OMB, is CUNY aware of any rationale for why the funding of these programs was not included and baseline, and how can this council support CUNY's need for these funding?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So part of the question is no, we don't know the rationale.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We, in both ASAP and ACE, these are programs, ASAP is replicated in nine stages.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: SUNY is actually building up an entire ASAP component sort of doing the work that we're doing.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we think that the results are fairly obvious in terms of the impact that it has.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So if they work, we must continue to fund them to bring people through the doors.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about CUNY Reconnect enrollment and outcomes. Provost Alvero reports 25,342 students in 25-26, with 14,597 graduates since inception and $1.2 million used for debt forgiveness.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on CUNY Reconnect enrollment and graduates

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So the fiscal 2027 executive budget also did not restore $7.8 million for CUNY Reconnect, which aims to bring working-age adults with some college credit back to CUNY.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How many students are currently enrolled in the CUNY Reconnect, and how many credits does the average student come back to CUNY with?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: So in 25-26, we had 25,342 students.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: We do not have the average number of credits that they've come back with, but we've asked for that analysis.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: I can add some additional details that $1.2 million was used for debt forgiveness for the students and to clear their balances.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: One of the things that I would love to highlight is that 14,597 students have graduated from the program since the program inception across associate, bachelor's, and master's degrees.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So we know it works, so we have to make sure it's baselined in our budget.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about College Discovery and SEEK budgets and enrollment. The Chancellor reports 1,537 students in College Discovery and 6,141 in SEEK for fall 2025, with SEEK receiving additional state funding.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on College Discovery and SEEK programs

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Discovery and SEEK.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: CUNY's opportunity program include College Discovery and the Percy Ellis Sutton Search for Education Elevation Knowledge.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: These programs support students in their academic and professional goals by providing additional academic, financial, and advising support at the two- and four-year colleges.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: What are the budgets for FY26 and FY27 for College Discovery and SEEK, and how many students are currently enrolled in each of those programs?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So the number of students enrolled in this program is 1,537 for the fall 2025 headcount for the college discovery,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: 6,141 for the fall 2025 headcount for SEEK.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I know, and I'll defer to Ken, that there was an additional increment in the SEEK budget, right, in the state budget.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: But that is the only additional support that we have received for either SEEK or CD.

utterances.speaker: Ken Gardner
utterances.text: 28,000 for CD, for college discovery, right?

utterances.speaker: Ken Gardner
utterances.text: And the budget for college discovery in 26 was about $6 million.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Any of these programs that I mentioned earlier are going to be baselined?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The chancellor said it helps with planning.

utterances.speaker: Ken Gardner
utterances.text: You're asking if the city's going to baseline the funding?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: For these programs that works.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: They're evidence-based.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, the SIC funding is mostly coming from the state.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And so, I mean, again, from our perspective, any funding for student success program that has regularity and is baseline is better for us in terms of planning and delivering the services to the students.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: In any form of education, if you know your funding is there, you're better to plan.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: You know how to hire educators and how many seats you have and all of that.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So that's across the board.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Agreed.

chapters.summary: Joseph raises the Student Senate's request for $1.4 million for a free OmniCard pilot program serving 3,000 students. Provost Alvero lists programs currently providing OmniCards (ASAP, ACE, CHAMPS, SEEK) and the Chancellor emphasizes transportation costs also barrier internships.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on OmniCard pilot for student transportation

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Omni cards.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I know the committee has reported, heard from Student Senate regarding the barrier of cost of transportation creating, causing many students to miss class, lose out on internships and campus activities.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Or risk a fee for fare evasion.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The Student Senate have put forward a request for 1.4 million for free OmniCard pilot program, which will provide free transportation to over 3,000 students.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Which CUNY programs currently provide OmniCards to students and what are the limitations of the OmniCards?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: You want to go?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: The programs that currently provide the free OmniCard are ASAP, ACE, CHAMPS, which is at Medgar Evers, and the SEEK program.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And I want to add, Chair Joseph, another component.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I know that the students talk about getting to class and the things that we do, the key essentials, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: As we move to providing more opportunities for students to do paid internships and all that, they also need to have the transportation cost to be able to get, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: If we want to be exposing them to a lot more experiential things, they also need to be able to get there.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So I also want to, and this is one of the reasons why we supported the idea of the pilot, that

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The obvious thing that we all think about is getting to class, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Basic, important.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: But in addition to that, if we want them to be out there taking advantage of paid internships and apprenticeships, the cost of transportation can be a barrier.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay, thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The proposal includes students who are in foster care system.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Does the existing programs for foster care youth include transportation costs?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Let me check.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: I can get a breakdown of the funding received.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: I know they receive a stipend, and I'm not sure if it's specifically allocated.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: They do not receive an actual OmniCard, but in some programs, for example, in SEEK, we give a transportation stipend for that utilization, and I can look to see if the stipend is for transportation costs specifically.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about the state's Reconnect for foster scholar program funding and eligibility. Provost Alvero reports $250,000 allocated, with active conversations with the state on program details.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on state Reconnect foster scholar program

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The state budget includes $18.8 million for state's Reconnect program, which covers the cost of tuition, fees, and book for community college students age 25 to 55 pursuing select associate

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: degrees in high-demand occupations, including nursing pathways into teaching, technology, and engineering.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The state budget includes a new Reconnect for foster scholar programs as well.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How much funding was allocated to the Reconnect for foster scholar programs?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Will the Reconnect for Foster Scholar programs also have a limited age range and degree field?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: If so, what are they?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: We are in active conversations with the state on the details of the program.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And this will be an incredible opportunity also.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I know you've been a big advocate about this in the future.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I mean, it's an opportunity to create something from new.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So suggestions about what's the best way to use that funding will be welcome those from you and others.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I would love to do that.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Many people who have aged out of foster care are unaware of the options available to them.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How will CUNY ensure that people are aware of this new program?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: of community groups that serve those students and getting the word out.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And again, welcome any suggestions on what other strategies we might be able to pursue.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I would love that.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it back to Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Lee passes questioning to council members, noting new arrivals including Council Members Aviles, Dinowitz, Maloney, and Narcisse.
chapters.title: Chair Lee passes to council member Q&A round

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Council Members Aviles and Dinowitz, and we're going to start with Council Member Wong, followed by Lewis.

chapters.summary: Council Member Wong asks about infrastructure problems at Hunter College's main campus including water main and sewer line ruptures, and about the Brookdale campus renovation. COO Batista explains the demolition phase and nursing school relocation to 40 Breck Street.
chapters.title: CM Wong on Hunter College Brookdale campus infrastructure

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Chancellor Rodriguez, thank you for coming today.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I have a question concerning Hunter College.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: It says over here, executive budget, CUNY's four-year plan allocates $421.8 million for new school construction of the Hunter College Brookdale campus.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The funds would, this funds two million square feet of modern academic healthcare, life sciences space.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I'm interested in two things, because I hear from students that the experience in the Hunter College building has not been good.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: We're talking about water mains and sewer lines that ruptures.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Instead of looking at the new space, is anything being addressed to the current problem?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The current infrastructure, like how can students go to class with the water main or sewer problems that I've been hearing?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Please talk about it.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you for your question, and I'll let our COO address some of that on the capital side with more specificity.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Two things, too.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Clearly, the Hunter main campus requires, and it's part of the conversation that we had before, as to the allocation for critical maintenance to be able to make sure that, A, we get to systems

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: before they collapse or break down, and that's why we want additional.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: funding for critical maintenance, but you were talking about the Brookdale campus where we have also the renovation of the nursing facility too,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: and construction was just, did we begin that already for the

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: In Brookdale?

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: No, I mean, let me.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Yeah, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: So let's take the Brookdale property.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: As you know, that was part of a large announcement that was made by the previous administration around building sort of a life science facility.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: CUNY was going to have its own sort of building that would have.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: The nursing school, as the chancellor alluded to, the School of Public Health, and some additional labs.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: That project right now, as I understand it, is going to, they're going through the demolition phase of it.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: They're getting the, so they can begin the demolition phase of it.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: We have now moved out of that facility.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: The nursing school is in a really, really wonderful facility at 40 Breckett Street where we have,

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: was a previous school and I think one of the previous colleges was in there and we retrofitted that facility to accommodate our nursing school.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: So our nursing, I mean,

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Yeah, nursing school.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: So our nursing school is fine.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: As part of the...

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: As I stated earlier, as part of the deferred maintenance and state of good repair, we allocate to colleges various funding.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: We focus in on those vital systems that could come offline for a particular college at a time.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: The last time you raised the concern of the, and that was a pipe that was frozen, and it was fixed immediately after that, so the information was...

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Have been taken care of a while ago.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Like, as you probably know and have heard, our infrastructure is an aging infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Most of our buildings, 60% of them are over 50 years old.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: And on a five-year capital plan, our need is about 9.5.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: $4 billion, and we get about $2 point something billion in a five-year plan.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: So the need is higher than what we get, and so we try to really be targeted.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: We've done an assessment of all our facilities and really try to provide a real targeted approach.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Obviously, we're here advocating to make sure that you help us get additional funding.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: But I'd be happy to, if you have anything at Hunter College, I actually think that facility is one of the facilities that's really well run and they really stay on top of things.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: But I'll always, like anything else, things do fall through the cracks.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: I would love to hear more about it and be able to do that.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: I know our chair of your committee has really been laser focused, and I really do welcome the partnership because we haven't had that partnership before at the council, so that's really welcome.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Chair, I'll save my questions for round two.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Lewis, followed by Morano.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lewis asks three questions: outcomes from the $15M baseline investment, impact of federal research cuts on graduate education, and how CUNY Reconnect opens opportunities for prospective students. The Chancellor responds addressing each in reverse order.
chapters.title: CM Lewis on baseline outcomes, federal research cuts, and Reconnect

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Good to see you, Chancellor, and to everyone on the panel.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I have three questions, so I'm going to try to barrel them out.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The executive budget includes $15 million in baseline operational support

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: beginning in fiscal 2027 for academic advisement, mental health services, career readiness, workforce preparation, and tutoring programs.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: What enrollment growth, retention, and graduation outcomes does CUNY expect to achieve from this investment?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And how will success be measured over the next several years?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: My second question.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We know that graduate students, doctoral candidates, and students involved in research often depend heavily on federally funded grants and fellowships.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Has CUNY assessed whether potential federal cuts would disproportionately impact graduate education, research opportunities, and workforce pipelines?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And what investments would be necessary to maintain those programs and federal support if federal support continues to decline?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: My last question.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: I'm taking notes.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You know,

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You mentioned earlier to the chair, the chair Lee had spoke to you about enrollment.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: So I wanted to know how has enrollment significantly grown over the last few years?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And I wanted to know for constituents that are watching today, that's watching this hearing today, I wanted to know how do you think

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: the CUNY Reconnect can open opportunities for them if they're thinking about college and how could that help?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: So those are the three questions.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you for the questions.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Let me, well, begin, I'm going to respond to them in the inverse order that you did them, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And I'm sure you'll remind me if there's something missing.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: A, so we have intensified on the enrollment side our partnership with the New York City Public Schools.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: That is our main source of students, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And we've gone deep in working with them, sending personalized letters at the beginning of senior year, working with their parents, right, even earlier than senior year to make sure that they know that CUNY is ready for them and it's an opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And, for example, one of the things that I'm very proud we did this year.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The welcome letter we've been doing now for, I think, three years.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: This year, it went electronically.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: My old-fashioned self wanted an old piece of paper, but the electronic version allows us to tell the story.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: students if they have been taking College Now courses, it could tell you you already have 15 credits of courses banked in and you'll be saving this much money, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we are going to the questions that the caregivers and the students have.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we're trying to individualize that as much as we can to make it personal and bring you in.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Reconnect key three-quarter New Yorkers, three-quarter million New Yorkers, some college, no degree.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: That is, and Reconnect has been our way to be able to bring all those people back who have some credit and need to finish their program to be able to get to their next, to the next job.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So Reconnect has been

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: critical for us to be able to do that and to listen to what was it that led you to drop out in the first place so that we can prevent that.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: For the students we have.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: On the graduate student, your question clearly, the main, I mean, people talk about

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: the funding being cut by the feds in this attack on higher education and research funding.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And of course, innovation is being targeted.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: But the other part that's a real tragedy is the pipeline of talent that we need to keep the research infrastructure that is getting decimated.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And in our case at Acuna, I'll be happy to provide you with a breakdown.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: In some of the programs that

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: got canceled by the administration were many pipeline programs, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: For students of color and other groups to be able to get PhDs, to be able to get post-doctorate awards.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And we've lost a number of that, and that is really going to have an impact in the diversity and the number of graduate students that we're going to be able to have, which is a teaching force of the future and the research force of the future.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So I'm happy to provide you with some of that information because it's one of the things that we are very, very concerned.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And I know that locally we have incredible leadership at the Graduate Center with Dr.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Bromberg working very closely to be able to see how we can be supportive of our graduate students in their PhD route.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Morano, followed by Brewer.

chapters.summary: Council Member Morano asks what three metrics taxpayers should use to judge CUNY in three years. The Chancellor identifies enrollment growth, career readiness through CUNY Beyond, and capital investment as key metrics.
chapters.title: CM Morano on metrics for judging CUNY outcomes

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Chancellor, thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you for keeping it on the brief side, but chock full of information.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: We've heard from a lot of other folks in these hearings that have much longer testimony, but shockingly much less information.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So my compliments.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Your testimony highlights enrollment growth, retention gains, internship expansion, workforce development, AI initiatives, and several requests for additional funding.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: If we come back here three years from now, what are the three metrics you would want taxpayers to judge CUNY by?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Oof.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I mean, enrollment growth and retention are vital to the fiscal health of the university, and we are in a budget hearing, so I will highlight that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: There's also human people with degrees and human capital going to make our city great and stuff, so I think that that is a key measurement.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: That's what we led with enrollment.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We are really focused on rethinking and CUNY Beyond is that the way that from the get-go we work with our students in having them be exposed and thinking about careers.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: It's not about forcing anybody to do anything they don't want to do.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: It's opening the doors of opportunity and then having curriculum, courses, opportunities that allow you to be exploring things earlier and then transitioning in an easier way

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: into the workforce, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And I think that the nation is asking all of us to be doing that for the students.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And we need your partnership on the capital side.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So with those three that you mentioned, retention, enrollment, and career success, and capital.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Would you support publishing those metrics annually in a simple public dashboard so taxpayers can see whether the investments they're making are actually producing the outcomes that are promised?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So I would say that many of those metrics are public.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: available in our website now, and maybe there's a way in which we can be more direct and focused.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I think if I'm getting a gist of your question, it'd be in that direction.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Our enrollment, graduation, retention data is, I'm making sure with the provost here, available on the website.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We are actually

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: working on what are the right metrics to be thinking about career success, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we'll welcome feedback on that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And then our information on the capital projects and all of that, it's also available on the website.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: If there's suggestions about how to do that differently, always welcome to that.

chapters.summary: Morano asks which degree programs produce the strongest employment outcomes and which struggle in the AI era. The Chancellor says this is prospective and offers to provide existing data on employment outcomes by program.
chapters.title: CM Morano on AI impact and employment outcomes by program

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You spend a lot of time on AI, which is great.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Which degree programs currently produce the strongest employment outcomes and highest earnings for graduates?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And which programs consistently struggle to connect graduates to jobs, particularly keeping in mind the AI era that we're going into and that we're in?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, so your AI question is perspective, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So that is a crystal ball of looking at how things are going to.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We do have data on some of the metrics that you mentioned in your question.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I'd be happy to engage with the committee on those and provide them.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And they're becoming also part of some of the things that we're getting asked for in the state and the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So happy to provide that to you.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thanks very much.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Council Member Brewer asks about the John Jay North Building for affordable housing and mental health services. COO Batista cites active litigation preventing discussion on North Building. Provost Alvero reports 120 mental health FTEs with a request for 14 more.
chapters.title: CM Brewer on John Jay North Building and mental health staffing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer, followed by Epstein.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I always get asked about the North Building.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I want it to be affordable housing at John Jay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What's going on with the North Building?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And you always give me the same answer, which I don't like.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: You have my answer already, counselor.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: No, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Same old stupid answer.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: We are, you know, obviously still analyzing

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: As you know, there's an active litigation, and based on the active litigation that's going on with the government foundation, we

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: can't really talk about sort of what the plan is for the future at the school, but our goal is to continue to assess what the need of that school is and to make sure that that need is being met.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: When it comes to, with regards to North Hall, we are analyzing North Hall to see what's the best use and what's in the best interest of the university.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, I still want affordable housing there.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It seems to me that that would be a priority, so we'll keep working on that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The other issue I have is, I think we talked about mental health services, but just generally, how many folks are employed who are mental health professionals?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How many more would you like to hire?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I must admit I was a little disappointed in the numbers for

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: For ASAP because I thought it should be higher in terms of finishing.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I think that these services are helpful to get people through.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So how many sessions is a student provided with the council?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Just generally, mental health services, what you need and what you think is working.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: So the number of mental health counselors that could be hired with requested $2.8 million.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: We currently employ 120 FTEs.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: We would like to have two more per community college for a total of 14 additional mental health.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: clinicians.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And if those have to be left campus because they're not able to be dealt with locally, do they have insurance?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that an issue that you have to address or is it not an issue?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: If you have to go outside of the campus for help, do you know if there's always ability to get insurance or is that an issue?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Well, I can say that we provide some free services.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: We have partnerships with other entities and organizations, a hotline that's available 24-7, chats that are available.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: So we make as many services, free services available to our students should insurance be an issue if they were recommended out.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do you know in terms of the new excellent program with public schools, do you know how many more students came in with that program that I think you initiated with SUNY a few years ago?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do you have some sense of the numbers of people who came in because of less waiver fees, all the ways in which you're trying to get more public school students to go to CUNY?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, I mean, I think that one metric is not all, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The 3% or 4% increase that we receive in enrollment, the bulk of our enrollment comes from the New York City Public Schools, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So that is a rough estimation of that investment.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: All right, so there's no new number.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Wasn't it an increase?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I thought there would be a large increase as you did the letters and you made extra effort.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I can get you the exact figures for that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'd love second round if possible.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Council Member Epstein asks about OmniCard funding, top reasons students leave school, and housing insecurity support. The Chancellor identifies medical emergencies, employment conflicts, food insecurity, and housing insecurity as main reasons, and describes the CUNY CARES navigator model.
chapters.title: CM Epstein on OmniCards, dropout reasons, and housing insecurity

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Epstein followed by Wilson.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: You doing, Chancellor?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: This is new.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I'm used to seeing you in Albany.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: It's good seeing you here as well.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Same, yes.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Hey, can we just check in about the OmniCards for students, what the $700,000 will get you, how many additional students you'll give cards to for that additional funding?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Do what?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: It's in the...

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: You mean with the pilot that we recommended?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah, you asked to confirm another 700,000.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I'll get you that number.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Give me your next.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I'm concerned about students who can't continue in school.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: What are the top three reasons you hear students are leaving school?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Is it because of transportation?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Is it housing security?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Is it cost of tuition?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: What are we hearing from students that are reasons that they are not be able to finish their degree?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So the most common reasons tend to be non-academic reasons, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: They have to do with either medical emergencies in their families is a big category.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Situations about employment, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Many of our students work part-time and full-time, and that has impacted their capacity to be able to stay focused.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Those tend to be sort of the highest categories.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I mean, food insecurity and housing insecurity are also big categories for students, too.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So economics plays a role in the percentage of students who are dropping out?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And so just on the economic side, so Omnicard's one.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know you're doing well with getting food pantries on campuses.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So on the housing and security, I know for ASAP and other programs there are potential funding.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Do you see that as a bucket where we could solve for the housing and security?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, I mean, I think one of the main, in the CUNY CARES model, right, and those are just,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: a pilot with the schools in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: What we have learned there is that we can be good providers with the navigators of information to the students about what their options are.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Sometimes they're not aware of housing options that might be available to them.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So that would be a model for us since we don't have

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: really a housing stock, right, huge number of dorms.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We need to rely on partnerships, and the CUNY CARES model has been a way in which we've seen many students refer and dealt with those issues, and I think...

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know two years ago we talked about tracking students who had dropped out of CUNY and come back in and that you were following up with them to try to get them back in the program.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: How successful has that been in the last two years?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, so we can get you some numbers on that from the Reconnect.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I don't have those with those, but those have been a key.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And for us also, not only is the joy of having them back and now seeing them being in track to succeed, is learning about what led you to leave so that we prevent it in the future.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, we've talked a lot about that, especially students.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Obviously, this is, you've said it from the beginning, that CUNY is a pathway out of poverty for many New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: This is a pathway to get people to have successful education.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And if we can really target our energy of people who wanted to get into a CUNY.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: But how to drop out and figure out what those economics are, we can really kind of protect them and support them in that process.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And I think that's what we're trying to get to here.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And the baselining of Reconnect will be indispensable to get that done.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And the demand is clear.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah, and the data, I'd love to be able to share that data so we can look at it.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we've also been joined by Councilmember Maloney, as well as Mealy, who I saw.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: There she is.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And next we have Councilmember Wilson, followed by Aviles.

chapters.summary: Council Member Wilson asks about the NYC Public Schools portrait of graduate framework and CUNY readiness, federal research cuts, and the vacant Hunter College building at 450 West 41st Street. The Chancellor describes CUNY's collaboration on the framework and COO Batista discusses the property.
chapters.title: CM Wilson on graduate framework, federal cuts, and Hunter vacant property

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Hi there, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: How are you?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: A couple questions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the first one is regarding, so next year the New York City public schools will be, to graduate, will be evaluated under a new framework, the portrait of a graduate framework.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So, which is great to show that there's other ways to get a diploma other than regents testing, but the standards are a little vague at the moment.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I'm curious what resources are being applied to ensure New York City public school graduates who enter CUNY, which is a huge number,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: that they're going to be fully prepared to meet the demands and reap the rewards of a CUNY education.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: First, we have a number of members of our faculty, of our administrative team, who are part of the conversations in that redesign, along with our friends in the New York City Public Schools, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we're really part of the conversation to be as ready as possible.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So whenever that is finalized, right, we can then be ready to adapt and face those things.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The other great component that we have is we have such a deep investment in college now.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And early college, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we are, you know, providing those courses and doing those things along with the New York City Public Schools.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we're getting all that feedback early to be able to be in the best position to be ready for whatever that new portrait that would have probably more options and more flexibility, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I think to your point, for us to be able to be ready for them.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Great, so you've been a collaborative partner in building the process.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's great.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we know that the Trump administration has made really devastating and arbitrary cuts to humanities and sciences and medicine.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: How does this budget reflect steps you're taking to ensure that we

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: that you're taking in step to save research that's being cut?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, thank you for that question and have to commend the provost here when we faced some of the first lines of cuts on some of the federal grants, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And later we found ways to fight some of those and get some of that money back, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And I can give you the breakdown of what we lost.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: But the provost initiated a funding program to be able to support some of the researchers, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I mean, we couldn't make the entire amount of the grant, but enough to sort of keep some of the key projects going and moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And, you know, as I mentioned in answering the question from Councilwoman Lewis,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I'm also very worried about, and those we couldn't restore, the pipeline programs that many of our schools had, the graduate centers, some of the other campus, to bring the next generation of scholars

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: with, you know, that were decimated, and that's going to affect our human capital in terms of teachers and researchers.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, it'll be quick.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So just about capital needs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we talk a lot about that across the university system.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But at the same time, in my district, the Hunter College owns a vacant building at 450 West 41st Street in Hell's Kitchen.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's been unused for a decade.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's a huge source of strain in the community.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So how do you evaluate underutilized properties when determining your capital strategy?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Specifically, how do you balance the generating revenue from assets that could have public benefit, like affordable housing, community facility, or future educational uses?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So you have beautifully addressed our dilemma in terms of that, but I'll let our COO answer that question.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: So you're talking about the MFA building?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Oh, yes.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: So as you know, we have been in conversations with the community board, with your predecessor, now the senator, around what are the possibilities with that site.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: We have always been open to having partnerships and thoughts around that site.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: The one thing that I really have been focused on, as you know, we had a situation.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: We have squatters in there.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: How to go in and really remove those squatters and try to seal up the space.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: We try to do things to address some of the concerns the communities have raised.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: I'm always open to hear what your concerns are so we can be together addressing.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: But, you know, it's a property that we're analyzing to determine what's the best use.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Obviously, my main concern is always to make sure that we're using the property in the best interest for the university first.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: And then, obviously, how can we provide what the university needs, the colleges need, and also...

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: Work on the community, the greater good.

utterances.speaker: Hector Batista
utterances.text: And so we're always open to those discussions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, the first, the current use is in the best interest of no one.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I would really value us sit down to talk about the future because it's been a real problem for a long time.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Happy to have that conversation.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Majority Leader Abreu as well as Deputy Speaker Williams, and we have Aviles followed by Deneuiz.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we've been joined by Ressler.

chapters.summary: Council Member Aviles asks about the Constructive Dialogue Institute's funding, duration, and expansion needs. The Chancellor reports CDI began 18 months ago, is requesting $750,000, and is the only university system doing this work system-wide.
chapters.title: CM Aviles on Constructive Dialogue Institute funding

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: First and foremost, I just want to thank the whole CUNY team for being here, for the work that you do shepherding New York City's prized institutions.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: We deeply appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I wondered if you could, I know in your testimony, which I was not here to hear, but read, could you elaborate a little bit more on the Constructive Dialogue Institute?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Can you tell us a little bit about how long this initiative has been in play?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: How much the funding of the program currently supports, and what would be the need, the funding need in order to appropriately expand it throughout the campuses.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The funding for the CDI, which began about a year and a half ago, depends on funding from the city council.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We're asking for additional funding for that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I think we're asking for three quarter million for the year, and it's indispensable to continue the work that we've been doing.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We also got some grants from two foundations initially to do a pilot and get it going.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: One of the things that is really wonderful about that program is that we have the work of the trainers from the CDI team.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: But they're working to adapt that to however campuses are doing different kinds of initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So some campuses are including engaging in constructive dialogue and dealing with difficult conversations in freshman seminars.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So they're working with the faculty who are doing that to bring in that to that component there.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: In some other cases, they're working with student groups and other groups to be able to work with the student groups as the student groups get ready to face programming that they do.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So it's been a very, very adaptable program.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And again, I am really proud that we're the only institution in the country that's doing this system-wide, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So it's not just a campus.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And the funding from the city council on this will be crucial to keep it going.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Does the current level allow cover the entire system?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we did that with some of the combining the past funding from the council with some philanthropic funding that we received.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: You know, the funders like to give you money for a couple of years and they say somebody else should pick it up, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we're hoping that in that case, it's the council that does that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we will need that to continue to be system-wide.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Definitely something within our interest.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, yes.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: To baseline as an important program.

chapters.summary: Council Member Dinowitz asks about CUNY Citizenship Now and immigrant student safety. The Chancellor describes legal triage services funded by the Petrie Foundation for students intercepted by ICE, and the central office supporting campus efforts.
chapters.title: CM Dinowitz on CUNY Citizenship Now and immigrant student safety

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Just to segue a little bit into, you know, immigrants make New York City the beautiful city that it is.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Can you talk to us a little bit about...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I know you host CUNY Citizenship Now, which we are very big supporters of.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: They do fantastic work.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Can you tell us a little bit more about how you are feeling about our current environment and plans on keeping New York City immigrants, college students safe?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, thanks for the question and thanks to the council for the great support of citizenship now throughout many, many, many years.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I was at John Jay about a month ago and, you know, 300 people, I think Councilwoman Brewer and some of you were there too, 300 people trying to get that last,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: moving from, you know, permanent status to full citizenship.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So the demand is there.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We've also, in order to support our students, we raise independent funding

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: from the Petrie Foundation that help us provide triage legal services for students who might be

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: you know, intercepted by ICE, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We found out that in many cases,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: either individuals go to places that basically take their money and don't provide any real legal services, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we provide a service.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We cannot take them on as clients, but at least at that time when you're having the first triaging, we can point out and say, here's some legitimate lawyers, here's some legitimate nonprofits that work with you.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We can stabilize.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And that's been, I think, very well received by the community.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We have an office, the central office, dealing with how we can support our students, and campus do that in different ways.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: But it's very, very much top of our mind, and thank you to the council for the support of citizenship now.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Just to make a last comment, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You know, New York City public school educators and parents have asked the public school system to provide revised protocol around how to keep their students safe, their staff safe,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: because the protocol within the building is one thing, but right outside the building is a whole other thing.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I would also like to posit that challenge to CUNY around how it makes sure that

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Everyone from the security guard to the groundskeeper to all the staff are very clear about their obligations under New York City Sanctuary City policy

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: and also their moral obligations to keep each other safe during such a dark time.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We'd love to talk to you more about that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you for that suggestion.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Councilmember Maloney, followed by Majority Leader Abreu.

chapters.summary: Council Member Maloney asks about CUNY Beyond's AI Innovation Fund and how it meets workforce needs. The Chancellor describes CUNY Beyond's five pillars including career-oriented curriculum and AI integration, and offers to provide job placement data.
chapters.title: CM Maloney on CUNY Beyond and AI workforce alignment

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I wanted to ask more about CUNY Beyond.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: You mentioned that you're asking for $2.7 million for that program with a plan to expand to 19 undergraduate campuses by 2030.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I want to hear more about how investments in CUNY Beyond, in particular the AI Innovation Fund, are meeting the city's workforce needs and how you evaluate where you should be investing more with matching with employers.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So, you know, CUNY Beyond is really going, when you take CUNY,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: 77% graduate debt-free, almost equal number, don't pay any tuition, incredibly affordable, great asset in our faculty.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So what we want is to build more on the human capital of our students as they think about careers.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So Beyond is about making sure that our students are thinking of being exposed to careers from day one when they get into a campus, right, and they can then contribute more.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We think that that differentiator, when we get to do that well, is going to be a driver of enrollment, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Parents and students are asking for places that are giving them better chances to connect with careers at the end of graduation.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we really see it as what we owe to our students and a great tool.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: to BFAP enrollment and to be responsive to the community of employers out there.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: If we have, as the five pillars of CUNY Beyond have, more career-oriented curriculum, the AI component will be part of that, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Because we'll be looking at all the different majors and careers and how AI is...

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Figuring that out in the marketplace is going to be coming to us in terms of upgraded curriculum,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: more individuals from industry coming to teach as agents for our students and our students being also more active in paid internships and apprenticeships, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So all that should create students that are ready to meet the moment and that's why we are very bullish on CUNY Beyond as a key strategy for us moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And what are some of the metrics that you can share on job placement, earning potential by different segments like teachers, healthcare workers, technology professionals for CUNY Beyond program and the benefits that come from it?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So I'll be happy to provide you with details on what we have.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The other component is that we're trying to figure out what are the right metrics in terms of return of investment, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: In terms of a student comes to CUNY, they participate in this process, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: How do you really measure?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: That element of success.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: If you look at what most schools use now, it's very rudimentary data, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: It's surveys going out to students, and we want to be a lot more scientific to be able to come back and really measure ourselves to make sure that we're delivering for the students.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: But I can give you what we have now, which is mostly based on information that comes from the New York State Department of Labor, is what we use, and we'll be happy to provide that to you in any way that you...

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Are interested in.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Could I ask one follow-up?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Okay, quickly, yes.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks how AI tools are being used in classrooms and how CUNY prepares AI literacy. Provost Alvero describes the $3.2 million AI innovation fund across four areas: education and literacy, student support, ethical implications, and an upcoming AI 101 course.
chapters.title: CM Maloney on AI tools in education and AI literacy

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Okay, and on artificial intelligence, I'm wondering if you could paint a picture on how those tools are being used as educators in the classroom and also how you're preparing AI literacy skills.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: If I don't let the provost answer that, she will explode.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: So last year we received some funding from the state in AI and we created an AI innovation fund and we allocated $3.2 million across the 25 colleges across four specific areas.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: One was AI education and literacy.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: So how faculty created courses, started embedding it within the general education framework.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: We also have embedded it into an AI-enabled student and academic support.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: So looking at the academic advisement service.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: and the technologies and the tools.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: We also had a category on addressing ethical and social implications of AI, which is just as important as AI literacy.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And we are getting ready to roll out an AI 101 course that would be available to all students, faculty, and staff to ensure the literacy.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And we were very strategic in the funding, welcoming and partnering with the colleges, with the faculty, with the staff on how to embed AI ethically and make sure that we support this and piloting some of this.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And so in the fall, everyone now has provided reports on the outcomes of these pilots so we can assess, evaluate, and see what is scalable.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Majority Leader Abreu asks about challenges students face with transportation and OmniCard discussions with the administration. The Chancellor reports the pilot would serve 3,100 students at a cost of $750,000.
chapters.title: CM Abreu on OmniCard pilot for students

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Majority Leader Bray, followed by Councilmember Ressler.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I'm just trying to be mindful of time so we can keep both questions and responses as succinct as possible.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
