utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And thank you, Mr.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Chancellor, for being here today.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's been a push for CUNY students to receive free Omni cards to help them get to and from classes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Can you please talk a bit about some of the challenges students face currently and if there's been any discussion with the administration on this?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We actually found the number of maybe 3,100 students.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We got asked before about for the pilot that we put in the green sheet, how many students would benefit.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So that is the number.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: That is the number.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We want to make sure that as many students who need the OmniCards to get to class, but not just to get to class, but also to be able to go to paid internships, to be out there connecting to the programs that we have so they can be fully participating.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We want to try to maximize what programs already exist, like encouraging students to be part of fair fairs.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: If that's something that they qualify from.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we're trying to take a holistic approach to cover as many students as we can with that very basic support.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And what's the price tag for that?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: For the pilot, what we're asking the council was $750.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We would love to see that in the adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As the chair of the Transportation Committee, this is something that we find very, very important.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Council Member Restler asks about pedagogical hiring headcount, noting a 10% decline since pre-COVID. The Chancellor explains hiring takes about a year and the $50 million was not recurrent last year, so they were cautious, but expects improvement in FY27.
chapters.title: CM Restler on CUNY pedagogical hiring and headcount decline

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Restler, followed by Deputy Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee and Chair Joseph, for both of your great leadership.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Chancellor, good to see you and team.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I imagine that Ken is happy to be here wearing a CUNY hat than an OMB hat.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's a pin, not a hat.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Hat.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Pin, excuse me.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But a friendlier response from this side of the dais.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It's good to see you all.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I really just first and foremost want to thank you for your work.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Underscoring the points I heard Councilmember Aviles make, you know, CUNY is the place where social mobility happens.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It is the place where we transform people's lives from

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: you know, struggling in poverty to real economic opportunity and being able to provide and sustain support for a family.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I did want to ask a couple questions about hiring at CUNY.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: My recollection was that we were able to restore, I think, $94 million after six or seven rounds of budget cuts by Eric Adams in last year's budget.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do I have that number right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: That's correct.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And already the mayor has added and baseline $15 million for CUNY in the executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is that right as well?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So you guys are doing better.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Still more to go, and we hope to be great champions for CUNY, and I was very pleased by some of the suggested investments in the council's budget response.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm concerned that we haven't seen a restoration in hiring that would match that increase in funding.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: If I have my numbers right, prior to COVID, there were 4,648 pedagogical employees at CUNY, and we're now down to 4,257, almost a 10% decline.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: With the $94 million that we got restored last year, with the additional funding that we've gotten restored this year, are you able to provide any indication to us of the increased headcount that can be anticipated in FY27, on the pedagogical side in particular?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So getting an exact number of that gets complicated because hiring on faculty is done on a campus-by-campus basis, and those needs are very individualized, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Can you give me a...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Ballpark, if not an exact headcount of where we're heading.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Because I think we want to make sure that when we're investing in CUNY, that we're also investing in the faculty of CUNY and expanding it.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And with the 10% plus decline that we've seen over the last five, five years, we want to see it reversed.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And we're on the same page.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: A couple of notes, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Even though we've done gains in enrollment, we're still about 10%, 12% less than we were in the pandemic.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So I think that that needs to be put in context.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And the restoration last year in the budget is one year hiring, right, particularly a full-time personnel takes about a year to be able to do that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: On the $50 million, we had to be careful last year because it was not recurrent, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So you're careful about not putting people that are going to be on recurrent costs.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So I think that in the next year's budget, you'll begin to see that being corrected in a more precise way because it takes about a year or so to see those things coming to the budget because of the way we hire.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can I have one more?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: But we're committed to the same thing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm glad to hear it.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: If you're willing or able and follow up to this hearing to provide any estimates or goals of what you're anticipating on the pedagogical hiring front for the year ahead, I think it would further reinforce the council's commitment to support for CUNY.

chapters.summary: Restler asks about City Tech capital conditions including mold and leaks, and the timeline to get 100% of vital building systems to state of good repair. The Chancellor references the $9.7 billion capital request and efforts to move quickly with DASNY.
chapters.title: CM Restler on City Tech capital needs and state of good repair

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And then lastly, I represent City Tech.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We regularly get incoming from the faculty at City Tech about the conditions that staff and students are working in.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I recognize that we have significant outstanding.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: capital needs across the CUNY campuses, and we need to do a whole lot more to invest in that capital infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But at City Tech, we get reports of mold in classrooms, leaks, and more.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I understand CUNY's current goal is to bring about 55% of vital building systems into a state of good repair by 2030.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Gosh, I wish we could get that to 100%.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But I recognize that it's a lot of resources and work that's needed just to get to over half of our building systems in a state of good repair.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What do you think the timeline is to get to 100%?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do we even have that kind of big picture goal of by 2040, we're going to get 100% of our vital building systems in a state of good repair?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What is there a capital need?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do you have a kind of complete physical needs assessment for the CUNY portfolio of what we would need to come up with to be able to get there?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, that is a request in the total capital plan, right, which is nine.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: 9.7 billion, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So that would be the answer to that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: To get to 100, you have to go through 32, 35, 50.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we're trying to move as quickly as we can.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And depending on the level of resources that we receive from the state and the city.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Another thing that we've been very, very working very hard is to do better at being quicker at being able to use the funds that we're given, right, which is another component of this.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We've been also making sure that our partner in that entity, which is DASNY, is also, you know, also very responsive.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So the more funding that we receive, and particularly for the community college, where you have to do the match with the state,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: we'll get to be able to have a lot less of those concerns that you identify, which we take very seriously, and we appreciate you bringing them to our attention.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I appreciate the answer, and I think we just, we want to, we all want to do more together to improve the conditions on our CUNY campuses, and I think being even more ambitious in our capital plan is necessary.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So thank you for the extra time, chairs, and thank you, Chancellor, as always, for your tremendous leadership.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Deputy Speaker Williams asks about investments impacting York College. The Chancellor announces York is included in the second wave of CUNY Beyond expansion, which will benefit enrollment and employer partnerships.
chapters.title: Dep. Speaker Williams on York College investments

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've been joined by Councilmember Hudson, and now we have Deputy Speaker, followed by Hudson.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Almost afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: York College is an important institution in Southeast Queens.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Are there any planned investments, program expansions, or workforce development initiatives specifically impacting York students and the surrounding community?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, thank you, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Williams, for your question.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And I am very happy that in the second wave of CUNY Beyond, which is really our

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: innovative strategy to be able to make sure that our students are thinking about careers and getting ready for careers since day one,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: that in the next, the second wave, of course, is your college is one of the schools that are being identified, which will mean that should we be receiving additional funding from, say, in the city, it would go to your college for investment.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And it's also a badge of distinction for your college as they go seek to talk to employers and get philanthropic dollars.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we really feel that that designation is going to be really, really instrumental to York in terms of enrollment and all those factors.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Williams asks for an update on the $30 million for York College groundwater flooding. Dr. Williams reports phase one grouting is complete, phase two mechanical design will be done by early 2027, and value engineering reduced the cost from $25 million to $10 million.
chapters.title: Dep. Speaker Williams on York College groundwater flooding update

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And then last year, I asked a question about the $30 million that was supposed to be allocated to your college to address the groundwater flooding issue.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Do you have any updates on that?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Because last year, the answer...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Sarah was not precise.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Do you want to know the answer?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Last year you all talked about it and said that it was going to happen, but there wasn't a clear timeline.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Williams
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Councilwoman.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Williams
utterances.text: We just completed, the consultant just completed phase one of the work that's being done, that's the grouting work around the plan.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Williams
utterances.text: Phase two, which is the mechanical design, will be completed by early 2027.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Williams
utterances.text: The project was, originally was,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Williams
utterances.text: It was about $25 million.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Williams
utterances.text: Based on the work that we have been able to do, it's now down to $10 million.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Williams
utterances.text: So we've been able to do some value engineering that has allowed us to bring that project.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Williams
utterances.text: So that project is right on target to be completed.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Williams
utterances.text: We are also, I know one of the other concerns you've raised, we've also are in conversations with DEP about the charges that they're charging the college, which is over the last couple of years has been about $400,000

utterances.speaker: Dr. Williams
utterances.text: to try to really figure out how those charges could be removed from the, because it's a burn on a college that really needs the resources.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Williams
utterances.text: So sort of more to come there, but we're right on target to be able to address this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Williams
utterances.text: So hopefully by next year, when I'm

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: In front of you again, we'll be able to celebrate that we've got this project sort of completed.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thanks, Chairs.

chapters.summary: Council Member Hudson asks about CUNY CARES funding for students experiencing homelessness and food insecurity at Medgar Evers. The Chancellor describes philanthropic funding for food pantries and the CUNY CARES model, with a $2.5 million request for food insecurity in the green sheet.
chapters.title: CM Hudson on CUNY CARES and food insecurity funding

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Hudson, followed by Dinowitz.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Hello, Chancellor and team.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: You mentioned in your testimony work that you do around CUNY CARES, really important work.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: We have a transition academy at Medgar Evers College, which is in my district.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And I'm just wondering, you know, given the number of students that are experiencing homelessness, food insecurity, things of that nature, how much in your budget is allocated towards supporting those students?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So it varies by campus and depending on the kind of support, and thank you for your question,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: in, for example, things like the food pantries and that level of support, that is mostly philanthropic dollars that are raised from foundations.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The Petey Foundation has been historically a great partner of ours for decades in some of that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Partnership with local vendors, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So that tends to be a bit more local.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: In CUNY Cares, for example, one of the things that we learned from that experiment in the Bronx is that we're probably ready to do something and be part of the next expansion of CUNY Cares, which is to get students

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: immediately to know whether they qualify for SNAP or not and be able to get all those benefits.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We mentioned the testimony that we got about $3 million back for the students.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The other thing that I want to say is that is also $3 million that went to the local bodegas and businesses where our students buy their food too, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So this is something that if we do right, we're helping the students with food insecurity, but it's also a little bit of economic development in our neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Yeah, I would agree, and I commend you for that work.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I think it's really important work.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I have legislation trying to make automatic enrollment possible for certain benefits.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And also, I fund food pantry, the food pantry specifically at the Transition Academy and provide services.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: We do resource fairs, all of that.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: But I'm wondering if you can maybe ballpark a figure that would be helpful for you to have to address the needs of, you know, because the reality is there are so many CUNY students who are struggling to make ends meet.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And if there was, you know, you're helping them to bring in $3 million in resources and food, let's say.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: But I'm trying to figure out how much are you spending?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I get that some of the money is philanthropic, but how much are you spending in order to make that happen?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And could, I mean, I know you could use more, but what would be an amount that would help?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Well, so in our green sheet, which is our request, we have 2.8.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: The council already has a food insecurity initiative to be able to supplement and expand that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And if you think also then about the CUNY CARES component, if we expand the benefits hub, that's something additional that can help all the campuses.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So the request on food insecurity is 2.5.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: You're great.

chapters.summary: Council Member Dinowitz asks about the IEP data sharing agreement between DOE and CUNY under Local 18 of 2023. Provost Alvero confirms the agreement details are worked out and IT departments met last week, on track for summer completion with proactive student outreach planned.
chapters.title: CM Dinowitz on IEP data sharing with DOE

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Councilmember Dinewitz?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Good to see you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Good to see you as always, the whole team here.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Last hearing, I asked a question about Local 18 of 2023, which provides a DOE and now CUNY.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: A DOE can send IEP data electronically to colleges of their choice.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Since that time, I know we've been working together, CUNY, DOE, to create a memorandum of understanding that the data will automatically be sent over.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm confirming that this is on path that all graduates, high school graduates with an IEP,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: 2026 will have their IEP data shared directly with CUNY?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Let me allow the provost to give you an update on this very important initiative.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Yes, I can confirm that last week, so the agreement and the details of the agreement have already been worked out, but last week

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: both the DOE and CUNY's IT departments met together to talk about how does this actually work and have fleshed out a few things.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: They might have one or two more follow-up meetings.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: before things then proceed to the lawyers for signing.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: So we are on track by the summer to have everything in place.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: And yeah.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Good.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And one of the goals of that was not just so you have the data, but that you can proactively reach out to students with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I believe according to your estimates, you believe half of students with disabilities are not declaring or don't, for whatever reason, maybe they don't know there are services available to them.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So will CUNY reach out directly to students that are on their radar now as having listed as having an IEP or disability?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Will you be reaching out by phone, by email, by mail?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What does the outreach look like?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: Well, the students indicate and provide contact information.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: So it may be email, may be phone, but traditionally students prefer email.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: But we will try any means that the students have given us permission to do so.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Good.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Just confirming that CUNY will be reaching out.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So is the expectation, if they leave a phone number and an email, will then some professional from CUNY be reaching out by phone and email to make sure they know the service is available to them?

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: I can receive confirmation if we do both, but absolutely, it will be on CUNY to reach out, and that was part of the agreement, that we'd receive that information so that enables CUNY to proactively reach out to the students.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: If we have the permission.

utterances.speaker: Alicia M. Alvero
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: And the information will reach out.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: What we're trying to be careful here is not to promise something that...

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Legally, we might not.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Well, this is the point of the memorandum of understandings, to make it legal.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And then the ultimate goal is for CUNY to do the outreach so the students have all the opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Same page.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks about the personalized recruitment letters sent to graduating seniors. The Chancellor explains letters now include College Now credits earned and projected savings, with plans to add major recommendations based on coursework data.
chapters.title: CM Dinowitz on personalized CUNY recruitment letters

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Secondly, I'd like to follow up on your personalized letters.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There was a hearing on this a few years ago.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I just want to clarify what personalized letters mean.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: My understanding is personalized letters means every DOE student gets a letter with their name on it.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But do these letters also include things like course recommendations, specific colleges based on the coursework the student is taking?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Is it based on the student's GPA?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Is there any targeted letter other than the individual student's name?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So don't minimize what it took to get an individualized letter the first time that didn't have some of those things, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: But with every iteration of the letter, we are better at being able to personalize it more.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: For example, this year, the letter, and it goes electronically to be able to do that, included, for example,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Congratulations, Eric.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We look forward to seeing you at CUNY.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You heard it here.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I just got an admission to CUNY.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Since you participated in College Now, you already have, if you come to CUNY, you have already 15 credits, which will mean that you'll be saving X amount of money, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we've been able to tailor the letters each time more to be able to provide the students the most.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: accurate information that they need.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We want to continue that level of individualization, but the letters are to that level of detail, and we want to continue to do more to be able to provide them.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: For example, for some of the students that might be part of Future Ready,

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We might be able to, based on that data, make some suggestions about majors that they can take.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So the more that we know, the more we'll be able to make that letter to them and to their caregivers.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yeah, that would be the ultimate goal, I think, is to make actual recommendations.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The addition of college now, it seems, is very good.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Making recommendations for particular colleges or particular majors based on their coursework seems like the ultimate goal and the actual personalization of the letters.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, chairs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks for vacancy data by campus and position. The Chancellor commits to providing the data before the budget is passed.
chapters.title: CM Dinowitz on CUNY vacancy data request

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: At our preliminary, this will be my last question and you can leave.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: At our preliminary budget hearing, I asked data on the specific campuses and positions where vacancies exist.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do you have this data?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If not, what is your timeline for providing it?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: How many vacancies currently exist across the whole system and what are some of the challenges you face as folks are retiring?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: So we don't have that.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: We'll get it to you ASAP.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Before the budget, before we pass the budget, right?

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Lee wraps up the CUNY portion of the hearing, thanking the Chancellor and team, and noting follow-up questions will be sent.
chapters.title: Chair Lee wraps up CUNY hearing portion

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes, and apologies to the council members for round two.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They have a very important board of trustees meeting where they're going to be deciding on a lot of important things.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we will send all of our follow-up questions, and we also have been joined by...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: My Councilmember Mercedes Narcisse.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with that, thank you so much, Chancellor, and to your team and for everyone for being here.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Félix Matos Rodríguez
utterances.text: Thank you for your support.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Oh, yeah, they said they were going to be on the right.

utterances.text: Okay?

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: How do you say, like, lots of drama in Creole?

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I'm feeling.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I'm feeling.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: And I want to stay away.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Who?

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: How do I stay clear?

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I want to stay away from the drama.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: What drama?

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I want to stay away from the drama.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Me too.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Do what I mean.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Just say, I want to do like Mercedes.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I'm a people problem.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I'm a people problem.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I'm a people problem.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Yes, I know.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: That situation is messy.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: It's not only Haitian that's messy.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: That's not my problem.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I'm not in the state.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I'm not.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: It's not only Haitian that messy, by the way.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Did Solene tell you what he said?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: What the bar said?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Oh, that they need capacity training?

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: No, and he was like, oh, the Haitian community keeps asking me for stuff.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I was like, I said no comment.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I'm a Haitian, okay?

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: So I love that.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I'd be like, I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: We just say no problem.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I'd be like, I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Whatever.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: That's not me.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: It is cool.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: And they already have a picture.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I told them I would, like, show them, but they didn't ask me for it.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: They didn't ask me for capital this year.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: That?

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: The mic.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Oh, the mic?

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Oh.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: I'm not to ask you.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: So, like, next year I would.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee opens the DOE FY27 Executive Budget Hearing, introducing Education Chair Eric Dinowitz and listing council members in attendance, and welcoming Chancellor Kamar Samuels and his team.
chapters.title: Chair Lee opens DOE FY27 executive budget hearing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Welcome, welcome, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think we are good to go.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So good afternoon and welcome to the second FY27 Executive Budget Hearing for the day, focused on the Department of Education.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Linda Lee, and I'm pleased to be joined by my colleague, Councilmember Eric Dinowitz, who is the Chair of the Committee on Education.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we have been joined by our speaker, Julie Menon, Deputy Speaker Williams, Council Members Lewis, Brewer, Wong, Marano, Felder, Hudson, Ressler, Farias, Joseph, Maloney, and Shulman.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sure, Felder.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And Councilmember, oh, Councilmember De La Rosa, and did I say Mealy?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I saw her earlier.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Mealy, just in case.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So welcome, Chancellor Kamar Samuels, and thank you all for joining us today to answer our questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Special shout out to Daniel Junta, sorry, and Deputy Chancellor Foti.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, all of you guys.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I'll stop.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Now I'm going to get myself in trouble.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On May 12, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for FY26-30 with approved FY27 budget of $124.7 billion.

chapters.summary: Lee presents DOE's proposed FY27 budget of $37.9 billion, noting the $105.8 million decrease from the preliminary plan due to a $922.2 million savings plan offset by restorations and expansions.
chapters.title: Chair Lee outlines DOE FY27 budget and savings plan

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: DOE's proposed fiscal 2027 budget of $37.9 billion represents 30.5% of the administration's proposed FY27 budget in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This is a decrease of $105.8 million, or 0.3%, from the $37.6 billion originally budgeted in the FY27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This decrease results from several actions, mostly a $922.2 million savings plan, which is offset by increased funding to restore learning to work

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: and early childhood and education outreach, as well as expansions of Every Child and Family is Known, special education pre-K, and teacher recruitment efforts.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of April 2026, DOE's pedagogical headcount was 2,936 less than their fiscal 2026.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: budgeted headcount, while their non-pedagogical headcount was 890 more than budgeted.

chapters.summary: Lee urges the administration to invest in the $5 million mental health continuum serving 50 high-needs schools, noting the program has been funded one year at a time despite its positive impact.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on mental health continuum funding

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In the Council's preliminary budget response, we called on the Mayor to add $64.6 million to DOE to invest in one-time funded educational programs such as Learning to Work, the Mental Health Continuum, please, I'm making a plug for that,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: and Immigrant Family Engagement and Outreach.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We were pleased to see that two programs, Learning to Work and Early Childhood Education Outreach, were included in the plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we are hoping that the majority of the one-time funded programs will be included in the future before we adopt.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: During my time as chair of the Committee on Mental Health, I consistently fought for a mental health continuum to be added and baseline.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And this plan does not include the $5 million for this crucial program that brings together the DOE, H&H, and DOHMH to support students with significant mental health needs at 50 high-needs high schools.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, high-need schools.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The funding has continually been added for one year only, which creates instability, as we know, and uncertainty when it comes to planning around the program's future, despite its positive impact on students.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So today we urge the administration to invest in this program to support the mental health needs of students at high-need schools.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And now I will turn it over to our speaker for her opening statement.

chapters.summary: Speaker Menin delivers opening remarks noting investments in early childhood education, special education, and Learning to Work, while raising concerns about the $922.2 million savings plan and lack of transparency in DOE contracting.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on DOE executive budget investments and concerns

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Good morning, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Good morning, First Deputy Chancellor and your entire team.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Today, we will, of course, as the Chair said, be discussing...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: saying the Department of Education's executive budget for fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The executive plan includes significant investments in our education system.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: As a shared priority between the council and the mayor, we are happy to see investments in early childhood education, programming for students with disabilities, and learning to work, among many other crucial services included in the most recent plan.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The executive plan includes an additional $81.5 million in fiscal 2027 for early childhood education, on top of the hundreds of millions of dollars included in the preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: These investments are critical as we work towards a universal child care system.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We are also pleased to see funding to increase contract rates for ECE providers who have not seen an increase since 2022, despite rising operating costs.

chapters.summary: Menin highlights that the executive plan's ECE rate increases do not provide pay parity for the ECE workforce, whose wages vary greatly across sites, and calls for improved recruitment and retention of ECE educators.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on ECE pay parity concerns

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Notably, these rate increases do not provide pay parity for the ECE workforce, whose wages vary greatly across sites, experience, and union affiliation.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: For a strong ECE system, we need a strong workforce behind it.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The Council is ready to work with the DOE to improve recruitment and retention of ECE educators, including increased wages for workers.

chapters.summary: Menin criticizes the lack of transparency in DOE's $12.8 billion contract budget, noting the Council's preliminary budget follow-up received only a generic response weeks after the request, and calls for detailed contract information.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on DOE contract transparency failures

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: While the executive plan includes new investments in the education system, it also includes a savings plan of $922.2 million.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: In fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This savings plan heavily relies on cost containment strategies for class size and due process cases.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The plan recognizes a savings of $149 million for due process cases coupled with investments in pre-K special education as well as services for students in non-public schools.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The Council appreciates the due process briefing that you provided last week, and we just want to say that this is in stark contrast to what we feel is a lack of transparency on DOE contracting, which is something we're going to focus on.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We look forward to discussing the savings plan with the DOE and better understanding how the DOE will realize these savings next school year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Additionally, the savings plan includes multiple savings and contract spending, most notably $30.3 million for procurement reform, as called for in the Council's fiscal 2027 preliminary budget response.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: While the Council is pleased to see an effort from the DOE to reform the DOE's $12.8 billion contract budget, there is a concerning lack of transparency in this spending.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The Council has continually called on the DOE to provide more details on their contract budget, especially the procurement method.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: However, these calls have largely remained unanswered.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: DOE's initial response to the Council's preliminary budget follow-up on DOE contracts was, quote, this is being reviewed and we will get back to the Council.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: council, end quote, a response that was provided weeks after our request.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: OMB's response similarly avoided answering the council's questions about DOE contracts, suggesting that DOE would separately follow up with specific information.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: While the DOE did share some information on DOE contracts with the council just late last week, the lack of transparency on DOE contract spending, a significant portion of not only DOE's budget, but the whole citywide budget is very concerning.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The Council continues to seek additional information to fully evaluate the procurement methods, scope, purpose, and cost of DOE contracts, so we very much look forward to a robust discussion today on DOE's contracts and savings plan.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I will now pass it back to Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Education Chair Dinowitz delivers opening remarks emphasizing accountability and transparency for the largest school system in the country, noting the executive plan includes major investments but also significant gaps in council priorities.
chapters.title: Chair Dinowitz on DOE accountability and budget gaps

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I will pass it to Chair Dinnowitz for his opening.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Good afternoon and welcome to the Fiscal 2027 Executive Budget Hearing for the Committee on Education, joint with the Committee on Finance.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: My name is Eric Dinnowitz.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm the Chair of the Committee on Education.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm honored to be here as a proud DOE public school alum and a forever educator.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Today we'll be discussing the Department of Education's $37.92 billion fiscal 2027 budget as presented in the Executive Financial Plan.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you to Chancellor Samuels and your team for joining us today.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The DOE has the largest budget among all city agencies.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: one-third of the city's overall fiscal 2027 budget.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It also has the largest headcount among city agencies with 141,414 budgeted positions, 46% of the entire city's budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: As chair of this committee, I will continue to push for accountability and efficiency and transparency.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The budget for the largest school system in the country should reflect the needs and interests of its students, its educators, its faculty, and its families.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The executive plan includes major investments in programming for students with disabilities and District 79 students, as well as the expansion of New York City Reads and Solves, which provides standardized curriculum.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm particularly happy to see the inclusion of Learning to Work and Early Childhood Education Outreach funding, both of which were called for in the Council's fiscal 2027 preliminary budget response.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: However, there are multiple Council priorities that were not funded in the Executive Plan.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz highlights unfunded priorities including restorative justice at half its current budget, mental health continuum gaps, student success centers, and missing immigrant family engagement funding.
chapters.title: Chair Dinowitz on unfunded council priorities

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Restorative justice is funded at only half of its current budget.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: In fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Many mental health clinics are in jeopardy of closing because of the gap in mental health continuum funding.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Student success centers face an uncertain future.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Funding for immigrant family engagement is missing from this plan despite being critically important given the current climate.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: For immigrant communities.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: All these services and programs are facing funding cliffs because they were not

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Funded in the executive plan, and both I and the council will continue to push for these programs.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz discusses the $137.5 million for pre-K special education and $86 million for IESP, but notes no expansion of K-12 special education programs like Horizon, AIMS, and NEST despite thousands of applications for few hundred seats.
chapters.title: Chair Dinowitz on special education investment gaps

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Between prelim and exec, the plan also includes two major programs for students with disabilities through an additional $137.5 million for pre-K special education

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: and $86 million for IESP support for students with disabilities in non-public schools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: However, beyond a small expansion of District 75 classes in the prelim plan, this budget does not include any additional special education support for grades K-12.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: With a financial plan that hopes to reduce due process case spending by $149 million, it is disappointing to see that there was no expansion of DOE's wildly popular K-12 programs for students with autism.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Horizon, and AIMS.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: DOE reports getting thousands of applications for these programs, though they are only able to offer a few hundred seats.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: These programs are not only popular but effective, producing high graduation rates.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yet they remain elusive for many students and families.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The executive plan also does not fill a funding gap for sensory exploration, education, and discovery, or SEED, programming for students with autism.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The DOE must expand support for students with disabilities across the entire school system, including training for teachers in general education classes.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: When we set our teachers up for success, they can address the needs of all of our students.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: In a due process briefing that the DOE shared with me and the council staff last week, it's clear the DOE is taking these escalating costs seriously.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Not only is due process spending a huge financial burden for the city, it also does not always solve the important underlying goal, which is getting students with disabilities their much-needed services right here in our public school system.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We're glad to see that the DOE is making progress on curbing costs and look forward to further discussing how they will realize the $149 million in savings the executive plan recognizes starting in fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: However, truly tackling due process case costs is going to require greater investment.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: In K-12 special education.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It's what's best for the city, and it's what's best for our students.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz raises concerns about cuts to both school safety agents (264 vacancy reduction) and restorative justice programming ($6 million cut), noting both are complementary investments that must be fully funded.
chapters.title: Chair Dinowitz on school safety and restorative justice cuts

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: While the plan includes investments in many areas, it also falls short on school safety and restorative justice programming.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: School safety agents and restorative justice

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: While often pitted against each other in policy debates, are complementary investments.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Both make our students and school communities safe.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yet both are cut in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The DOE saving plan includes a vacancy reduction of 264 school safety agents, meaning that schools would not be able to fully staff SSAs if desired.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Additionally, restorative justice programming faces a $6 million cut.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: If you are a person who views these two things as not complementary, as in opposition, wherever you fall on that debate

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: between asking for more school safety officers or transitioning completely to restorative justice, there is a reason to be concerned.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Both SSAs and restorative justice must be fully funded to keep our students, our teachers, and families safe across the system.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz discusses the class size compliance extension, urging DOE to reach 100% compliance equitably without prioritizing non-compliant schools at the expense of schools that meet the mandate but have other needs.
chapters.title: Chair Dinowitz on class size compliance and teacher retention

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: While the administration and the DOE strive to meet universal programming across ECE, New York City Reads and Solves, and other supports, I want to emphasize inclusion and efficacy.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Programs are only universal if everyone can participate fully and meaningfully.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I look forward to hearing more from the DOE on how they will ensure that 2K students will be identified for early intervention services.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm interested in learning more about the expansion of New York City Reads and Solves.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: DOE should use this expansion to promote cultural competency and develop teachers'capacity to lead programs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: professional development for their colleagues on these curricula.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And in the wake of yet another school bus contract extension, I look forward to a discussion on how DOE will ensure students in temporary housing and foster care

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: get to school despite relying on decades-old school bus contracts that do not adequately consider their needs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: As the DOE reconfigures its class size plan with a two-year extension for full compliance, the city needs to not only invest in teacher recruitment, which increases by $17 million in fiscal 2027 in this plan, but also on teacher retention.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: While most of DOE's savings rely on the state's extension for class size implementation, there are still many outstanding questions on the DOE's new class size plan and expectations for the 2026-27 school year.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: DOE must get to 100% class size compliance.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: In a way that is equitable.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We cannot prioritize funding for schools that need help reaching compliance at the expense of schools that meet the class size mandate but have other needs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It is the Council's responsibility to ensure that the City's budget is fair, transparent, and accountable to all New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It is essential that the budget that we adopt this year is also reflective of the priorities and interests of the Council and the people that we represent.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: This hearing is a vital part of this process, and I expect that the DOE will be responsive to the questions and concerns.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: of council members.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I look forward to active engagement with the administration over the next few months to ensure the fiscal 2027 adopted budget meets the goals the council has set out.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I would also like to take a moment to thank my Chief of Staff, Jenna Klaus, Theo Solter, my Legislative and Budget Director,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Andrew Lane Lawless and Grace Amato, the committee's financial analysts, Alejandro Carvajal, the committee's counsel, and Chloe Rivera and Katie Selena, the committee's policy analysts.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I now turn it back to Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Council Members Brooks Powers and Joann.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And before we pass it off to the Chancellor and his team for swearing in, I have to do this PSA announcement that I've been doing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: before every hearing, which is that our public testimony, because I see a lot of our community advocates and leaders in the audience, we're doing public testimony on one full awesome day on Wednesday, January, June 10th.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That's how my brain is.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Wednesday, June 10th, this Wednesday, starting at 9.30 in the morning.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So please make sure to sign up for public testimony if you have not done so.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We will try to make it as amazing and entertaining as possible.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So with that, I'm going to hand it off to our amazing committee counsel, Brian Sarfo, to swear you all in.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel swears in Chancellor Samuels, First Deputy Chancellor Gentes, CFO Scott, and Deputy Chancellors Hawkins, Foti, Puello-Perdomo, and Moran before testimony begins.
chapters.title: Swearing in of DOE witnesses

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth and nothing but the truth before this committee and respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chancellor Samuels.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: First Deputy Chancellor Gwinta?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: CFO Scott.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Chancellor Hawkins.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Chancellor Foti.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Chancellor Pueblo-Perdomo.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And Deputy Chancellor Moran.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I do.

chapters.summary: Chancellor Samuels delivers opening testimony describing his community engagement through a 10-stop chancellor conversation tour and visits to all 45 superintendent districts, shaping a vision focused on safety, academic rigor, and family empowerment.
chapters.title: Chancellor Samuels on vision and community engagement

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Speaker Menon.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You just have to push the button.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Check.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Speaker Menon, Education Chair Dynowitz, Finance Chair Lee, Early Childhood Chair Gutierrez, the Committee on Education and Finance, and the Subcommittee on Early Childhood Education.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Thank you for inviting me to testify on the fiscal year 2027 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I am New York City Public Schools Chancellor Kamari Samuels, and I am joined by my first deputy chancellor, Daniel Ginta, chief financial officer, Sarita Scott, and other members of my senior leadership team.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: The mayor's FY 2027 executive budget for NYC public schools, which totals $44.9 billion, will both

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: sustain critical programming and enable us to enact a new vision for our schools.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: One shaped directly by our communities and aligned to the administration's plan for a vibrant, equitable, and affordable New York City.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Since my last testimony, I have continued to engage deeply with our students, families, and staff.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I visited schools in all 45 superintendents districts and completed a 10-stop chancellor conversation tour.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Soon we will publish a report.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: detailing what we heard on that tour and how those insights will inform our work.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We've gathered extensive community feedback on high priority topics such as AI, held listening sessions specifically with students and families residing in shelters and more.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: These conversations have continued to shape my educational vision, one focused on safety, academic rigor, and true integration with a consistent through line of family empowerment.

chapters.summary: Samuels announces the launch of 2K and expansion of 3K, five new schools in the Bronx and Queens, the first-ever bus vendor scorecard, and a Know Your Rights comic book for immigrant students.
chapters.title: Chancellor on 2K launch, new schools, and student rights initiatives

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And I am proud to share that we are already hitting the ground running.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We are launching 2K and expanding 3K, both incredibly exciting milestones towards universal child care.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: and a more livable city.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We are opening five new schools in the Bronx and Queens, offering unique, high-quality models focused on the arts, STEM, and more.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We are growing New York City Reads and Solves, including bringing New York City Solves to elementary schools, where many math habits and mindsets are formed.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We have released our first ever bus vendor scorecard to strengthen transparency and accountability around school transportation.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We have published a Know Your Rights comic book to teach immigrant students and all students about their legal rights.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And we have hosted new celebrations to uplift communities from New York State's Seal of Biliteracy Convocation to our recently concluded Parent Coordinator Appreciation Week.

chapters.summary: Samuels details executive budget investments including $7.2 million growing to $10 million for Every Child and Family is Known to support students in temporary housing with attendance, safety, and belonging.
chapters.title: Chancellor on investments for vulnerable students

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: This is just the start, and I'm eager to continue listening and partnering as we advance our vision.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: The Mayor's FY 2027 executive budget also furthers this vision, investing deeply in the next generation of New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: That starts with a focus on our most vulnerable, including our students in temporary housing.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: This budget will expand support, resources, and access to caring adults via Every Child and Family is Known.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Thanks to a $7.2 million investment in FY 2027 that grows to

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: 10 million in FY30.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: This work will promote attendance, physical and emotional safety, mental health, and a sense of belonging.

chapters.summary: Samuels highlights the $81 million annual early childhood investment and $67.5 million growing to $82 million for special education pre-K expansion, giving students with disabilities a strong start.
chapters.title: Chancellor on early childhood and special education investments

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: The executive budget also invests in our youngest, the administration's commitment to early childhood.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: $81 million annually is momentous and game-changing.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: This is especially true for our students with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: In addition to the overall early childhood investment, the administration has committed $67.5 million, growing to $82 million

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: by FY30, to expanding special education pre-K.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: This investment gives our students with disabilities a strong start, fosters true integration, and allows us to better serve them within NYC public schools.

chapters.summary: Samuels describes the $17.3 million investment in NYC Reads and Solves expanding to 104,000 additional reading students and 102,000 math students, including $6 million for culturally responsive practices building on the Black Studies and Latinidad curricula.
chapters.title: Chancellor on NYC Reads/Solves and cultural curriculum

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Additionally, this budget bolsters rigorous academics via New York City Reads and Solves with a $17.3

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: million investment that will transform reading instruction for an additional 104,000 students and math instruction for an additional 102,000 students.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: This funding includes $6 million to improve culturally responsive practices for teacher preparation, building on this council's support for the Black Studies curriculum, the Latinidad.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: curriculum and the Hidden Voices resources.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We are bringing high quality, truly integrated instruction to classrooms across our city.

chapters.summary: Samuels announces $31 million in baseline funding for Learning to Work, confirms schools will continue to be held harmless in initial budgets, and describes $390 million in baseline funding for IT, cleaning, leases, and facilities.
chapters.title: Chancellor on Learning to Work, hold harmless, and operational funding

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Our focus on student support extends to college and career pathways as well.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: In partnership with community providers, we are preparing overage and undercredited students for economic mobility through Learning to Work, which will now be

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: funded with an additional $31 million in baseline funding.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I also want to address a topic that I know is on many principals'minds.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Hold harmless.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Providing schools with stability and predictability is a top priority, and to that end, as we prepare to allocate schools'initial budgets, which will be released next week, schools will continue to be held harmless.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: As I mentioned, engagement is a critical function of my vision, and I look forward to opportunities to engage schools, communities, this council, and others about how our school budgets work

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: to ensure that long-term fiscal stability and comprehensive support is available.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And finally, this budget also reinforces the essential operations that undergird our programmatic

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: priorities and keep our 1,600 schools open, safe, and running smoothly with

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: over $390 million in baseline funding towards IT, school cleaning, leases, utilities, and facility maintenance.

chapters.summary: Samuels describes the nearly $1 billion FY27 savings strategy including cost containment for class size and due process cases, and discusses state budget impacts including foundation aid increases and class size timeline extension.
chapters.title: Chancellor on savings plan and state budget impacts

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: While we are extremely grateful for these tremendous investments, I am proud to say that this budget does not only invest, it also saves.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: In line with the Mayor's Executive Order 12, the FY27 Executive Budget advances a

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: comprehensive savings strategy that streamlines work efforts, strengthens fiscal discipline, and better aligns resources with students'needs.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Together generating significant savings of nearly $1 billion in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Today, I'd also like to speak to budgetary and legislative decisions from the state that impact NYCPS on foundation aid.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We deeply appreciate the increase in L weights and new weight for students in temporary housing and foster care.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We will continue to advocate and partner with the state for further changes to the formula, including a regional cost index increase and an increased weight for students with disabilities.

chapters.summary: Samuels discusses the multi-year class size reduction plan with SCA, $17 million for teacher recruitment including NYC Teaching Fellows, and $3.2 million for family empowerment initiatives, thanking the Council for partnership.
chapters.title: Chancellor on class size plan, teacher recruitment, and family empowerment

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: On class size, the extension of the implementation timeline will allow us to execute our capital and hiring strategies sustainably and with fidelity, building on the progress we've made.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: since my last testimony.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Specifically, we've worked shoulder to shoulder with SCA to create a multi-year plan for all schools with class size related space needs.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: This work, which strategically blends capital and non-capital strategies, will

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: will be detailed in the class size reduction plan, which will be released next week.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Additionally, we are continuing to boost the staff to meet the mandate.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: This includes allocating funding to hire more teachers and investing nearly $17 million

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: in FY 2027 for teacher recruitment, enabling us to expand programs like New York City Teaching Fellows, the same pathway I took to become an educator.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I also want to thank the Senate for its extent, the state for its extension of mayoral control.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: An essential ingredient for equitable transformation, transformative leadership of our schools.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: But let me be clear, the other critical component is family partnership.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
