utterances.text: The mayor's executive budget reflects that with 3.2 million

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: dollars allocated to FY 2027 towards family empowerment initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Engagement is core to who I am as a leader, and I believe mayoral control and local decision-making are both

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: to effective governance and must complement each other here in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I want to thank this council for your continued partnership and support of our schools.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I've had the privilege of meeting so many of you and seeing you at schools and community events.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Your dedication is making a positive impact every single day.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I want to especially thank Chair Dinerist for his leadership of the committee, education committee.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Thank you all for your commitment to young people, and I look forward to answering your questions.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Menin questions why it has taken months for the Council to receive DOE contract data requested in March, noting the limited response was short on detail. CFO Scott explains the challenge of pulling 600-plus contracts from a secure system.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on DOE contract transparency delays

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm passing it off to Speaker Menon to ask her questions.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay, thank you so much, Chancer.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I've got a number of questions, and I know my colleagues do as well.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So thank you very much for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I want to begin with some questions around contracts.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So since we had the first preliminary budget hearing in March, the council has repeatedly requested additional information about DOE contract spending, as you know.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We didn't receive a reply to our questions until late last week, and the limited analysis that DOE provided was very short on detail and not responsive to all the questions that the council had.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So my first question is, why is it taking so long for the council to get answers on this, and when can the council expect answers to our remaining questions about the contracts?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: First of all, thank you so much for this question, and I deeply understand and hear your frustration.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: So the initial read of the question was, you know, we were looking at what it would take to supply, you know, more than 600 contracts in detail to the council.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: It was initially set up as a very laborious process that, you know, our team was looking into and didn't necessarily have the resources to get to right away.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: So I'm going to turn it over to Alishaba to talk a little bit more about that.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And then I'll come back to actually talk about some of the plans we have going forward.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: Thank you, Chancellor, and good morning, Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: So as the Chancellor stated, I think there were about 600-plus contracts that were requested, and each of those contracts are like hundreds of pages long.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: And the challenge that we have in a very resource-constrained environment is that it would require dedicated resources.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: It would take months for us to pull it because we're pulling it from a secure system that very few people have access to.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: So as an alternative, we offered up to provide requests for authorizations because they are pretty much a concise summarization of each of the contracts.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: It includes the pertinent contract terms, including the contract value.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: And we were hoping that the council would accept that and choose a subset of those of contracts from that that we could pull and we would be able to turn it around much quickly.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean, I just find this troubling that of the 600, we're not asking for a printed copy.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're asking for a file of the contracts.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean, this should take like an hour of work to do.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And it's been since March that we've asked this to get a copy of the, like an online file that has the contracts in it.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This should not, I mean, I just find it astounding that this has taken months and that we still don't have these answers.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: I completely understand, but it actually does take, we have to download.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: the contracts from a secure system and not very many people have access to that system so it wouldn't take hours it would actually take months but the fact that not a lot of people have access to the system in and of itself also raises a concern as i mentioned in the march hearing i

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: asked if there had been any auditing of these contracts and i know your response was there had not

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that is, I guess, what is concerning me.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That is why it's very hard for us to do our charter-mandated oversight function if we can't even have access to this data.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And one of the things that I am completely committed to doing as we've offered is really making sure that we have consistent briefings with you on this and rolling over time so that we can do

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: sections of the big ask that you have.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And that's where I am committed to ensuring that we have an ongoing conversation, that we have a rolling conversation with all of the, eventually all of the data that you've requested.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And that's in addition to the briefing that we offered earlier.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: With all due respect, Chancellor, we're not interested sort of in the rolling conversation.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're specifically asking for the contract data, which numerous councils have asked for data about other contracts in the past.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is not...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You know, some kind of new idea.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is, again, based

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: sick rudimentary data that DOE should be providing to the council and I'm not sure why we're getting stonewalled.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Well, I can assure you we're not stonewalling.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I think that, as you said, we did provide more recently a summary of a lot of the terms of those contracts.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And if that doesn't suffice, we'll definitely, and it sounds like it doesn't.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: It does not suffice.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean, again, we're asking, as we asked in March for the contracts, I don't think this is in no way unreasonable.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This is a very reasonable ask, and it's just concerning that we're not getting this data.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We don't want a summary of what the terms say.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We actually want the contracts.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay, thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So DOE's contract budget, as you know, totals $12.9 billion.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that's one of the reasons why we're interested in this.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's a very significant number.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we want to be able to see some of these contracts so we can understand what is happening.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We know that $3.7 billion in contract spending for charter schools, $1.4 billion in contract spending for due process cases.

chapters.summary: Menin raises a discrepancy between the Council's Checkbook analysis showing 40% of contract spending is non-competitively bid versus DOE's reported 12%. Scott clarifies the 12% refers to number of contracts, not spending, and agrees to provide the contract spend breakdown.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on non-competitive contract spending discrepancy

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The council analysis of fiscal 2025 DOE contract spending from checkbook suggests that at least 40% of contract spending was through non-competitively bid contracts.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we know that checkbook is not a one-to-one comparison, of course, with DOE's contract budget, but this analysis was limited to contract.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: spending for which a procurement method was available.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The Council's estimate of 40% differs significantly from DOE's report to the Council late last week,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: which indicated only 10% of contract spending is through contracts that are not competitively bid, and only 12% of contracts are not competitively bid.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So how do you explain this large discrepancy?

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: So I'll address, and I believe it was 12% that we had reported, and I'll address that.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: When I had reported that at the last hearing, I was only referencing that it was 12% of the total contracts, and I wasn't necessarily addressing contract spend.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: So without seeing the data that was pulled from checkbook, I'm unable to really assess whether or not that 40% is correct.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'm sorry, I'm not understanding the distinction between the 40% that the council's finding from checkbook versus the 12% that you're...

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: Is it 40% of spend or 40% of the contracts?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So the...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Contract spending so you're saying your 12% figure it was related to the actual contracts number of contracts got it Okay, could we get them the contract spend number because that is that we have an apples-to-apples comparison Yeah, okay, and could we

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: please get that by you know in the next couple days?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We'd like to see it.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We don't want to we want to get that information expeditiously yes

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So the fundamental question is why is some contract spending that is clearly not competitively bid not included in the DOE's accounting?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the $13 billion that you quoted, it does include, as you stated, due process cases in charter schools.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But in both of those areas, they don't actually go through our procurement process.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so what we tend to do is just parse it out because those are state and federal mandates that we have to meet.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay, the Council's analysis of checkbook data found that in fiscal 2025, another 21% of contract spending came from contracts that were renewed, again, considering only contracts for which there was data on the procurement method.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Does DUE track the portion of renewal contract spending that is competitively bid?

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: Sorry, can you repeat that question?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Does DOE track the portion of renewal contract spending that is competitively bid?

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: I don't necessarily know that I report out on it, but we do keep track of it, yes.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Can you also then provide to the committee this data?

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: So you want spend on contract renewals?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, exactly.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Exactly.

chapters.summary: Menin asks about the $8.9 million savings from cutting the New Visions data portal contract. Chancellor Samuels confirms it was the cost savings team's recommendation but says conversations are ongoing with New Visions and agency partners to avoid service disruption.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on New Visions portal contract cut

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I want to move on to New Visions portal contract.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The saving plan includes a reduction of $8.9 million in fiscal 2027 and the out years for contract reduction.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have heard that this cut is targeting the New Visions data portal.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Former Chancellor Ross Porter wrote an impassioned op-ed that I assume you probably saw about the value of the New Visions data portal in schools, which integrates data across agencies in support of student planning, program management, and analytics.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The council would obviously be extremely disappointed if this contract was cut next year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Can you confirm if this savings is cutting the contract for the New Visions portal?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Yeah, so I can confirm that that was the recommendation on behalf of the cost savings team.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And I will say that we have since had significant conversations across agencies.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: and as well as with new visions to really think about opportunities for moving forward in a way that would be sustainable that is not necessarily

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: That would be advantageous, first of all, to us because despite the fact that it's used across agency, all of the cost sits with the OE.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And so we wanted to have a conversation with the vendor as well as with our agency partners about how to move forward.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And we're involved in those discussions, and we're hopeful that we can get to something that doesn't involve the disruption of the service.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So then can the council know that it's no, it will not be cut, or?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We're not exactly there at no it will not be cut, but we are having significant conversations.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I will tell you that I value the New Visions portal.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Many around certain DOE do value the New Visions portal.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: One of the reasons that it was put up as a potentially duplicative

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: resource is because it's used by administrators in schools, it's used by central, but not necessarily as much by teachers in schools, but we recognize the...

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: The great utility of the new vision support.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Menin asks for average salary and working hours for ECE workers by setting. Deputy Chancellor Hawkins explains the data does not live with NYCPS but the Mayor's Office of Child Care is leading the effort to collect it, and expresses support for compensation parity beyond pay.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on ECE pay parity data collection

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Moving to pay parity.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: for early childhood education workers, what is the average salary and working hours for the ECE workforce by setting, including contracted home-based providers, contracted CBO providers,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: and NYCPS-run programs?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: First of all, thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We recognize the need in this area, the great need for ensuring that some of our most vulnerable employees

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: are made, that pay parity is moving.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: It's a really important issue, and so I'm going to turn it over to Deputy Chancellor Hawkins to talk a little bit more about this.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: Thank you, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: Thank you, Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: So we're working really closely with the Mayor's Office of Child Care and Early Childhood Education, Speaker, to get that data set.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: I want to be clear that there is a subset of the provider universe that lives within and partners with New York City public schools.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: We do not manage the full gamut.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: And so to understand what that average true cost is across the settings, we are working with the Mayor's Office.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: What we can say, it does vary, to your point, right?

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: And so we have a subset of provider-based that are contracted and center-based programs.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: Those that are unionized with DC 37, they have one rate.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: Those that are non-union have a different rate.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: And then our colleagues in New York City public schools working in district schools have a separate rate of pay, which is dictated by the U.S.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: of T, collective bargaining agreement.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: The hours vary across these settings, so I can't give you the average, but we are going to work to provide more centralized data at some point.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: I want to also reinforce that we are in full support of compensation parity, going beyond that of pay, but understanding that some of our provider partners don't even have select insurances to support their fringe rate.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: And so we are in active discussions with the mayor's office.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'm very concerned about the collection.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: This data has to be collected.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I mean, we have to understand this issue because if we ever want to get to universal child care, and this council has worked for years, we passed a whole package of bills back in 2022.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Mandating universal child care.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So this is not a new issue for us.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We've been hammering on this issue of pay parity for many, many years.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I'm concerned that we don't have, that DEWE doesn't have that data.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Do you intend to collect data either through a survey, the contracting process, perhaps in partnership with agencies such as DOHMH and others?

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: So again, we agree, Speaker, but that data would not live with New York City public schools because we are talking about the entire system of early care and education.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: And so we are very excited that the Mayor's Office of Child Care and Early Childhood Education is really interested in leading this effort.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: So yes, we will be pursuing that data.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'm going to ask on two other topics and then...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: turn it over to my colleagues and I'll come back on second round.

chapters.summary: Menin asks what DOE can do to support NYC Kids Rise account activation. Chancellor Samuels reports the last five months saw the largest increase in activation on record and commits to continuing the program.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on NYC Kids Rise account activation

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So Chancellor, thank you for coming to our 529 conference for NYC Kids Rise and for what you've done as superintendent in districts 13 and 3 to support the program.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We believe it is so important to expand upon this program and we want to understand what can DRE do to better support schools to work with families to view and activate their

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: New York City Kids Rise accounts.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Thank you, Speaker Manon.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And as you know, the last five months have been the largest increase in activation in our New York City Rise accounts

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: in the records that they keep.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And so I am committed to the program, and we've done significant work with our superintendents and work with our partners at Kids Rise to ensure that our schools are continuing to

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: give the necessary resources to parents that they need to be able to sign up and activate their accounts, specifically in our most marginalized populations.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: So we are absolutely working on that and committed to doing that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Menin raises the Mariela Graham contract splitting incident and asks about DOE procurement protocols above and below $25,000. Scott explains the procurement thresholds and processes, and the Chancellor acknowledges the need to strengthen purchasing practices.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on procurement protocols and Mariela Graham incident

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I want to shift gears.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It was reported last week that former District 3 Deputy Superintendent Mariela Graham split contracts for the same vendor to the same vendor.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: bypass the typical financial oversight of the contracts.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The contract with this vendor resulted in the placement of a former DOE employee with substantiated charges of sexual misconduct in a Manhattan school.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So this incident, of course, raises concern that there may be more widespread practice of contracts being broken up to avoid appropriate spending scrutiny.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And in fact,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: SCI's report on the matter noted that schools are systemically plagued by vendor check splitting and other procurement problems.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The report concluded, quote, violations of procurement rules often resulted in financial losses to the DOE, led to state and federal prosecutions, and or prevented the DOE from working with the most qualified

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: vendors at fair market prices.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Can you describe DOE's typical procurement protocols and how that differs for contracts over and under $25,000?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And before I turn it over to Alicia, I want to address the specifics of the specific question you raised.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And, you know, I have devoted my career to expanding opportunities for young people across the city.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I want to focus on building a public school system for families that does not sacrifice the education of our children for bureaucracy.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: But having said that, I do regret the lapses in policy and procedures that took place.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: when I was a superintendent in District 3.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: The actions and questions were undertaken in pursuit of educational opportunity for children, not for personal gain, and benefited no one other than children in the district.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: While our current system

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: in NYC public schools tries to strike a balance between system-wide purchasing and ensuring that schools and districts have a level of autonomy to

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: get to day-to-day transactions as well as their vision.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We have a critical need to strengthen and improve purchasing practices across our system.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And as a chancellor,

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: This is something I'm really going to be deeply focused on.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I'm working to make sure that it's easier to truly deliver for students with training and oversight that is enhanced but supportive, not imperative.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And so I want to really turn it over to Alicia to talk a little bit more about the procurement process on the 25,000.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: Good morning, Alicia Beloui.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: I forgot to state that earlier.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: So as the Chancellor stated, schools have autonomy because it's seen as a way to allow schools to get the goods and services that they need as quickly as possible.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: And so with that, schools have

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: They allotted what we would call non-contractual spend threshold of $15,000

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: for goods and $25,000 for services with an individual vendor, that they follow the proper standard operating procedures for purchasing.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: For any spend above that $15,000 and $25,000 cap, there are specific types of procurement methods that they're supposed to initiate when they know that they have a need for a good or service.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: I would say at the simplest form, the up to $100,000 schools are allowed to initiate what we call a simplified procurement process.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: And it's probably the simplest and the fastest.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: It doesn't require going to PEP.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: It also doesn't require controller registration.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: Anything above that has to go through a formal procurement process.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: Primarily, the preferred process would be a competitive bid, but we also have RFPs.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: We have negotiated services.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: We have multiple task award contracts.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: The multiple task award contracts are the contracts that are primarily used at the school level.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: And what it does is it's a competitive process, but when it's initiated and a vendor is awarded a contract, it serves as kind of like a pre-qualified list of vendors who can provide a good or services.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: So a lot of...

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: A lot of times you'll see contracts in that category that are for professional development, for assessments and things of that nature.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: Schools also leverage what we call a listing application, which is essentially a sole source.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: Contract and it's used for textbooks, educational software, because those items tend to be proprietary and you cannot purchase it anywhere else.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: Once a procurement action is initiated by a program office, they work very closely with my team to go through all of the necessary steps and processes that are outlined in our procurement.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: policy.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: So that would mean, you know, releasing the bid.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: That would mean receiving responses, analyzing those responses or evaluating them, and then moving toward award.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: Once the contract is awarded, then we go through another due diligence process where we're doing background checks to ensure that the vendor is responsible.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: All vendors are required to go through a data privacy protocol, and they have to sign data privacy agreements.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: And once they're done with that, then we present those contracts to the panel for educational policy.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: We meet with the panel before the actual panel vote to give them an overview of each of the contract items that they're going to vote on.

utterances.speaker: Sarita Scott
utterances.text: And once you're done with that, once we're done with that,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We hold the public panel meeting and they vote on it there.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Once it is approved by panel, or if it's not approved, we don't move forward with it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But once it's approved by panel, we enter into the contracting stage.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Once we get to the contracting stage, that's kind of where additional negotiations occur.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And once we finalize that, then it gets submitted to the comptroller for registration.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's just cliff notes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I mean, that's a very cliff notes version of it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: A couple questions on that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You mentioned textbooks as an example.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Why would tech, and you mentioned that they're proprietary.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Why would.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Textbooks be a proprietary suit that seems kind of commonplace why would they be proprietary because you so oftentimes

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: so textbooks are proprietary to the actual publisher and oftentimes you can only purchase those items from the publisher and that's the definition of a sole source if i can't purchase it anywhere else but with that with that particular vendor

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: then that that that's that's a sole source it's it's it's it's not competitive so then how does do we ensure that the staff with the authority to sign contracts that are not

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: competitively bid are not improperly splitting payments to avoid standard procurement protocols.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Repeat the question again, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it goes into the questions that I was formerly asking the Chancellor, which is how does DOE ensure that the staff or the authority to sign the contracts that aren't competitively bid

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: aren't improperly splitting the payments to avoid the standard procurement process?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I think you're referencing the non-

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: procurement violations.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I think, so we provide training to staff that are, who have the authority to initiate procurement actions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In addition to that, we also have standard operating procedures.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Now what I will say is that, you know, clearly if these things are happening, then there's always room for improvement.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And as a part of the procurement reform initiative that was recently launched at New York City Public Schools, we're looking at ways to strengthen

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: auditing and oversight at the practices at the school level in order to identify those inefficiencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we're also seeking to strengthen our training, primarily focusing on ensuring that training is occurring on an annual basis and that it's certified, and also strengthening the training around what we deem as non-contractual purchasing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In addition to that, we're also looking at ways to modernize our procurement infrastructure, looking at ways of centralization and also making upgrades to our existing procurement technology.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So just building on what you just said, so there are...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Is currently an effort underway to review the existing procurement protocols?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You mentioned an annual training, but is there more that is being done to reform the procurement process to prevent these things from happening in the future?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, we're in the preliminary stages of developing that, yes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But there is an actual procurement reform that is focused on these things, so increasing governance primarily at the school level.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we also think that centralizing the procurement function itself will increase transparency and accountability and visibility.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, so just in closing, I would just say you mentioned you're in the preliminary process of reviewing that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I would like to get an update on that, so an update to my office directly and to this committee on exactly what is happening to reform the process.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I have more questions, but I want to pass it over to our chairs now.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about procurement reform feedback from providers and the timeline for PEP contract review. Scott says they are in preliminary stages of reform, looking at centralization and technology upgrades, and that PEP briefings occur one week before votes.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on procurement reform and provider feedback

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Actually, I wanted to just piggyback off the speaker's questions on the procurement because I love talking about this topic.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But have you also looked at, because I know it's a separate system than what MOX has with Passport and all of that, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And have you gotten feedback from providers and different folks who maybe perhaps use both systems, right, who can then look at it and say, here are some best practices from either or that would actually help us to streamline things so that we're not spending so much time working on this?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think that would be helpful to maybe go back to the community groups that use the system and try to get.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Their feedback.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If you're in the preliminary stages, I'm just wondering what the input is from that side.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We haven't gotten to that stage.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I mean, so right now, we're really invested in trying to find a replacement system.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The way that Passport is configured to date, it's not really functional for our use.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Oh, no, yeah, nor would I suggest you use it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No offense.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we're engaged in conversations with City Hall's innovation team and the Mayor's Office of Contract Services to develop a plan or a pathway for replacing our procurement system, because I think that that's one of the

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: biggest challenges, and it's one of the reasons why we don't have as much transparency and visibility as we would like.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, yeah, the only reason why I suggest that is because I know that oftentimes the providers are using both systems, and so I'd be curious to see what overlap there could be to streamline things.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then also on that note.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Because I think you had mentioned, if I heard you correctly, that at some point in terms of the RFP process, the PEP needs to vote for it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But before it gets to that point where they have to vote on it, I'm assuming, and correct me if I'm wrong, that they've already gotten a download and have been prepped beforehand of the different groups that are part of it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So in other words, they're part of the process so that it's not a surprise, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we do hold briefings at least one week prior to the actual panel session, and the actual agenda is posted publicly.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: 10 days before the actual meeting.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it gives them time to read through the proposals and ask as many questions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And after we have the briefings, we often follow up with additional responses to questions that may arise during those meetings.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, so one week before actually seems kind of a short time period before.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And the only reason why I'm saying that is because it seems like you all on your end have to go through all these arduous steps before you get to that point.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so I'm just wondering if there could be that input beforehand, because then the last thing is, like, you have to go through this entire thing, and then what if they don't?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Like the final bidders or whoever.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I completely understand what you're saying, but it takes about a year for us to even get to the point where we can present it to panel, and we're generally scrambling.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But again, we're looking at ways to streamline our processes, and maybe there's something that we could incorporate that would allow us to do it, but I can't make that promise right now.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No, I get it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And why does it take so much longer versus other city agencies?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I mean, just to simply ask it that way.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, the end-to-end procurement process is relatively long, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, releasing bids, awarding them, and then, you know, oftentimes when we release a bid, we're awarding contracts to multiple vendors, and that in itself can take some time.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So you have to ensure that once, you know, we award a contract, we've got to do, we have to do negotiation on pricing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We also have to do background checks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That can come.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: be pretty extensive and then we have and even and if something comes up in that background check process we have to do our due diligence

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: by going back and doing some investigation to ensure that when we deem a vendor responsible and responsive

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: that we've done all of the necessary taken all of the necessary steps that to take that we were required to take to ensure that these vendors can actually work with our students and in our schools right

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: and and i will say i think in terms of this plus the purchasing power that DOE has and the procurement process.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I wonder if this is an area where AI technology can maybe play a part.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Understanding that legally, I get that there are certain mandated steps you need to take, but then maybe truncating the amount of time that it takes in between each step would be great to look at.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We would love to be able to leverage it, but we would have to make sure we're leveraging it responsibly.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Right, of course.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Chair, Chair Lee, may I, first of all, General Counsel Vladek, I swear to tell the truth on all the things.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I just thank you for letting me add to this because my office has actually, for a variety of reasons, been pretty deeply engaged in our procurement processes and

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: school and district level purchasing over the last few years.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've actually been doing kind of a health assessment, so to speak, to see how strong and robust our practices are in terms of protecting against fraud.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But not only for that purpose, also for the purposes that you're identifying, which is to help communities.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Unities understand better how our procurement process works.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To level the playing field for vendors so that we're expanding opportunities for businesses around the city

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: and organizations around the city to work with us to make sure that bad actor vendors who we may be able to identify but wouldn't necessarily be visible to a school are

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: identified, referred where appropriate.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so all of the things that you and the speaker are pointing to are elements of this very complex and vibrant ecosystem.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I think

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think I have a certain amount of pride in the core soundness of the apparatus, given the size and scale of what we're doing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It is so complex.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But at the same time, just to echo the Chancellor's point about trying to find that balance between

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: efficiencies, oversight, central visibility, consistency across the system versus schools and districts knowing their individualized needs better than anyone else.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That is a balancing that requires constant adjustment.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so all of the reforms and oversight that you and the speaker and Alicia have referred to are all part of that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And also, sorry, we've been joined by Councilmember Epstein, who I forgot to mention you earlier, sorry about that, as well as Councilmember Gutierrez and Marte.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the importance of extending mental health continuum funding. Deputy Chancellor Puello-Perdomo describes the program serving 50 schools in the South Bronx and Brownsville with 2,330 clinical services provided this year.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on mental health continuum funding

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I'm just going to quickly shift to mental health, both on the continuum as well as the school-based mental health clinics.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So what are your thoughts on the importance of extending funding for the mental health continuum and what would happen if the $5 million funding is not restored, which I know we talk about this every year, but I love this program and I know you guys do too.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I wanted to know if you could speak to that.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Well, the idea of mental health and what our young people need on that continuum is really critical and something that,

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: as I've become chancellor, it's even more profound as I see firsthand how our young people oftentimes are struggling.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And so we are deeply committed to it.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We have resources that we've dedicated to mental health within our system.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And I want to turn it over to Deputy Chancellor Padermo to talk a little bit more about this.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Lee and members of the councils who are here today.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: So we, yes, agree and value city council commitments and partners to this work.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: I want to emphasize again that this is a share.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: This specific program is a share of support.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: So it's a combination of resources that go into health and hospital, the Department of Health and New York City Public Schools, and only

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: a smaller percentage of the funding come to New York City Public School because the clinical services are actually residing within health and hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: So we believe we value the partnership.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: We understand that our young people are navigating challenges and making sure that they get the help that they need quickly is critical.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: And for those 50 schools that have benefited from this program, not only have we been able to onboard

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: 16 Article 31 clinics, in addition to the other clinics that reside in New York City Public Schools, servicing over 400 schools in total.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about school-based mental health clinic services. Puello-Perdomo reports 250 clinics serving approximately 400 schools and 180,000 students, with 16 Article 31 clinics embedded through the continuum.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on school-based mental health clinics

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then actually that's a perfect segue to the school-based mental health clinic question I had, which is you said 400 schools was for the continuing?

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: For the overall.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: For the continuing, we support 50 schools.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: 50, right.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: Primarily in the South Bronx, Brownsville, and those 50.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: Schools are supported in two ways for clinical support.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: They're supported by the five expansion of clinicians in some of the hospitals, which are Lincoln Hospital, Morisania, Kings County, Woodhull, and East New York.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: So the students who didn't get access to the clinical support there, we then embedded 16 schools, mental health clinics, into the schools.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: That's separate and apart from the other.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: Plthora of clinics that already exist within the school system.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Awesome.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then for the school-based mental health clinics, how many students have been served so far in FY26?

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: In terms of the continuum, if that's what you're speaking to, the mental health overall clinics are under my colleague here, but the continuum itself,

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: We have a total of 2,330 clinical services that have been provided this year, and that includes a range.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then for the school-based mental health clinics, how many have been provided?

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: We can look into that, how that's tracked.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: What I can say is that out of the 250 clinics that we have in the DOE, they service approximately 400 schools and around 180,000 students.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: That's in addition.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: To our social worker and our school counselors that are also part of our ecosystem of mental health and wellness support, because I do want to echo

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: that those services are there in a tier.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: Not every student will necessarily need a clinical intervention, but when needed, we have, I think, a very robust

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: footprint, and we need to continue to do more so that our parents know all of the supports that exist within the system, and children are getting those direct services from our schools.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then which services, because I know the, at least for the school-based mental health clinics, they're mostly Medicaid reimbursement.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so what are the services that are not covered by that program?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I mean, sorry, well, I mean, that are not covered under the Medicaid funding.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: What are the clinical services that are not covered under the Medicaid funding?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sorry, let me re-ask.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the school-based mental health clinic services are excluded.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And how many, wait, excluded from the Medicaid reimbursement, but that's with the Article 31s, right?

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, so the Article 31s get the reimbursement.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And what's the gap, if any, for services?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In the school-based mental health clinics that maybe are not covered by the reimbursement.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: See, we can get back to you on the specific questions about the reimbursement.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: What I do want to emphasize that because we also have an ecosystem of social workers across our school system that are working in-house, that that's another layer of support that can get done.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: And if you want more specific nuances about the reimbursement, we can definitely get back to you on that.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the 97 filled central pedagogical positions affected by the $2 million savings and which divisions are impacted. Michael Cheatham says they are working with program teams to identify unfilled positions to prioritize, with no disruption to schools.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on central headcount reductions in savings plan

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, perfect, especially with a lot of the Medicaid cuts coming down the line.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: For the savings plan, how many of the 97 filled central pedagogical positions are affected and which divisions are impacted or excluded?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What initiative are you referencing?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, I mean, based on the savings plan.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Hold on.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Let me just bring up a more detailed question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There, hold on, let me just read this whole, oh.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: The vacancy reductions.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Oh, vacancy reductions.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So actually, let me just go through this.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The executive plan includes $922.2 million, correct me if I'm wrong, savings plan in FY27, which grows in the out years.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And the majority of the savings plan relies on cost containments and class size due to and due process cases.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the remainder of savings are focused on contract reform, OTPS spending reduction, and other spending efficiencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So out of this large savings plan, which items were identified by the chief savings officer, and were any of these savings identified by OMB?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: How did DOE and OMB work together on the savings plan?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Right, so all savings proposals were submitted by New York City Public Schools in collaboration with City Hall and OMB.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, and they were all accepted by OMB?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, the ones that obviously made it into the budget.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And there's a $2 million savings for a reduction in central headcount, which would move some pedagogical positions out of central administration positions into unfilled school-based positions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So according to DOE data, there are currently, this is what I meant, sorry.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So according to DOE data, there are currently 97 filled pedagogical positions in central across multiple divisions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So how many of these positions is DOE planning to move and eliminate?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Yes, thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: You know, that's one of our values is to make sure that we're really supporting schools with our human resources.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And that's getting closer and ensuring that teachers and all of the pedagogues that we have are getting closer and closer to schools.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And so I think, Michael, you might need to be sworn in.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: But I'm going to turn it over to Michael Cheatham to talk about this some more.

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: Good morning, my name is Jidam.

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: It's where the truth.

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: But yes, on the reduction, you're talking about the reduction of central staff or are you talking about the access?

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: Just want to clarify because there's multiple.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, so according to the DOE data, there's currently 97 filled pedagogical positions in central across multiple divisions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So how many of these positions is DOE planning to move and eliminate?

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: Well, there's various titles, so we're going to just work with our program offices to achieve the savings, the target.

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: So you're saying 97 pedagogical positions?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, currently 97 filled pedagogical positions in central across multiple divisions.

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: I'm not, that's data that I'm not familiar with on that part.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, so which divisions potentially could see a reduction in pedagogical headcount?

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: Well, this is going to be, we're going to work with the divisions across all teams to make sure that they identify, it's going to cross various titles, vacancies that haven't been filled for some time are going to be the first ones that we prioritize.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, so that hasn't been finalized yet.

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: The central budgets were working when on teams to when we roll it out for next fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I know that it is often helpful to have the pedagogical staff in DOE Central, which is something you guys were talking about, I think, at the CEC meeting last time in our district, when crafting the programming for specific student populations.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So how will DOE ensure that the savings plan will not negatively impact that work?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So throughout our process of looking through savings, even proposing and submitting them, we wanted to make sure, one of the key things that the chancellor wanted to make sure was that there was no disruption to the schools whatsoever.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, so no disruption to the programming services, great.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, which is what we wanted to make sure as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $17.8 million vacancy reduction in school support associated with 197 civilian positions. Cheatham confirms these are unfilled positions being prioritized and that no school-based social workers or guidance counselors are affected.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on school support vacancy reductions

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Also, the plan includes a vacancy reduction plan of which $17.8 million is cut in the school support UA.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And this savings is associated with 197 civilian positions set to be cut from the school support.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So what types of positions are being eliminated there?

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: I think this is going to the earlier question.

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: We're working with the program teams and across various titles, prioritizing positions that have been unfilled for some time.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: And working our way through.

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: That process.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, right.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To be clear, these are positions that were never filled.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Or?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Yes, that's where we're prioritizing and starting to work through.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then if you could, so this will not reduce the budgeted headcount for social workers, guidance counselors, or other similar positions?

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: The school-based insiders?

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And what's the current vacancy rate for social workers and guidance counselors, and are any of these vacant positions identified for the savings plan?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We can get back to you on that number.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Michael Cheatham
utterances.text: Confirm that no, none.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We can confirm.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: that that's not a part of a savings plan to reduce guidance counselors and social workers in schools.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Perfect, okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how DOE plans to optimize transportation routes without disrupting service. Deputy Chancellor Moran describes the Strides modernization program, GPS tracking, ridership tracking, and new routing algorithms to reduce travel time for students with IEPs.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on transportation route efficiencies

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And the FY27 budget includes 27.9 million reduction, which grows to 55.9 million in the out years due to transportation route efficiencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: How is DOE planning to optimize transportation routes without disrupting service?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And which the council is constantly fighting to improve.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This came up in the last hearing as well.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: So thank you for this question.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: When I think about our bus routes and our bus contracts, we have obviously decades-old contracts, as the chair talked about.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We have over 9,000 routes that we do every day for 150,000 students.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: This is something that I've learned.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Probably the most about this topic since becoming chancellor and you know thinking about the modernization that we need to do how we make our book our

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: our roots actually more efficient i'm going to turn it over to deputy chancellor kevin moran to speak a little bit more about that good afternoon everyone um

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I want to thank you very much for that question.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: It was the modernization began here in this chamber in 2018 with a series of local laws.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: In 2019, it was local law 32, 33, and 34.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: mandating GPS and other pieces of information that were not, one, transparent and where we couldn't see accountabilities.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So over the past many years, we implemented this modernization effort, one of which is actually having people log in every day when they drive the bus.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: That's up to 95%.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So I want to thank our drivers across all our companies for logging in, whereas parents can see now in their New York City school bus app.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Where their children are at any given time.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And 900 bus routes this school year started what's known as ridership tracking, where students can actually badge on the yellow school bus and badge off, and there's where we can determine time traveled.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: What happens now this fall is we start our routing optimization, all predicated on our Strides program, which is

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: our modernization program that took all 39 legacy systems and put them into one system so we can actually do something with the data that we're collecting that wasn't spread across disparate systems.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So now, for this September, we're actually going to run for all of our, what you call, curb to school.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Which is door-to-door busing, where our students are going to get this new routing system.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: It's our first modern effort to do the routing.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: What that'll do is allow us to reduce routes and travel times.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Our students with IEPs are traveling 137% more time to get to school than their gen ed peers.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And so what we want the routing algorithms to do is to reduce time travel, find efficiencies.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: But I think it's important to realize

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: our standard operating

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: procedures maybe not being adhered to at the school level that relates to this specific issue, whereas by default, a student with transportation on their IEP is defaulted into the

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: smaller type bus settings rather than being exposed to a continuum of least restrictive environments.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And so students could get on a smaller bus at age four and graduate high school at 22 years old in the same small passenger bus.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So what this effort is to do is introduce

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: where appropriate and where comfortable, a less restrictive environment within traveling.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So that is ultimately cheaper.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We do anticipate cost savings.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And ultimately, we believe it's the right thing to do.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I have a bunch of questions on 2K, 3K, which I'm going to forego because I have a feeling a lot of my colleagues are going to ask about 3K, 2K programming.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I will play cleanup at the end if there's any other questions that haven't been asked.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But I do want to just mention that it would be great if we could.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As you mentioned, Chancellor, Deputy Chancellor, sorry, Deputy Chancellor, the pay parity across all the different systems.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I also have a bill in the council about that versus the school-based and the CBO-based 3K program.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So whatever we can do to bring it as close to.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Similar as possible would be great.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about DOE's plan to expand the Black Studies curriculum and how many schools will use it next year. First Deputy Chancellor Gentes describes pilot districts, professional learning, and integration with NYC Reads implementation checklists.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on Black Studies, Latinidad, and AAPI curriculum expansion

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then my last question is just on the Black Studies, Latinidad, and AAPI curriculum, which I know that this has been an effort.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: over the last couple of years that the council has made.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So over the last three fiscal years, the council has invested a lot of money into the education equity action plan initiated in the black studies curriculum.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: DOE reports this curriculum is now in over 400 schools, and the curriculum has been fully developed.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The council's funding supports work to update this living curriculum, as well as professional development for educators.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So how does DOE plan to support the expansion of the Black Studies curriculum to additional schools, and how many schools does DOE expect will be using the Black Studies curriculum next school year?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Thank you so much for this question.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We are deeply committed to the Black Studies curriculum, the Latin and DAG curriculum, AAPI curriculum, all of the Hidden Voices resources.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And what we are doing now is ensuring that at the school, at the superintendent and principal level, that there is a pathway for seamless implementation.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And the person leading that initiative is sitting right next to me, First Deputy Chancellor Danielle Gentes.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I'll leave Danielle to talk more specifically about how we're going to make sure that that happens.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: Good afternoon and thank you for asking.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: Since our last time here for the preliminary budget, we've made a lot of progress around the work that we're doing, getting our schools and our communities ready for a deeper implementation of these resources.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: We discussed last time that we've had these resources.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: We've been building these resources over time.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: But we needed to get better at helping our schools, our district teams and our schools, make strong connections so that they're an embedded fabric of the Tier 1 curriculum and not just resources that were being used unevenly.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: So we have spent the better part of spring.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: Deeply involved in professional learning about using these resources.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: We have also selected some pilot districts to learn deeper about implementation with starting with five.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: They're coming to an end this month and growing to nine to launch next year.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: One of the things that we've done that's really exciting, and this is more like hot off the press kind of example, is how we can use these resources against the district's chosen tier one curriculum.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: So an example that came out of the work of the social studies team and the literacy teams at Central with the district teams and hitting our schools is, for example, in

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: in grade four of the HMH module on what makes someone a hero, they were able to bring in from our hidden voices the story of Sylvia Mendez

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: and her family's fight for desegregation and use that to complement the curriculum and go deeper to

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: help children really understand heroism, standing up for rights, and deepening their engagement in the knowledge-building curriculum with really strong historical figures.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: And we've been seeing those kinds of stories, whether it's where we can literally intersect this resource right into the curriculum for ELA.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: We're also doing that with our social studies curriculum.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: The vast majority of that curriculum is our

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: in-house passport curriculum.

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: And so we've also, as part of some of the outgrowths of

utterances.speaker: Danielle Gentes
utterances.text: when Chancellor Samuels came in and the transition of the cabinet really did survey and talk with our superintendents about the depth with which they were using these resources to help sort of drive these next steps that we are already seeing some outcomes from.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And just to add this idea of how do you integrate New York City Reads with the cultural responsive work that we're doing, as a part of our implementation process for New York City Reads,

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We do have implementation checklists that our superintendents use, and we are infusing now

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: the Black Studies curriculum, the Latin and that curriculum when it comes, and Hidden Voices in that implementation checklist so that superintendents have an option to be able to see what's happening in their schools and report on that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, that's actually good to know because I know part of the challenge is that it's opt-in, and so the question is how do we get as many principals to offer this curriculum to the students as possible.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I, that's good to hear.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just in terms of whether or not, in terms of how much money would be needed to expand the program and how can the Latinidad and AAPI studies learn lessons in terms of how to get a lot of the schools to offer this programming?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I think.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
