utterances.text: Well, in terms of the funding that would be needed, we can get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: But the idea of how do you integrate and create an interdisciplinary approach to

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: learning experiences for young people and how do we put the wind beneath the wings around that happening, I think that's a systemic responsibility for us working with our superintendents, working with our principals to create those experiences for young people.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And I think there is a desire.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: A desire to actually do this work on behalf of, on the part of our

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: superintendents and the part of our school as I think there we've heard if there's one

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: criticism of New York City Reads is that the tier one curriculum is really meant for vast number of people around the nation and the desire for us to get

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: curriculum that really connects with young people in the classroom that is culturally responsive is an area that we're really serious about working towards.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz presses on procurement accountability, citing SCI reports of $386 million in sub-$25,000 contracts and multiple examples of principals circumventing procurement rules. General Counsel Vladek cites active investigation concerns but acknowledges systemic issues need addressing.
chapters.title: CM Dinowitz on procurement accountability and SCI investigations

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it to Chair Dynowitz.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I have a few questions about a few different topics, but I want to start where one of the things Speaker Menon brought up.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Which is the stories that have come out recently about the procurement and the contracts and Mariela Graham when you were superintendent.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And, you know, of course, I think you've shown time and again over the past few months your ability to confront an issue and say, hey,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There were mistakes made, but it's exactly what we want our students to do.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But we also want students and adults alike to be held accountable and know that moving forward, changes are going to be made and also look back to actually discuss what actually happened.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: This is the largest school system in the country.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: This is the largest agency in the city.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It's about a third of our budget.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Parents throughout

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Throughout the city, there's a deep level of trust, whether you're a parent or a taxpayer, that we must have in our school system.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Parents send their kids to school every day and trust that the teachers in the school system are going to do right by their kids.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And we pay taxes, and we trust that those tax dollars are going to go to where they need to go.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So this is a question about accountability at the top, because it's critical that people are held accountable and that the culture of accountability is set from the very top, because all the safeguards

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: are all well and good, but they're meaningless unless they're instituted and demonstrated at the top.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And just to make sure everyone knows what we're talking about, according to SCI, it's $386 million in fiscal year 25

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: were from these individual contracts that were below $25,000 or $15,000, which don't hold the same scrutiny as those above their amount.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So that is a significant sum of money.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And over the years, SCI has...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Done investigations, and this is all online for anyone to see, about how people have circumvented these rules in order to be below that $25,000 mark.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And so, you know, in 2024, a principal knowingly approved multiple purchase orders below $25,000.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Provided by two separate companies that had common ownership.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Another principle circumvented the DOE bidding process by approving purchases separated into $5,000 increments for two vendors.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There were manipulations of bids, artificial competing bids.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Another principle circumvented DOE procurement policy.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And of course, in the case of Mariela Graham, we all saw the articles.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: As SCI said,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: violated several DCB rules by improperly entering into a non-bid negotiated services contract valued at $180,000

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: with a non-DOE contracted vendor for the purpose of circumventing DCB bid thresholds and DOE's multiple task award contract processes.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So there's a lot of layers of this, but I first, if you could tell the committee and the public what your role was during that time.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: So, again, I regret the lapses in policy and procedure that took place while I was the superintendent, and the actions in question were all meant

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: in the pursuit of educational opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: Chair Denowitz, I think you, and I'm sure the committee can appreciate that when there is active speculation going on in the press about

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: past investigations, prospective investigations, as counsel, it is

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: unfortunately sometimes my job to curb the instincts of our very open and forthcoming chancellor and counsel and advise that we are not going to comment on

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: questions involving law enforcement.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: SCI, of course, is a law enforcement agency that are actively open and stirring in the press.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: Okay, so I'm going to assume, please.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: If I could just finish, though, because I do want to speak to the broader points that you made.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: Again, I think some of the answer to your question is embedded in the question itself.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: When SCI talks about $386 million in non-contracted spending, that is across 1,600 schools

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: and however many, 35 district offices and all of the staff and superintendents.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: And so it's critically important that we identify when someone has engaged in criminal activity or misconduct.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: We work closely with law enforcement, as I mentioned earlier, when those things happen.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: But it's just unfortunately the case that there are bad actors out in the world who see our

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: budget and see an opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So I don't just want to talk about just bad actors.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There are systemic problems that I am going to ask about.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: Agreed, but I think what I'm trying to say is it's important to make those distinctions, right?

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: That there's a very important difference between some of the examples in the report you're referring to where people were indicted and went to jail and examples where we discover at the central level that we need to strengthen training.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: We need to recalibrate how much of discretion or decision-making about spending happens at the school or the district level as opposed to at central.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I want to talk about accountability.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It sounds like this may currently still be under investigation, so I won't ask about that.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But I will ask about the decision.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks about the decision to keep Mariela Graham on and promote her despite SCI's recommendation for immediate termination. Vladek cites active speculation about reopened investigations and declines to comment, but confirms he personally signed off on the disciplinary decision.
chapters.title: CM Dinowitz on Mariela Graham personnel decision

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm interested to know about the decision to keep Mariela Graham on and to promote her.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Because the SCI recommended her immediate termination due to lapses in judgment.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And my understanding is she was given a two-week suspension under the previous administration.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: That's correct.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: And I'm also, once again, I apologize, Chancellor, but I'm just going to say that is a fact connected to an active, I don't know if there's an active investigation, but I do read the press carefully.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: What I will say is it is not uncommon.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: It is my office that receives SCI reports, works up what, not only

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: only the report, but the evidence that the investigative file that comes along with the report, and then makes a recommendation about proper disciplinary action.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So it was your office that recommended not to go by the recommendation of SCI?

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: Not going to comment on what happened in this case.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: What I will say is that it is not uncommon.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm asking about a closed investigation, or my understanding is it's a closed investigation.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The recommendation was made.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: This is outside of any current investigation that may be going on.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm asking about a personnel decision.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It's important for the city and this committee to understand that when there is what is a legal activity, right, that we understand why the decisions at the DOE were made that were

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: in conflict or in contrast to what the recommendations by SCI were.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So it's about trust and accountability.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: Certainly, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: I totally understand that.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: The challenge that I face at the moment as counsel is that there is active and,

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: to my mind, somewhat wild speculation going on in the press about whether the closed investigation may be reopened, whether the investigating agency properly fulfilled its obligations.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: With all of that open, I hope very much that at some point in the future we'll be able to return and get into more detail about this situation, but I think it is just too dynamic right now with too many moving.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: pieces for me to counsel my client to be as open as I know he would like to be and as I know you would wish him to be.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, just to be clear, I'm not the press.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm not asking about press stories.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm asking about a closed investigation that is public.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: This was from their public website.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I didn't acquire some secret documentation.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Of course.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: This is on their website.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Anyone can go to their website and download this.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: They list a number of things which I'm going to go through.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But it is a question, I think, of judgment and accountability, and I think the committee deserves to understand

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: that if the SCI makes recommendations, what the thought process is or what it isn't, it seems in this case, for not abiding by those recommendations and why this person gets a promotion when it was recommended they be terminated.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I think we deserve to understand those thought processes.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: I agree.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: I do not disagree.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: But what I'm saying is the matters that you are describing as closed

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: are being discussed in the press as possibly not closed or possibly something that law enforcement is being encouraged to reopen.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But you're a lawyer.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It's closed.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It's closed.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: Yes, but my job is not just to look back, but to look forward and assess risk and have a duty of care.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So looking forward then, looking forward, does the DEA make any commitment that if a...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Employee of the DOE engages in impropriety,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: breaks the law but doesn't go to jail, can they commit that there will be accountability for that person?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Like these are our tax dollars, these are our children.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Certainly.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So let's look forward.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: Chair Dinowitz, very important distinction.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: SCI's finding, SCI did not find that anyone broke the law.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: In this report.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: SCI's finding was that there was a violation of New York City Public Schools policy.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: That is not, breaking the law suggests illegal activity that could be criminally chargeable.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: That is not anywhere.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: In or near this report.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you for the clarification.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So in the case that someone violates policy, which resulted in an adult being in a classroom with children that shouldn't have been there, that this adult

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Presented serious lapses in judgment, SCI did recommend termination.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Looking forward, how seriously does the DOE take the recommendations of SCI, or are they just recommendations that DOE will not abide by?

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: We always take SCI's recommendations seriously.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: We also have an obligation to have fidelity to the idiosyncrasies of any given case.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: And so while I can't sit here and discuss the evidence that we reviewed that was developed by SCI in support of their report,

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: I can tell you that my team and I, I personally, signed off on the disciplinary decision that was made in this case.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And that was your, you signed off and that was your recommendation as well, or you just approved it legally?

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: I'm not going to discuss internal deliberations.

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: I'm willing to share the outcome, and that's what it was.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I will say it's a very lawyer answer, and I understand, but it's very disappointing.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I don't want to belabor this one employee, but this is indicative of problems that do exist.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I believe they're more systemic than any SCI investigation will let on, because any teacher...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Any professional who works in a school understands a lot of the nonsense that they've seen some principals spend money on.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I've been sent on professional developments.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Where thousands of dollars were spent under that 25.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: $5,000 cap, which deserved a lot more scrutiny than would ever be done under the $25,000 cap.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Given that, given that I understand you don't take SCI's recommendation for individual employees, they did make recommendations for improving the systems.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And that is not under litigation right now.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That's not under investigation.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So I'd like to talk about each of those.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks about SCI's recommendations for procurement reform including required training for principals, increased oversight of non-contracted vendors, and stronger safeguards for sole source procurements. The Chancellor commits to required training pending union negotiations and centralizing procurement.
chapters.title: CM Dinowitz on SCI procurement reform recommendations

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You mentioned training.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Enhanced training.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Now currently that training is not required by principals.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Is the DOE's policy is going, is the DOE going to make that training required for principals who deal with millions of dollars in their budgets?

utterances.speaker: Vladek
utterances.text: Chair, this is of course something we have to discuss with our union partners, but in my lawyerly view, we should, and I hope we'll be able to negotiate to that outcome.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Is that the view of the Chancellor as well?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That they should.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: SCI recommended increased oversight of non-contracted vendors.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: In what ways has DOE implemented or are attempting to implement this recommendation?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: So those recommendations also implied that we would need an influx of resources that we don't necessarily have at the moment.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And this is why at the top of the discussion when we were talking about procurement reform, one of the ways that we think we could address that is by centralizing the procurement function.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Because with it, with having that.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm sorry, just to clarify, centralizing procurement for those sub $25,000.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Because we'll have more, well, you know, that would make me accountable as the chief procurement officer.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Well, maybe not given some of these answers.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: You know, well, to some extent, right?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I mean, because then, you know, if you, you know, when, I mean, and I think you know this, when you decentralize things that, you know, it creates, you know, all of these pockets of vulnerabilities, if you, I mean, I think that would probably be the best term.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: It creates vulnerabilities, right, because you just don't have oversight.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And so I think centralizing the procurement function itself will solve for that to some extent.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Now, not saying it's going to solve for it all, right, because, like I said, we're 1,600 schools.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: But it creates, you know, it will allow us to standardize processes, internal processes.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: It will allow us to strengthen our standard operating procedures and also ensure that we're all.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: also looking at our policy to ensure that we can, you know, tighten that up to ensure that, you know, that we can minimize these occurrences in the future.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, in an ideal world, right, because the Chancellor mentioned autonomy of these schools, and for, I think, most all of them, that's very important.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: In an ideal world, you'd want to raise that $25,000.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You know, Deputy Speaker, Council Member Dr.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Nantasha Williams organized a roundtable of MWBE providers, and one of the concerns they raised was that it's hard for them to compete, especially for these sub-$25,000 contracts.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And my response to them is what I'll share on the record now, which is I think it would be great if your companies would more easily do business with DOE.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: However, we do have this systemic problem of these under $25,000 contracts, which has not to date been, I think, truly addressed.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And unfortunately, we can't just rely on...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Principals or school leaders volunteering to take training, I think they should be required to take this training, right, and be held accountable.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You know, if SCI makes recommendations, it's still a puzzle as to why someone who exercised serious lapses in judgment, which resulted in

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: An adult being in a room with children, he shouldn't have been in, would continue and be promoted.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: SCI recommended stronger safeguards to ensure the integrity of bids and procurement documentation.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Has the DOE implemented this recommendation?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: We haven't implemented a lot of the recommendations because, again, I had stated earlier that it's going to require some structural changes and also some changes to policy.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: These things take time.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: So it's under advisement, and we're currently working internally to figure out how we could roll it out.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What is a time?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I mean, you have the best lawyer in New York City sitting right there.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What's your timeline?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Unfortunately, I don't have a timeline in the moment, but it's all a part of the broader procurement reform strategy.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So I will ask, and I'll say it's under advisement, but SCI recommended increased scrutiny of sole source procurements.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: So they made a recommendation that we actually absolutely agree with, and that's something that I'm working with my team on, just in terms of ensuring that schools are seeking proper approvals for those items.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And what does proper approval look like?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: So, you know, for a sole source, you know, there are instances where vendors will say that they're the sole, you know, they're the sole provider of this good or service, right?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: But the truth of the matter is if a sole source item, if an item is not considered or good or service is not considered sole source, if I can purchase it elsewhere, right?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And so.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Well, just to be clear, I understand it as if you're a vendor and you tell me that it's sole source, that that's good enough.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That's not the case?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, please explain.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: So, yeah, vendors tell you that, right?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The chair Lee says check Amazon.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Exactly.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Check Amazon.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: If you say that you're the only person that sells this pen, right, it's my team or whoever, the school's responsibility to actually do the proper due diligence to ensure that they cannot purchase it elsewhere.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Because if you can purchase it elsewhere, then you can bid it out competitively.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Right?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Because the goal is to ensure that you're getting the best pricing, right, for this pen.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: But just, you know, a vendor stating in a letter that, you know, they're the sole provider of that, that to me, that's...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I could save you millions of dollars.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Shop DOE is the most expensive place to buy pens.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You ever go and shop DOE?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You don't go and shop DOE?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That's like the website.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That you're supposed to use.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It's like one pen, $15.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It's awful.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Go on, Councilmember Joseph, another forever educator, she knows.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So if you want to save millions of dollars, go on that website and watch your eyes bulge out.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And we're not allowed to use coupons.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: No coupons, no discounts, you can't go to Staples.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You seriously don't know about this?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: You're asking me if I don't know about Shop D.O.E.?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Shop D.O.E., yeah.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Of course I know about Shop D.O.E.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Of course you know about Shop D.O.E., okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: My God, I thought you said you didn't.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What a...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: She's never bought pens from Shop D.O.E.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Don't.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Go to a city agency.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: They have all their free swag you can go to.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, I do want to move on.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: This is certainly an issue that is of great importance and great concern that we will continue with.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: As time goes on, I hope all the legal issues are worked out so that information can be shared publicly.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But also getting a timeline on what those changes are.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: $300 million is no small chunk of change, and if there are ways to improve upon that, and also ideally, and someday in the future, allow for more of our NWBEs to have access to our schools, that would be the ideal scenario.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks about the $6 million cut to restorative justice. Chief Rampersant confirms approximately 250 of 960 schools would lose access to the program, representing over 25% of schools served, and that it would prevent expansion to additional schools.
chapters.title: CM Dinowitz on $6M restorative justice cut impact

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I want to ask about restorative justice.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: This is a program that you talk to any educator, any principal, they love it, it works, our students love it, it works, it's important.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It's important for their social, emotional health and their academics.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It provides alternative responses to conflicts at schools, including interventions when harm has happened, as well as practices that help to prevent harm and conflict by building a sense of belonging, safety, and social responsibility in the school community.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And again, this is something I've implemented in my classrooms.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I not only saw greater...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Community in my classroom, but my test scores went up, engagement in the classroom went up.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The fiscal 2027 budget includes a cut of $6 million.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: for sort of justice, and I want to say that again, a cut of $6 million for a program that worked and one that I believe the mayor believes in and actually campaigned on.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What would be the programmatic result if the $6 million in funding in the budget for fiscal 2026 is not added for fiscal 2027?

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: I also believe deeply in restorative practices, and I believe that in order to do it well, there has to be significant capacity building, which we've been working on over the last number of years within schools.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: So there's significant capacity to do this work, and I want to turn it over to Chief Mark Ramprasant to talk a little bit more about that.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Were you sworn in?

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: I swear to tell the truth.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It's not just the truth.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I think we have to go back.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It's the whole truth and also nothing but the truth.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: Nobody else did that, but I'll swear to tell the whole truth and nothing but the truth.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, and I appreciate the sentiments of a school teacher, an educator, to talk about the importance of restorative.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: And what you shared is exactly what would be missing if we're not able to continue to restore those funds.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: One of the things about the whole notion of restorative is the work that goes into it is to really

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: build muscle so that the work can continue, right?

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: So we don't need to continue to bring in vendors to provide services when we have strengthened all of our adults to continue to apply those services.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: You talked, you talked.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So are you, I just want to be clear on what you're saying.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Are you saying a $6 million cut will not result in any service reductions that we already have the professionals there to do the work and we're good?

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: That's not what I said.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: I swear to tell the truth.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yeah, that's the whole truth.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: Not yours, mine.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Good, good, good.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: So what I am saying to you is that the work is important.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: And the foundation of the work is to build the muscle of our staff to continue that work.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: Yes, it will be painful.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: If the $6 million is not restored.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: It will not allow us to continue to provide the services to the level that we provide today and continue to strengthen more schools to be able to carry out these practices and change mindsets and do all of the things that you just described.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It will result in a cut to services to schools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: How many schools will lose restorative?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: How many schools, if a $6 million cut is implemented, will lose access to restorative justice programming?

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: Approximately 250 or so.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: 250 schools will lose access to restorative justice program if the $6 million is cut.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: How many schools total receive?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Restorative justice programming?

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: About 960 or so at various levels.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So you're talking about over 25% of our schools which rely on these services.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: in these programs will lose access to those services.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The previous administration began Project Pivot, which provides similar programming to the restorative justice.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: However, the council is unclear on the details of this program and how it differs from restorative justice.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Families and students seem to prefer restorative justice programs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Would the DOE be interested in combining the resources for these programs to expand restorative justice programming?

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: So let me be clear, right?

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: So the programs provide two different services, right?

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: The restorative is a mindset shift.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: The project pivot is bringing in vendors to support schools in the areas where they're struggling.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: So if there is a safe passage issue, they're bringing in vendors to be outside of the schools to safely transport or allow young people to transport safely to and from school.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: It's some of those initial vendor.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: Direct provisions of resources.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: What Restorative is doing is changing the mindset and teaching our staff to be sort of more mindful of the why around students'actions, right?

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: They're two totally different, and interestingly enough, they do both exist in some of the same schools providing different services, but for the same outcomes, and that is for the betterment of our young people.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, so there's no two ways about it.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There's no shifting money around.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There's no two ways about it.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Restorative justice, the administration proposed cutting it by $6 million.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Over 25% of schools will lose this service.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: This is a critically important service and program for our students, for the safety and emotional well-being of our students, and as so many of us know, the academic benefits to restorative justice.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm going to pause there and turn it over for questioning.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And we're going to start with Council Member.

chapters.summary: Council Member Schulman asks about New Visions portal support from principals and air conditioning in communal spaces. Deputy Chancellor Moran reports 60% of public assembly spaces lack AC, with schools using ResoA grants to fund installations.
chapters.title: CM Schulman on New Visions portal and school air conditioning

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Shulman.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Time to get to her seat.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Councilmember Shulman.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: It's very good to see you, and thank you for all the things you've done in my district and come out and all of that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So I have two things.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: One is I wanted to do a quick follow-up on the new visions tool from the speaker.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So the new visions tool has a lot of support from principals, particularly in my district.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Many claim it's been instrumental in monitoring and improving student performance, graduation rates, and attendance.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So at a time when Envy is working across agencies to support every child and family is known, you know, I just want you to keep that in mind while the Division of Instructional and Informational Technology hasn't really shown a record of success yet building tools in schools.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: For the record.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, one.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The other question, which is sort of simple but not simple, what percentage of schools do not have air conditioning in communal spaces such as gymnasiums?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Nasiums, auditoriums, and cafeterias.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Are schools expected to secure reservoir funding for AC in these spaces if they do not have it?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And how is DOE working to ensure equitable access to air conditioning system-wide?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Every year, and particularly this year, I'm getting calls about this.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: In addition to which, you saw that we've had a number of 90-degree days even before we got to, like, it's only the second week in June.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And it's supposed to, with climate change, it's going to get warmer and everything else.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So please.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: Totally.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: If you've ever had to sit in a graduation in a school, in an auditorium, and we're upon graduation season.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And so we've been really working.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: And yes, a lot of our schools have used ResoA to fund these air conditions in these common areas.

utterances.speaker: Kamar H. Samuels
utterances.text: So I just want to turn it over to Kevin Moran to talk more about our plans there.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Yes, great seeing you and thanks for the question.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Certainly as it relates to air conditioning,

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We benefit greatly from the AC for All initiative that covers all of our classrooms and all of our instructional spaces.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We've taken the HEAT law and put it on our InfoHub for all principals, and I want to thank all our principals and administrators that do this work on a daily basis to make sure our students are not in spaces that exceed 88 degrees.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: It's 82 degrees, you need to take action according to state law, ed law, and 88, you need to not use that space.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So we're thankful for the support of the council for all instructional spaces.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And so that brings us to your question, as how we do public assembly spaces.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So I think we're right now hovering at 5,705 public assembly spaces.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Many of those spaces, you've all helped us through ResoA grants.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We funded them.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Schools have figured out a way to fund them.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Our Division of School Facilities had just done Aviation's Cafeteria, Sheepshead Bay's Gymnasium.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So we're working with our tax levy base.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: It is not a capitally funded category right now, so maybe that's the next best thing.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: But currently, to answer your question, the number of PA spaces with AC is at...

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: 2,300, so about 60% of our public assemblies do not currently have AC.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Okay, we'll talk about that at some other point.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I'm going to stop my question, but what I will ask the chair is if we can send written questions that we don't get to ask to public schools so that they can answer it, okay?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, very much.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Okay, and next we have Deputy Speaker Williams, followed by Councilor.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Councilmember Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair.

chapters.summary: Deputy Speaker Williams asks about the Mayor's Office racial equity plan submitted by NYC Public Schools. Karina Pollan outlines priorities including early childhood equity, pathways, inclusive access, MWBE supplier diversity, and instructional equity.
chapters.title: Dep. Speaker Williams on DOE racial equity plan

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So the Mayor's Office of Equity and Racial Justice released its first prelim racial equity plans this April.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: This plan, of course, includes many goals from New York City public schools.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Can you speak to what you submitted and if any of these goals have any fiscal implications for this year's budget?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Thank you so much for this question.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: As you know, thank you so much, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Williams.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: As you know, we are truly committed to equity and racial, certainly school integration.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And we are going, I'm going to turn it over to Karina Pollan to talk a little bit more about this, but make sure you swear in.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everybody.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I swear to tell the whole truth and nothing but the truth, so help me God.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I think she's putting you out of a job.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So as it relates to what is in the plan, I'll give you a quick overview on some of the priorities that we've established in the plan, beginning with early childhood, leading equity initiatives, impacting early childhood programs and services.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Pathways is a huge priority, leading equity-focused career, college and workforce readiness initiatives with the vision of inclusive access.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Make sure I'm reading it.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Inclusive access, leading inclusive and accessibility-focused racial equity programs, including supports for students with disabilities and multigenerational learners.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: For minority and women-owned businesses, lead supplier diversity and contracting and procurement related to equity commitments.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And with the first deputy chancellor's office leading instructional equity initiatives, including New York City Reads and New York City Solves.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: In addition to that, working with the different departments within the school system to ensure a strong implementation, tracking, and accountability around that work.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Yeah, and are any of these goals, do they have any fiscal implications?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So are there any budgetary items that are attached to you all achieving those goals?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: As it relates to the funding, most of this is designed around, these are not standalone initiatives,

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: and so what we're doing is leveraging the existing dollars, but making sure that we're prioritizing across existing budgets and operational decisions.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We are embedding racial equity into how we allocate resources, set our priorities, and also make policy and programmatic decisions across the system.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And so in a system that is diverse as we are in New York City Public Schools, I think this racial equity plan reinforces this work by prioritizing and making sure that every decision that's made and every implementation and accountability.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: is tied to this work.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Yes, and we've been asking all the agencies very similar questions, and I feel like there's a trend of many of the agencies saying that they are going to reappropriate or look at your existing budget, which is great.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And I know that some of these programs may be duplicative based off of what you recently submitted, but I do hope in

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: future fiscal years where there may be budgetary implications to actually do the work that you don't just use existing money, but there be dedicated funding streams to support many of the goals within your agency.

chapters.summary: Williams asks about the fair student funding formula and its limitations. CFO Scott notes new weights were added three years ago for students in temporary housing and high-need concentration, with ongoing analysis and willingness to reconvene the working group.
chapters.title: Dep. Speaker Williams on fair student funding formula

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So just a question about equity in school budgets.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Fair student funding created and implemented in 2007 to the 2008 school year is the main funding source for traditional city public schools.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: This formula adjusts school budgets based on a school student population and student needs.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: This formula is meant to promote equity in school budgets as schools are funded based on need.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Has there been any recent analysis of the SFF formula and its ability to promote equity?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And what are some of the limitations of this funding model?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So we, there are significant limitations or some limitations, but really SFS

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: has, the weights that we've used over the past have led to more, you know, equity in our school budget system.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And I'll turn it over to our chief financial officer to talk some more about that.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So as you may be aware, about three years ago, we actually added new weights to our fair student formula for

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: students in temporary housing and to capture concentration of high need at schools.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And so there are ongoing analysis, and that was based on recommendations from the FSF working group.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I know there's been a call to reestablish it, and I know the chancellor has already committed to us, you know, having the necessary conversations around it.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So while we didn't make any updates for this upcoming school year, it is conversations that we are prepared to have.

chapters.summary: Williams asks how class size funding distribution affects equity, noting significant funding goes to low-need schools. Erin Jahant explains last year's opt-in approach and this year's plan to prioritize higher ENI schools while also considering absolute numbers of students in need.
chapters.title: Dep. Speaker Williams on class size funding equity

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: The current class size funding distribution model allocates significant funding towards low need schools as they are typically the schools struggling with class size compliance.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Has New York City Public Schools done any analysis on how class size funding affects the equity of funding across schools and are there any considerations in how

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: To distribute class size funding more equitably.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We've thought, we've been thinking about how to prioritize schools and areas that have a high economic need

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: in the process around ensuring a reduction in class size.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And I'm going to turn it over to Erin to talk a little bit more about this.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Hi, I'm Erin Jahant.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I swear to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth about class size.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So I think, again, last year's approach was schools opted in to the funding, right?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So that was mainly driven schools that were in, you know, maybe had a greater gap in terms of where they were and where they needed to get to to decrease class size.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Because, again, it was driven by the schools.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We know that, you know, schools in, say, wealthier communities might have larger class sizes.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And so, again, I think the process last year that it was opting in from the schools up probably led to some of the funding or definitely led to the funding outcomes last year.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I think what we're looking at this year, right, acknowledging that equity is a critical priority of the chancellor, and we are looking at prioritizing schools in

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: higher ENI schools this year in the funding distribution, which we're working on right now.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: But that's a core part of our work, looking at higher ENI schools alongside schools that have space to comply.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I think it's also important to distinguish how we think about what a high ENI school is.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: If you just go by percentages, then some schools may look like

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: They're low ENI, and some schools may look obviously like they're high.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: If you have a school with 200 kids, that's 90%.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Free and reduced lunch, then that's going to be a number that's about 180 students.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: But if you look at a school that has 3,000 students and there are, you know, 50% ENI, that might look like it's a lot lower, but that would be 1,500 students, right?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So the way we're looking at ENI, we want to really focus on the numbers so that we're getting to

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: we're getting to address a large number of students rather than only the percentages, which may distort the actual number of students that you're impacting.

chapters.summary: Williams raises PTA funding disparities, teacher workforce diversity with 53% white educators in a majority non-white student body, and demographic disparities in due process cases. She also asks about STEAM center investments and geographic distribution.
chapters.title: Dep. Speaker Williams on PTA disparities, teacher diversity, and due process equity

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Okay, in the interest of time, and I'll make sure we send the questions, but I just wanted to talk about these topics really quickly, still under the theme of equity in school budgets.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I know this has come up a ton of times.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I feel like it's coming up a lot more recently.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Just wealthier districts reported raising of PTAs, so there are PTAs that have reported raising over a million dollars, while other schools report no additional funding through their PTA.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And so, again, we'd love to see how New York City Public Schools is addressing this particular issue when it comes to school resources.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: On another topic, teacher recruitment and retention.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: You know, we know that there has been a considered focus on workforce diversity.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: 53% of educators are white and the majority of public school students are not.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Love white teachers, love Mr.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Peterson.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: He's literally the only person that has ever made me love math in my entire academic career.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: However, New York City is a very diverse school district.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And so just wanting to understand, as you all have been increasing the funding for recruitment this year to comply with class size mandates,

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: using this as an opportunity to continue your efforts to hire a diverse workforce.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Like we know you have New York City men teach to recruit black, Latino, and Asian men.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So we'd love to just understand how you all are using your additional funds to continue to increase diversity amongst school educators.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Equity and due process cases.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So we have a term and condition

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: that the council has received from New York City Public Schools showing the demographic makeup of the student population with due process cases settled or closed

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: in fiscal 2025, which is significantly different than the demographic makeup of public school student enrollment in the 2024-2025 school year.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So just want to understand your thoughts on this difference and what it means for the question of equity and who is actually receiving these services when our school systems are failing children who have

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: learning differences, as I like to say.

chapters.summary: Williams asks about STEAM centers in Southeast Queens near the airport. Deputy Chancellor Gentes describes the Brooklyn STEAM Center, the new Bronx STEAM Center with Montefiore, and feasibility work for a Queens aviation-focused STEAM center.
chapters.title: Dep. Speaker Williams on STEAM center expansion and Queens aviation

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Questions related to things in my district.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So New York City Public Schools has consistently

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Talked about preparing students for jobs of the future.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: You know, yet access to high quality programs remain uneven across schools and communities.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So I just want to understand what investments is New York City Public Schools making to expand dedicated STEAM centers citywide?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: How many currently exist?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Where are they located?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Do you have any timelines?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And just making it a loaded question, and I would love to get your answer on this, and then I'm done.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So students like mine in Southeast Queens were surrounded by a major economic engine, which is the airport, yet many students lack direct access to specialized STEAM facilities that connect learning, careers in aviation, engineering, cybersecurity,

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: AI, advanced tech.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So also just wanting to understand your plan to ensure geographic distribution and whatever other resources could be included in this budget to align with the many goals that New York City Public School has.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Yeah, so that was a lot.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So I should focus on the theme.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Yeah, essentially the theme centers.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: How many do you have?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Where are they located?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And what is your plan?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Oh, Jane's coming.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: The perfect person to answer the question on the record about New York City Public Schools theme centers.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Love that.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So, so, um...

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So in thinking about our STEAM centers, we have the Brooklyn STEAM Center.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We have...

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: have the wrong steam center that we're working on and as well and and i know we have uh plans and are in in conversations about the queens now when it comes to aviation and you know i do have to shout out

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: uh aviation high school that i visited in queens it was um it's in long island city and it's like an hour and a half to get there we do have august martin okay they have an aviation program so shout out to august martin but it is not as a person who worked really closely with August Martin.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: The kids were not in the cockpit for a decade, and I was able to help get outside funding to get the kids in the cockpit.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So I understand we do have August Martin, but that program is not specialized in the ways in which the STEAM Center is, and it is not connected to industry in the ways in which the STEAM Centers are.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Totally.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And so we're...

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Beginning to and having real conversation about the sustainability of the STEAM centers that are in place right now.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We are, you know, each year we dedicate funding specifically to those two that are currently in existence and have and are expanding their programs, planning to expand their programs into next year.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And so I'm going to turn it over to Jane, who said to talk a little bit more about what we're doing regarding STEAM centers.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Deputy Chair Dr.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Williams.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So with our steam sensors, you correctly say they are an incredible innovation.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We have our innovation at the Brooklyn Navy Yard, which is a

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: now not as new as the other ones.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And then this year, we have really focused on ensuring that our Bronx STEAM Center, which is focusing on our partnership with Montifior around healthcare access, is beginning to grow.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: As you know, you've been an incredible part of the feasibility work that's happening in Queens to ensure that we have that same kind of opportunities in the aviation industry for our Queens folks.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: It is an incredible investment that we think is very important to very carefully look through.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So we are working all year along with the aviation folks and with

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: several potential anchor partners to ensure that we can actually have this not just be an innovation but a real sustainable model that we can replicate in Queens.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And yes, I would love for it to be included in the budget.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So it truly does look like a sustainability model.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I mean, yes, there's one in Brooklyn, there's one coming to the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Hopefully we have one in Queens, but there are two other boroughs missing from that.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So I'd love to see it reflected in how you will do your budgets, because as far as I'm concerned, I think you only have the $15 million that I negotiated.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So I don't necessarily see a legitimate commitment from New York City public schools because you haven't committed any funds outside of, for Queens, outside of the ones that I negotiated from the previous administration.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So I'd love to see that reflected.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: In your budget.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks how NYC Public Schools coordinates with ACS, DOHMH, and other agencies for 2K students with disabilities. Deputy Chancellor Hawkins describes the intentional accommodations process and referral partnerships with early intervention and school health.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on 2K special education coordination with agencies

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We have Councilmember Joseph, followed by Wong.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Good to see you, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: DOE continue to struggle to provide students with disabilities with necessary services.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We're thrilled the DOE is expanding and to provide 2K.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Meeting needs earlier should reduce the number of due process case at a later stage in a child's education.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: As we begin to educate younger and younger New York, how is New York City Public Schools coordinating with ACS, DOHMH, and other agencies to ensure child's needs are met daily as practical?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, that is something that's very, very important to me, which is why when I

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: thought about a restructuring, really thinking about putting foster care, open arms, all of those pieces in the same place.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Environment and making sure that we have very seamless communication between the Office of the Deputy Chancellor for Community Partnerships and the and dial and so I'm going to turn it over to

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Christina Foti as well as Flavia Padema to talk a little bit more about that.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: To talk about 2K coming into the system, how they're getting services.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Remember, they come before early intervention.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We're going to talk about you in a minute.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Get your stuff to you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Together, but I'm going to talk to DC40 first.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sorry, Simone Hawkins and DC40.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Simone Hawkins and DC40.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: Thank you, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: And so what you raise is a really important point, and especially as we shift down the entry point into our school system.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: And so we've been very deliberate in partnering, and we already partner with our DOHMH colleagues, specifically the Bureau of Early Intervention.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: And so what we've been doing specifically on 2K is creating a very intentional accommodations process.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: We have some universal supports that we do as a through line, right?

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: And that's what we should be doing, not just for two-year-olds, but all of our littles, no matter how old they are.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: And so that really is around, like, the appropriate coaching, making sure they differentiate.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: They, as the educators and school leaders, differentiate based on the needs of our littles.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: But then we also know we need some more targeted interventions.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: So we have a referral, and we are creating a more thoughtful referral process with our EI colleagues and also our office, the Office of School Health at DOHMH.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: Because what we understand is early intervention meets a very specific purpose, but some of our littles might need something different.

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: And so we're partnering with the Office of School Health, Dial, they're working side by side, that's my girl,

utterances.speaker: Simone Hawkins
utterances.text: and her team, working with our team to make sure we're coordinated, that there's proper redundancy but not unnecessary duplication, and that our littles and their families are having a seamless experience through and through.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about the 63% chronic absenteeism rate for students in temporary housing and how DOE is working with DSS and ACS. Deputy Chancellor Puello-Perdomo describes 32 attendance teachers conducting 4,327 home visits, $125 million in Title I funding for shelters, and a 9% reduction in chronic absenteeism for shelter students.
chapters.title: Chair Joseph on students in temporary housing attendance

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And if...

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The service is not being provided.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: You're stepping in and providing, filling in that gap as well.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Right, Deputy Chancellor?

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: Yeah, we're going to, as DC Hawkins said, we're going to work side by side to make sure that students have what they need to access the program.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And real quick, thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Our students in temporary housing, attendance, absenteeism rate, the last time we spoke here, the absenteeism rate is 63%.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Always want to know how are you working with different agencies to combine that effort, DSS, ACS.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How are you combining that effort to make sure that we're bringing down that ATIA absenteeism rate?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And also academics, right?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Not to only buy students in temporary housing items, but are we providing support, academic support, tutoring, mental health support?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How are we supporting our students in temporary housing?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And as part of our conversations earlier, we really have spent in the last five months

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: concentrated effort to meet with our families and shelters and you know Flavia has led that effort so I'm going to lead her to talk I'm going to ask her to talk more about that.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: Thank you Chancellor thank you Council Member Joseph I want to just first begin I know that you're well versed on the definition but just so that we all understand that when we say chronically absent

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: it means that a student at any given point has mean 10% of the school.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: So 89% attendance, you're chronically absent.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: We know that for vulnerable student population, there's always disproportionate

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: rates of chronic absenteeism.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: And I want to call that out because counting today, we have 14 days of the school year remaining for all students and about five days for our high school students who take regents.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: So if you're out 18 days of the year, you're chronically absent.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: And it's not just an arbitrary number, it's a national standard because at that point, we begin to see correlations between academic achievement and attendance.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: And that is why that number is so critical for all of us.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
