utterances.text: And with that, I want to say that the support that we're doing both for attendance and for students who, overall, and for students who are in temporary housing, Barry, from looking at central support.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: So, for example, this year we recruited 32 attendance teachers who are part of our total attendance pool to provide dedicated home visits

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: during holidays, during weekends, and after work hours to try to make it more easily accessible to reach those families for students who have been out.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: I can say that out of, in total, 4,327 visits were done for these students, particularly targeting students in shelters.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: And an immediate result that we saw following those home visits is that those students then came into school.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: Similarly, which I think is when we look at like what are the

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: What are the ingredients and what are the things that the central team led by Dr.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: April Leon and the many leaders who are there have been doing is working with our schools

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: to ensure that the Title I funding that schools are receiving in their budget are really used in fidelity to look at, like, how do we mitigate the challenges for these students.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: In 2022, there were approximately $72 million that were provided to schools across the system to support that.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: 2025-26, this current school year, that's $125 million.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: And that's money that's sitting in each of our school budgets across the city, specifically dedicated to support the needs of students in shelters.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: So one of the things that we're working to is not just that the money is spent, but that it's spent with fidelity and it's spent on things that are not commodities, but things like counseling support services, things like attendance interventions,

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: looking at college and career readiness programs.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: Are there tutoring, like, for example, one of the work that we're doing with wind shelters where we have weekends programs that we do in those sites to meet the needs of those students.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: So I think the idea is like looking at the whole infrastructure that exists for attendance, like the work that we do to deploy attendance teachers to superintendent, the work that we have done that has allowed us, in fact, to reduce chronic absenteeism by 9%

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: for students in shelters as an example.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: But there's still so much more that we need to do.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: For example, one of the work that we're doing with wind shelters where we have weekends programs that we do in those sites to meet the needs of those students.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: That's also another example why initiative like community schools, in our second wave of community schools that we're expanding now about four years ago,

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: So I think the idea is like looking at the whole infrastructure that exists for attendance, like the work that we do to deploy attendance teachers to superintendent, the work that we have done that has allowed us, in fact, to reduce chronic absenteeism by 9%

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: one of the indicators that we were looking at were students, schools that had high concentration of students in shelters, because we know that wraparound supports

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: the notion of a caring adult, the notion that you're going to have schools that are going to be looking at data on a daily basis and making calls.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: for students in shelters as an example.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: We cannot wait for a 407, which is the indicator that you get when a kid is 10 days out.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: This is like everyday strategy, making sure that you're making calls, making sure that you're making connections, making sure that you understand the reasons why students are out

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: and that you are creating individualized plan, particularly for students that we know that are vulnerable.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: In addition to that, we're also working with the Department of Homeless Services to make sure that we

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: come up with strategies that allow us to make local executive order, that we get ahead of when this shelter movement that are happening so that we can plan thoughtfully and make sure that we're helping families navigate any changes that affect their home living situation

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: that has an implication for schools.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: Because at the end of the day, we know that when we look at these students and their families that have been impacted by homelessness throughout years,

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: School stability and access to high-quality education is what's going to make a difference so that they don't end up in another situation.

utterances.speaker: Flavia Puello-Perdomo
utterances.text: like that and have a different future for the generations to come.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong, followed by Lewis.

chapters.summary: Council Member Wong asks about a proposed lithium battery energy storage facility directly across from PSIS 128 in Middle Village and whether DOE has protocols to notify school communities. Deputy Chancellor Moran says schools do not get involved in land acquisition but acknowledges safety concerns.
chapters.title: CM Wong on lithium battery storage near schools

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Chancellor, thank you for coming today.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I want to talk about a situation in my district.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: A nine-dot energy company proposed a lithium battery energy storage system.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: To be built at 6430 69th Place in Middle Village.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That's directly across from the school, PSIS 128, is right next to the annex of the building,

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: and facing the lot to the left is the annex of the school, 128, to the right is a daycare center, a preschool, and a children's play area.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The project is as of right, but has not been built yet.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But we know, I watch YouTube, and I'm sure everyone watches YouTube, lithium-ion battery fires are extremely difficult to suppress.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: At the SBS fire in Warwick, New York, the fire burned for more than three days in December 2025.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Here's my question.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Is a policy question.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: When a developer proposes a lithium ion battery storage facility directly across the street from a school, before it's built, while there's still time to act, does DOE have any written protocol?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: requiring school leadership or the parent coordinator or the PTA to notify families that this proposal exists because this is happening right now and there's a lot of parents, even teachers, said they know nothing about it.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Please answer it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think I'm going to turn it over to Kevin Moran to see if there are any policies there, but that's something we can look into as well.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Yeah, first I'd like to thank you, Councilmember Wong, for your passion, advocacy, and support on this issue.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Keeping our school community safe is foundational.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I understand your concerns.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We watched carefully the fire in Warwick, New York, by the bus depot and by their school, and it did burn for three days.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Our schools do not get involved in land acquisition engagement activities in and around the school for a lot of good reasons.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: As a lawmaker, again, I thank you for your advocacy.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I'd also reinforce that there's no law that would prevent battery storages from sensitive locations like libraries, schools.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: churches, gas stations.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And so at current, battery storage facilities, battery farms as they're being called, can be built within 10 feet of an adjacent property.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And so to the point that you make, it's lawfully and legally permitted.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And the regulatory authority here is the Department of Buildings.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: They'll go ahead with the paperwork.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: There's advancements there on fire suppression, surveillance.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: close scrutiny from the fire department.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And so to the extent we could be helpful in this, we will be.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: But no, there's not a role for a PTA or a principal or anyone in the school community to play as it relates to land acquisition in and around the school.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, well, thank you for the answer.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because right now that...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: We still have teachers and parents coming to me and say they're unaware of this project, and there's a big gap where the whole community is unaware of this proposal, and that's across the street from where the children go to school every day.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That's a problem, and then I'll have to talk to legislators in Albany to get this patched up because this clearly is not safe, it's not the right place.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Well, I would say that it's an emerging industry in the city.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: It's meant to relieve the strain placed on the grid right now.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: In my neighborhood, I got it right across from my library, right next to a gas station.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I see them growing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so to the extent we can partner on ways to make sure this is done safely for our community, it would be appreciated.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Jim.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Jim.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Lewis, followed by Brewer.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lewis asks about District 75 students not receiving IEP services and family welcome center staffing. Deputy Chancellor Foti reports 468 providers hired this year, 92% compliance, and 15,000 students served in SEED weekend academies. The Chancellor promises follow-up on welcome center data.
chapters.title: CM Lewis on District 75 services and family welcome centers

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And good afternoon, Chancellor, to you and your whole team.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You guys look like the Avengers.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you for all being here.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Two quick questions.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Families of students receiving District 75 services continue to report that some elementary school students are not consistently receiving specialized supports and related services outlined in their IEP.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: programs.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The executive budget includes significant investments in special education services, including additional funding for IESP, support special education, pre-K expansion, and court-ordered special education compliance.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Despite these investments, many families still report service gaps.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: How will the DOE measure whether these new investments are actually resulting in improved service delivery, and what percentage of students currently receive all mandated services without interruption?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I'm going to quickly just go into my second question because I don't have a lot of time.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Families have expressed frustration with limited assistance provided by the family welcome centers.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: You probably hear this all the time.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The executive budget includes additional funding for family empowerment initiatives, and yet families continue to report challenges navigating the admissions process.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: What is the current staffing level and budget allocation for family welcome centers, and how have response times and case resolution metrics changed over time, and are there plans to increase staffing in the new budget?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Great question, and I'm going to start with the first one first.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: When it comes to services, we've done, as a city, a much better job over the last number of years.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: However, when one child does not get the services they need, to us, that's 100%.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so I'm going to...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Turn it over to Christina Foti, who's going to talk more specifically about services, both to students with disabilities in District 75 and throughout as well.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Lewis.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: It's so nice to see you.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Regarding District 75, there are a few things that we're doing.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: So the first is, to date, across the board, I've gotten to that point where I have to do this, is 468 providers have been hired this school year.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: We've also improved our contracting rates where we're working to provide incentives to high-need areas.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Additionally, we expanded our ninth session work, which means that our OTs and PTs can be paid to conduct a ninth session of therapy, which is also helping fill that gap.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: To date, we are...

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: At almost at 92% compliance, but as the Chancellor said, that is not 100%, so we will keep going until then.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: You also know about our seed and weekend academies.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: As of this past Saturday, our seed sites served 15,000 students for related services, which is pretty remarkable in sensory gyms.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: And so we're doing a lot, and the numbers keep growing.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: The mandates also keep growing, and that's why the investments are so important.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: If I get a response on the family welcome centers, thanks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I don't have the data with me right now, but I'm going to follow up with you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If I have it before the end of today, I'll share that with you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer, followed by Nurse East.

chapters.summary: Council Member Brewer asks about menstrual product availability (56% of schools report products almost never available), playground opening requirements, arts education (16% of schools lack certified arts teachers), affinity school funding, and class size progress data.
chapters.title: CM Brewer on menstrual products, playgrounds, arts, and class size

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I have a question about menstrual products, class size, playgrounds, art education, and affinity schools.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So starting with menstrual products.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So we did, we have a lot of interns.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They talked to a lot of schools.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: 56% of schools said that those period products are almost never available.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: 80% said they don't know if a student doesn't know how to report an empty dispenser.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my question is, for the young women in the school system, how much is included in the budget for menstrual products, and how are you dealing with some of these issues?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, so for those, we're going to have to take a look and get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I don't know if, Kevin, you have anything you want to share there.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I regret hearing that.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We do survey our schools and work with our principals and custodians on a daily basis.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: I will absolutely follow up with a- It's not working.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Well, we're gonna follow up on a message to custodians, make sure that they are fully stocked in all locations.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Apologies.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Playgrounds, so I passed a bill that said a certain percentage of playgrounds have to be open within the next 10 years.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Obviously, custodians have to be paid.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What we're doing about this program, opening up playgrounds weekends.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And during the summer, who's in charge of that?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Well, specifically, we are supportive when funded of opening up our parks and having our custodians there to make sure it's open and available.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: If there's any specific cases that are not open in your district.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: No, it's not my district.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's the law.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I passed the law.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And within a certain time period, the COE must open more playgrounds by law.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We're committed to that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, so you're going to make that happen.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: An arts education, can you give me a sense of does every single school have either an art teacher and then how many have arts programs and are pre-K and even zero to two going to have art programs?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've done really good work around our arts programming over the last two years, including at the principal level, having principals.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Who are now involved in pilots for arts programming.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I'm going to turn it over to First Deputy Chancellor Junza to add some more.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we have a great number of schools that are mixed between licensed art, certified art teachers, and then...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have in our elementary schools, you don't necessarily have to be a certified art teacher.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then we have lots of our schools that are partnering with cultural organizations to provide the arts.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Can you get back to me with a number of certified art teachers?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How many schools have cultural organizations and how you evaluate them?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Can you get back?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: For the first one with the certified, we have about 16% of the schools that currently lack a certified arts teacher.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: include 168 elementary schools, 23 middle schools, 37 high schools, and 25 multi-grade schools.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So conversely, they do, the vast majority do.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, but we still need 16%.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, we certainly do.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then in terms of affinity schools, I think we talked about this a little bit before, but what's the funding situation with them?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So for affinity schools overall, we haven't had any issues with the funding mechanism for affinity schools.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Specifically, we've been talking about the new visions portal, but overall for affinity schools, their funding is in place.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, because they feel differently.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then just, I love your comic books.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I hate comics, but I like your books.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And congratulations on Hold Harmless.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And just in terms of class size, I understand two years we'll have more time to do, but do you have some sense of how many class sizes have already been reduced?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because that's phenomenal.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And how many have not?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, so one of the things about the class size law is that that is interesting is that it's not about reducing class size necessarily.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's about getting to the actual compliance numbers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So, for example, you could have a kindergarten class that started off at 25 students and now is at 22.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's not at 20, so it doesn't get counted as compliant, but it's been reduced.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So, you know, I'm going to turn it over to Aaron to talk a little bit more about the progress that we've made.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But just to say, you have to hit the mark to get credit.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You can't just reduce.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think you all know this, but boy, when somebody gets to 20, which is ecstatic, just like I have hit gold.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And you can tell the private suits go to hell.

utterances.speaker: Erin Jahant
utterances.text: Well, I can share the data that we have on progress last year.

utterances.speaker: Erin Jahant
utterances.text: So grades K to 3 saw a 28.7% increase.

utterances.speaker: Erin Jahant
utterances.text: Grades 4 to 5 saw a 23% increase.

utterances.speaker: Erin Jahant
utterances.text: Middle school grew 20%.

utterances.speaker: Erin Jahant
utterances.text: High school grew 16%.

utterances.speaker: Erin Jahant
utterances.text: And to the Chancellor's point, and to your point about overall progress towards class size reduction, about a third of the noncompliant classes are only one to three students over the new caps.

utterances.speaker: Erin Jahant
utterances.text: And we've reduced the number of classes that were six students or more above the cap.

utterances.speaker: Erin Jahant
utterances.text: That rate declined 12 points.

utterances.speaker: Erin Jahant
utterances.text: So 29% of classes in FY25 had six or more above the caps, and now it's down to 17% in FY26.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, and it won't slip in the next couple of years, right?

utterances.speaker: Erin Jahant
utterances.text: I mean, what we're doing now is we're working to look at funding for schools and make sure that teachers are available in line with the new caps to make sure that class sizes are maintained.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just think you should keep going because I'm going to go.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: It makes a huge difference.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Narcisse asks about school nurse baselining, preschool special education seats in Brooklyn, community school closures, and after-school program contract disruptions. Officials confirm nurses are shared, 146 community schools remain in Brooklyn, and DYCD added 20,000 after-school seats.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on school nurses, preschool special ed, and after-school contracts

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you, chairs, and thank you, Commissioner and Chancellor and all your team.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And I have to say thank you to Kevin Miranda for being very nice and supporting me in the District 46.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: School nurse funding is baseline in this budget.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Does that...

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Put a full-time nurse in every school building citywide this coming year?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Or are schools still sharing one nurse across multiple buildings?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And how much of that health funding rise on federal Medicaid reimbursement that is now at risk?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We all know that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The second one, the budget adds new funding for preschool special education, a chronic shortage area, right?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How many new seats land in Brooklyn where parents are still waiting months for placement?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How many community schools operate in Brooklyn and are any of them at risk of closing or scaling back services this year?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And I have another quick things I have to ask you, even though it's DYCD, but I want to bring that to your attention, is after school program.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The shifting of those IFBs, people apply for them, and the family, you add a baby.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: big rally in my district where the community organization, the org that have been taking care of the schools, suddenly find themselves across New York City

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: for the contract similar while they have somebody new or some other organization, some

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: elsewhere, not even in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So I want to know what can parents do because they've been sending me email.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We were on the street rallying.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So I want to have your input on that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, you know, on the issue of our school, I know a lot of the work is done by the UICD.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But so going back to the beginning of your question about we've done a lot of work to ensure that our schools have nurses.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I'm going to turn it over to Kevin Mann to add anything there.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And after that, we'll follow up with pre-K special education to Christina Foti.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then finally to Flavia to close out the DYCD conversation on after school.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Okay, real quick on your question.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We meet all legal mandates as it relates to staffing nurses in all of our school buildings.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: So, yes, wherever there's a legal mandate to support a student or school community, there's a nurse that's provided.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I just want to know that we're not sharing nurses.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Are we still sharing nurses?

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: They are shared.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I'd rather they have their nurses and really.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Understood.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: But the budget on the preschool.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, and that's something that we are really committed to, given the history of not necessarily having those services in pre-K.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so I'm going to turn it over to Christina for you to talk about it.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Narcisse.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: You're having me open up Councilmember Joseph's tab of my binder, because usually this is her question.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: She was there.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: So in terms of the $67.5 million, we're so excited and grateful for that.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: I am going to ask you to bear with me on details on that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: In Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Yes, ma'am.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The council members in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Here's what I can say.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Brooklyn will be benefiting as well, and certainly we're going to be opening seats with that.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: I can say in the last round of funding, we had 75 seats open in Brooklyn, including the PS6 Flatbush Early Childhood Center, which is a very exciting addition, and more to come on that front.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So for the community school,

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: They're not going to close.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You're not planning to.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Councilman Berners.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think your question was asking how many community schools are currently in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The answer is 146, and those 146 are remaining.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The only instance in recent where there has been any change, if there's like any school consolidating, but the...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Since we refueled the schools that were previously funded through stimulus funding, we had retained

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: the number of community schools which we had citywide, which is a total of 419, still the largest community school investment of any district in the country, and hopefully we can continue to...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To look for ways to amplify it because we know that those wraparound support services are so critical and we have specific data that shows the linkage between the long-term impact of community schools and

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: all of the metrics that we're talking about including student achievement and reduction in chronic absenteeism but to your answer there's 146 and there will be 146 next school year

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: because i have um principals coming to my office sending email they're very much interested they feel like they should be part of oh they want to become community schools that's a little different now now i'm saying like we're going to talk about it more about community school in my

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: district

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And the last one, the...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The YCD, yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So just to be clear, there are contract nuances about how the YCD does their...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: procurement for their contracts that really, I know DYCD recently had a hearing, we don't manage their contracts, and I can't speak to all of the details of their contracts.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What I can say is that since 2015, we hadn't done, citywide, a procurement for after school.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So many of our principals that we have now, which I think, you know, in our system, have never had to go through this experience.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What we did do in partnership with DYCD was that as part of their process, we made sure that there was a principal ranking.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And a large proportion of the principals did get the CBO that they either ranked first or second.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There were multiple schools that didn't get it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I will say that DYCD leadership, including the commission and directly, has been meeting with some of the providers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We had held meetings with multiple CEC, our community parent councils, to make sure that they're clear.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The one thing that I want us all to walk away with, like, yes, as a former principal, if I had, like, I had a partnership in the South Bronx with one of the CBOs, and if I have to, made a change and I felt like I had.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Another CVO that I felt was more aligned to that, that's going to be a big shift and it's something that we're going to have to work with our principals and work in partnership with DYCD to make sure that

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're kind of resetting those relationships and are doing our best to make sure that the services, what kids are getting, continues.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But what I want to be clear is that we haven't had any reduction on seats.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The opposite.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: DYCD added 20,000 seats for after school that hasn't happened.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So if you have a particular partner or challenge, let me email me and we can work with you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Yeah, my time is up and my colleagues are waiting.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: But it's not only me.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I have Selvena Brooks-Bowery that had to go.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: It's the same issue.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I had principal out.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I have teachers out.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I have parents out.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: It's a continuity of care and the services at the same time.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Please email us and we can work with DYCD to work on those.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I appreciate your time.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I appreciate you as well.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And that was actually a large focus of our DYCD hearing, so it was good.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And we have definitely expressed our concerns.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And the commissioner is great.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: She's been working with us on that.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay, we've been joined by Councilmember Krishnan, and we have Councilmember Epstein, followed by Ressler.

chapters.summary: Council Member Epstein raises racial and economic segregation in after-school access with only 184,000 slots for 250,000 Title I students, literacy intervention needs, and District 75 internship denials. Deputy Chancellor Gentes describes MTSS and NYC Reads expansion, and Foti commits to investigating internship denials.
chapters.title: CM Epstein on after-school access, literacy, and District 75 internships

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you all, thank you for your time.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I wanna talk about after school.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, there are about 250,000 students who get Title I funding, only about 184,000 after school slots.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So what we're seeing, especially down by me, is racial segregation and economic segregation because we don't have free after school.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Jessler, how are we gonna solve this and can we solve this soon?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Look, I think we see this issue around the city, and it's certainly an issue where, you know, it's also an issue of the type of

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: after-school provider and what actually we're getting in terms of quality.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I think, you know, schools also work with providers to make sure that they have the ones that don't, you know, are not funded by Title I, that they have scholarships for young people and all this.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But it's a very difficult thing for a school by itself to ensure the equity in after-school programs.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: But don't we have a responsibility to make sure that our schools are racially and economically integrated?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And after-school is a critical component to it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm proud of that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And, you know, I've talked to DYCD about this.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Can we get a commitment from you that we can get a plan to ensure that, especially for our Title I families, they'll have access to free after-school in some period of time?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Because that's what we really need.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, so, you know, that's part, I think this is certainly a partnership between us and DYCD.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I know we have some expansion plans and so on, but I'm going to turn it over to Flavia to talk a little bit more.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Council Member Epstein.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I do want to flag that there was a commission assembled towards the end of the prior administration that was really a cross-sector commission on this notion of

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Universal after school.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we know that the vast majority of funding for after school for the whole city comes through DYCD.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Then the rest of it is funded through community schools.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: A lot of the community schools that I mentioned have the capacity to do that work.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We also have LEAPS, which is a state-funded grant that goes directly to CVOs that can do that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As a parent who benefits from after school myself, because I have a child that attends New York City Public School, I know that this is something that all the communities, especially in areas of need, welcome.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And this is something that I welcome the opportunity to partner with you and all of the agencies and CVOs that were part of this discussion so that we could actually look at that report that was published less than a year ago,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: some of the first steps to get into that vision about university after school, the funding that it will take and the collaboration between the agencies, the OHMH who offers the license because it's a multi-layered step, but it's the right

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: effort to pursue and push.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah, I'm happy to work with Chair Dinowitz, Chair Stevens, and to kind of move this forward.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: This is something we see as a racial justice issue and economic justice issue.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Chair, if I can just get another minute.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I know at the preliminary budget hearings we talked a lot about

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: literacy issues and making sure that young people, you know, if they get to third grade and they still aren't reading and writing, they're not getting support.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And we see this school to prison pipeline and talked a lot about.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: out trying to get to people earlier in their education.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Mentioned Literacy Academy, which does a lot of great work, and the two literacy dyslexic schools that were started in the prior administration.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I haven't seen anything in this budget that would advance the issues of trying to ensure that our young people are getting the support that they need.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And I want to just stress that, you know, we really need that because, honestly, if a kid by third grade isn't learning to read, the chance of them, you know, unfortunately failing later in life just in time goes up exponentially.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We'd love to see some commitments here.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Happy to be as supportive as I possibly can to make sure that happens and ensure those young people get the support that they need.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we are completely committed to ensuring that all of our young people are able to read.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And if they're not able to do so by third grade, that we have systems in place that we are developing and have developed to get them on track.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The main thing that we are doing is our multi-tiered system of support, our MTSS.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm going to turn it over to our first deputy chancellor to speak in more detail about that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But this is an opportunity for every school across the city to be able to implement this well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you for your question and passionate literacy fan.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Building off of Chancellor's comments, you know, our commitment to expanding New York City reads is one such step.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've expanded our reach now into middle schools.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so New York City Reads lives across all of our elementary schools and most of our middle schools at this point.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're layering that in with multi-tiered systems of support, which he just said, which is the using of screeners to really catch where kids are missing

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: key concepts in tier one and being able to really pinpoint what it is that's getting in their way and aligns high-quality intervention to help catch them up.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But it doesn't just live just there.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's also a close partnership with Deputy Chancellor Foti and the specialized programs that she's seeking to grow and the work of universal child care, because we all know and agree, like, the sooner we get our kids in a literacy-rich environment

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: in a thriving way and then in supports when they need them is the critical difference.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As it relates to the literacy academies that you spoke of, I have recently been able to meet with the team and learned so much more about their work.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And yes, we are in very early conversations around expanding those schools, and I know they're very interested in bringing one to Queens.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I look forward to whatever help I can provide on that because those six-week intensive programs really work.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And the last thing I'll just flag is love that we're doing more internships.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I have a District 75 school there.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Kids are thriving for internships.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: They were denied this year to get those internships.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Is there anything we can do to create more opportunities?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I have schools where the kids are in the country less than two years, and those young people want internships.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: They're able to get some resources.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: As much as we can do, because we're going to keep those kids in school if we get those internships and money and opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Otherwise, we see high dropout rates in those schools.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Yeah, absolutely, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: If you could send me the information on those, I'm very curious to know about why that was the case.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: If it was perhaps travel that was in the way, I do want to say that we are deeply interested in expanding our travel training programs.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: That is often a barrier of why kids have a District 75 struggle to get their work-based learning opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: And we've seen incredible results via our travel training.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: So I will definitely dig in and, yes, looking to expand this opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We would love to follow up, and I'll have my team reach out to you directly.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Wrestler followed by Aviles.

chapters.summary: Council Member Restler asks about Spanish language teacher shortages, hold harmless confirmation for initial budgets, 3K seat additions in Greenpoint, and the overdue school librarian report. The Chancellor confirms hold harmless for initial budgets and the librarian report was submitted today.
chapters.title: CM Restler on Spanish teachers, hold harmless, 3K seats, and librarians

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs, Chancellor and team.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Good to see you all.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I know you're all not going to comment on that oddly timed New York Post article, but briefly, could you just elaborate?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We've experienced an extreme shortage in Spanish language teachers over the last few years.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Especially with the migrant crisis, it was exacerbated.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Could you just briefly elaborate in some detail on the breadth of, or the breadth

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The shortage that we've been facing across the schools in New York City?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, we've had significant shortage in Spanish language and

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: L teachers in general prior to the arrival of New Yorkers and certainly exasperated by that and also continues to be a shortage area that we experience.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I know we have...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Our Amy Way here who can speak more to that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In 15 seconds.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm not kidding.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Yeah, 15 seconds.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, sorry.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's so big.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I know, but two sentences.

utterances.speaker: Amy Way
utterances.text: First, I'll swear to tell the truth, only the truth.

utterances.speaker: Amy Way
utterances.text: Amy Way, Executive Director for Teacher Recruitment and Quality.

utterances.speaker: Amy Way
utterances.text: World languages is an extreme shortage area.

utterances.speaker: Amy Way
utterances.text: What we are doing is on two fronts.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I wasn't asking that.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I was just asking for the data on what the shortage was over the last two years.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But if you want to offer that.

utterances.speaker: Amy Way
utterances.text: I can follow up with you on that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, we can send it to you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Next up, hold harmless funding.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Just to reiterate your testimony, no impacts on hold harmless funding in the budgets that will be released for every school early next week?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In the initial budget, that's correct.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But mid-year adjustments, TBD?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And any insight that you can offer to your approach on hold harmless funding, a gradual step down or anything to that effect if we do see mid-year adjustments?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Right now, we have held initial...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: budget's harmless and we're going to continue to have conversations about the sustainability of schools and you know but no decisions on the mid-year.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I appreciate the the policy I think that you know welcome a conversation on how we navigate this over time.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I appreciate budgets being released by early next week because I think the council is sometimes put in an impossible situation where we don't know the budgets of our individual schools and are expected to adopt the budget for the city of New York as a whole.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And there's no budget that's more important to me than the 40-odd schools that are in my district.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So we want to make sure that we're doing everything we can to protect them and filling gaps for them.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But we can only do that once we know the budget.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So thank you for getting that out by early next week.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: 3K pre-K.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Nice to see you, Deputy Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Appreciate all your work.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I am very grateful for the significant expansion of 3K and pre-K that we've seen across our district.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We have been told that additional seats are being added in Greenpoint.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can you quantify beyond the PS110 swap of a classroom there how many additional seats are being added in the 11222 zip code?

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: I'll get that to you, Councilman.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Because we've heard the number 42, and we'd love to confirm that if and when it's possible to do so.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I also just want to flag at PS31, also in Greenpoint, we've had...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Many families.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We've had families zoned for kindergarten that can't be accommodated into the school, which we're concerned about.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we've had families that have

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: applied for pre-K that have been assigned to locations as far as 11 miles away.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And so we can send all, if we haven't already, we'll send you all the details on those families, but we really hope to be able to work together to add a pre-K classroom and a kindergarten classroom at PS31 to try to make sure that local families are accommodated.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And since I've gone over time, my last question is,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Last year we passed a law with then Chair Joseph's help to have the DOE report on the presence of school librarians in our schools.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It showed that only in 47 out of 657 elementary schools did we have a certified librarian, which gosh, that is just far too many.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm a big believer in librarians and the impact they have on literacy outcomes.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The first annual report was due on June 1st.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do we have any status update on that report now that we're a week late?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I believe it was submitted today.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Submitted today.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Great news.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I look forward to reading it.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Chancellor and team.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Appreciate your partnership and look forward to working together.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Awesome.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Avila is followed by Zhuang.

chapters.summary: Council Member Aviles asks about declining restorative justice cohorts (from 30 to 8), guidance counselor ratios (1:259 overall, 1:160 combined with social workers), and charter school rental assistance ($340 million) with DOE having no negotiating power over leases.
chapters.title: CM Aviles on restorative justice, guidance counselors, and charter funding

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Hello, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you so much for being here and all the work you do.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I wanted to ask a couple of questions.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I'm sorry, it's like slightly in speed because we've all got to go.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: About restorative justice.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Our understanding is that this year DOE is funding eight tier one cohorts, which is down from 13 last year, down from 32 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: This seems to be in a very wrong direction.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: So can you talk to us about what is happening with restorative justice work?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And then I'd love to hear a little bit more about in 2025, the comptroller put out a report that noted 53% of the New York City schools did not meet the recommended number of guidance counselors in the buildings.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: In terms of guidance counselor ratios, have we made any progress there in terms of making sure there's enough guidance counselors?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: our students.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: Mark.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: Oh, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: I will start.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: I will get back to you with those cohort numbers in particular.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: Can you just repeat the question?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: My understanding is that there is eight this year, down from 13 last year, down from 30 two years ago.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Very wrong direction for restorative justice.

utterances.speaker: Mark Rampersant
utterances.text: I will try to have that for you before you leave today.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: In terms of guidance counselors.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: I can.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Hi, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: How are you?

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: I can speak to the guidance counselors.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: So in total, New York City Public School, as per a public report that was shared in February, we have a total of 3,296 guidance counselors in the school system.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: 3,256 of them are full-time, 41 are part-time.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: In terms of the ratio specifically, and with that I want to be clear that as we know, a few

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: years ago the state changed the requirement for guidance counselors at the elementary school level, making them just this notion of access, but not necessarily with the requirement of a full-time counselor.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: But the current ratio is 1 to 259 to all schools.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: And then when we look at historically what were the high school grades, which were the high school grades,

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: where a full-time person was mandated in the past is 1 to 182 schools with high school grades that have a school counselor.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Now, when you add the total of both, both when we look at school counselors and social workers, then those ratios change.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: The ratio then becomes 1 to 160 for all school when you look at the combined numbers, and 1 to 125 for schools with high school grades.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Better, but still not good.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: We need to do much better.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And I know we're working to get there.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: In terms of last question, in the budget we saw $3.7 billion to charter schools.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: How much of that is for rent?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Does DOE have any oversight in terms of those rental payments or any negotiating power in terms of rents and leases?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So for rent for charter schools is over $200 million of that total amount that you just quoted, and it's all governed by state law.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And what are the major categories?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: So $200 million is rental assistance, and then what is the breakdown of the other major categories?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Tuition.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Most tuition?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And rental payment is it?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The rental payments should be captured in the lease cost that I just provided to you, but the bulk of it is tuition for general ed and special ed students in charter schools.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And then there's no negotiating power that DOE engages in in terms of rental assistance or negotiating leases for schools and new buildings.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sorry, and just a correction, rental assistance is over $300 million, so thank you for that, Erin.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think there's very little there, again, because it is all governed by the state law, but I will defer to D.C.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Moran if there's a correction needed there.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: Yes, so exactly.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: On the budget side of the $3.5 billion, it includes $340 million in rental assistance.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: And in terms of rental assistance, we're just following the set policies by law to reimburse.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: So when a school is placed in an industrial building, in an industrial zone, on a track, on a truck route, DOE doesn't engage in any conversations that an educational institution should not be placed.

utterances.speaker: Kevin Moran
utterances.text: We're not the charter authorizer in many cases, and so they'll be going through the state or otherwise to get approval.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Krishnan raises SCA construction coordination issues at a school in his district and the $4 million immigrant family communication initiative not included in the FY27 executive plan. Deputy Chancellor Foti describes the Hello app, interpretation services, and 16 CBO partnerships that would be limited without restoration.
chapters.title: CM Krishnan on SCA coordination and immigrant family services funding

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Councilmember Zhuang, followed by Krishnan.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And first of all, not only Councilmember Mercedes Love, Deputy Chancellor Kevin, me too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you so much for putting the AC in Walmart School.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: This is a struggle for a long time, and also I realize SCA in general,

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: when our principal worked with SCA to make sure that no one used the auditorium service, and then

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: SCA told them, oh, we're going to do the work there in the summer, after the principal rerouted all the service in the school, and the SCCA told them, oh, we cannot do the construction in your school anymore.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: It really caused a lot of trouble to parents, to school.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I hope that part needs to be improved.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And also, I have another question about immigrant family services.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Over 40% of New York City public school families speak a language other than English at home.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: The DOE's Immigration Family Communication and Outreach Initiative is a $4 million program that helps immigrant families get important information about their child's education through

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: strategies such as sending paper notice, calling or text families, and collaborate with immigrants facing CBOs.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: The executive plan does not include this funding in fiscal 2027 or the out years.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Can you please talk about

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: this funding being used in this how this funding be used before what this type of will this type of service be cut and what the school should do

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: If they need the help.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, and I'm happy to jump in.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: So it is correct.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: We have received $4 million in support from council to be able to support our immigrant language efforts.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: That includes, when you say how it has been used, it has been used to develop the Hello app,

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: which allows for automatic language assistance designated to provide real-time interpretation in New York City cover languages to support impromptu translation when needed.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: We also have extended a pilot for five districts to be able to obtain free professional interpretation services for families.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: So, so far, 500 families have attended those events that we have used as funding.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: We also, as you mentioned, have partnered with 16 CBOs.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: So these are local CBOs in the community that have language expertise to make sure that those supports are expanded.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: We also have work on the training of our language access allocations coordinators.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: So this is relatively new.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Each school now has a designated language access coordinator, and our office works to bring them together to share resources.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: And to your last question, I'm looking forward to continue partnering with city council in this area because we do, without that, those funds.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: There will be limitation to provide all of those supports that I just mentioned and more.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I also have a follow-up question.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Has DOE had conversation with OMB about extending this funding?

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: I know that I'll let my colleague, the chief financial officer, share a little bit more on funding, but there has been multiple instances where we have highlighted some of the needs around language access and the needs for funding from my end.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I can confirm it's a part of our ongoing conversations with OMB.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Okay, I also want to share an incident that happened in my area in 2023.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Actually, it's an ongoing issue still in my area.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: There are multiple, not only just one high school, there are multiple high school principals reach out to me.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: They have been, they need assistance to communicate with some newer coming families.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Some language is not easy access in the...

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: With school and also because they are high school students they think they are adults

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: they don't let the school communicate with the parents they don't even they know both languages the kids don't want to

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: translate for the family and the schools so it is very hard for school to communicate with parents and I have an incident in 2023 one kid actually get a shot and a date

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: In my district.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I don't want this type of thing happen.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I want the school have frequent communication with especially the immigrant parents.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: They don't speak the language.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: They don't know how to navigate the system.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I'm an immigrant.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I know in different countries, the education is a different system.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Yeah, so am I, and I understand that very well.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: As an immigrant myself and somebody who started going in New York City public school as a high school student, I would also say that schools receive

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: some allocation, so they receive money in their budgets that they can also leverage to enhance the supports beyond the things that I mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: But I'm happy if you want to share with me the specific high schools.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: I'm happy to engage and happy to support with the team to make sure that our parents get access to that information that you're saying that is much needed beyond the required language.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: So anything that I can do, I understand that, not just from a policy and educational perspective.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: I understand it from lived experience.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: We'll definitely follow up with you.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Anything that you need, I'm here.

utterances.speaker: Christina Foti
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gutierrez asks about the class size working group recommendations and concerns about screen time in elementary schools. The Chancellor confirms DOE is considering working group recommendations and is surveying principals about ed-tech tools, with more details to come.
chapters.title: CM Gutierrez on class size working group and screen time in schools

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Councilmember Krishna, followed by Gutierrez.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Good to see you all, and Chancellor and the bilingual education team.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I want to thank you all for coming out with me to one of our programs at a school in Jackson Heights, and I appreciate the department's commitment to bilingual education.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I have a couple questions on a couple different topics, but the first one is I know we talked a lot about

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: about class size, the class size reduction plan and getting to there eventually.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I just had one question.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I know in December 2023, the class size working group had come out with some recommendations of what are ways to find and figure out smaller class size spaces through different ways.
