utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Has the DOE taken a look at that?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Are you guys considering those recommendations?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: How is that going?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We took a look at that both in

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: practice and consideration.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Most recently, as we said, we are looking at non-capital as well as capital investment.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Those non-capitals consider what's the utilization of our current buildings and where that is, and those are recommendations that came from the working group that you're talking about.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So yes, we have definitely looked closely at it, work shoulder to shoulder with SCA as well as partners across DOE to consider some of the factors there.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Erin, I don't know if you want to add anything there.

utterances.speaker: Erin
utterances.text: Yes, thank you, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Erin
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Erin
utterances.text: We are considering that, as the Chancellor said, our capital plan and approach to finding more space takes into account some things, including,

utterances.speaker: Erin
utterances.text: for example, like looking at shared space in buildings and these non-capital approaches that were identified by the class-sized working group in 2023.

utterances.speaker: Erin
utterances.text: And I think we're continuing to reflect on that.

utterances.speaker: Erin
utterances.text: what they shared then.

utterances.speaker: Erin
utterances.text: And again, as we release the class size reduction plan, I think we'll see that there's evidence of our consideration in the plan.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And then shifting gears completely to another topic that's more and more of concern on my mind is the presence and reliance of screens in schools, especially for elementary school students.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I know, Chancellor, you and I have had a conversation about that, and we're both committed to working together to address that issue.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: It's just a number, and I want to focus on elementary school kids in particular.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Obviously, there's discussions that have to be at middle school and high school, but between the Chromebooks,

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: programs like SAVAS or iReady tests and things like that, there's just a lot of, and it's a city issue, but it's also a national issue, of the amount of screen times, especially since the pandemic, that kids are spending in the classroom and at home on screens.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And I'm already negotiating with my kids over PlayStation time and TV time and everything.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And this is not one more thing I wanted to add to the negotiation list with them.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So, you know, there are a lot of contracts that DOE has with ed tech vendors, I guess I'd call them.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And I'm just curious how DOE is monitoring the funding that goes towards those contracts and the effectiveness of funding those contracts at those levels.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And how do you all keep an eye on those things?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, that is such a great question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And as someone who has young kids as well, something I'm super.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: concerned about in thinking about screen time and what that means for our school system.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We are having those discussions as we continue to hear feedback on our policies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: When we think about ed tech,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: resources and tech tools that are being used in schools.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We recently did a survey of all our principals to kind of get a sense of what's happening.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're looking at multiple opportunities to think through how we can begin to

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: or accelerate assessing the effectiveness of those tools, and as a part of our broader technology strategy in general.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I think more to come on that, but that's something that we are looking really seriously at.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Glad to hear it and look forward to continued conversations on those issues too.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Gutierrez asks about ECE pay parity, 3K utilization (85% of 52,000 seats), pre-K utilization (80% of 69,000 seats), and 3K/pre-K offer data. Deputy Chancellor Hawkins reports 70% of families received their top choice, 84% received a top-three choice, and 100% of applicants received an offer.
chapters.title: CM Gutierrez on ECE pay parity and 3K/pre-K utilization data

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Councilmember Gutierrez.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Good to see everyone.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: What time is it?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Oh, it's early, everybody.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, I have a few questions.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I'm going to focus, obviously, on early childhood education, although I do have maybe like two other questions for everybody else.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So first, I just want to start.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I just wanted to see if...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Deputy Chancellor, you wanted to expand.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I know Speaker Menon had asked on the pay parity.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I thought, I felt really encouraged by you referring to it as compensation parity and really wanting to look at benefits.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Is there anything else that you can expand on that?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I know we've spoken at length every time we have a hearing, we have a number of people come and testify.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: It still seems to be the biggest issue for folks to really buy into, expand to get to universal childcare.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And as you know, we're working really hard on trying to get more people to want to pursue a career in this.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So is there anything that you can expand right now on kind of how you're looking at it or how you're even thinking about beginning those conversations?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Because I understand it's layered.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I appreciate that, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, you know, nothing to share that would be any less than kind of a non-update, and I really want to be thoughtful about giving you something with substance.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But please know, you know, as a black woman and knowing that this specific sector is comprised of underpaid women of color, this is something we take.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: very seriously and the administration has been and the mayor himself has been very clear about taking this on firsthand.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we're looking at the pay structure, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We need to clearly define what equity is, understanding the nuances across the sector.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then the part around just health coverage and insurance, you know, some of the things we're looking at is the cost of insurance, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so nothing, again, substantial to share right now, but it is something we're taking very seriously.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Excellent, excellent.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And is there any...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Any kind of time frame where you think you'll be able to kind of come back and just kind of share anything on it?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Again, I get that it's layered.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I get that it's complicated.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I get that getting data even is difficult.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So kind of what's your timeline?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yesterday would have been nice, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I think we all want that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But, you know, what I can agree with, I will confer with our colleague, Emily List, because that's something we have to do and develop together, and we'll get back to you on what the timeline could be.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, well, I look forward to continuing those conversations and encourage for folks that are here or will be testifying this week just to continue to hold us all accountable because we all want to get there.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, I'm going to ask some questions about 3K that I think at the prelim were not shared.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Is there a current 3K utilization rate that you all have that you can share?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so the utilization...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: For 3K is 85%, and that is for about 52,000 seats.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And then do you have one for pre-K as well?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, that is 80% for about 69,000 seats.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And can you share at this time current enrollment for 3K and pre-K?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it's that utilization rate.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: That's what the current enrollment is?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Exactly.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And then I wanted to ask, is there anything, I know all of the,

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: The assignments went out.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: That's not the right word, but you know what I mean.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The offers?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: The offers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I got you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I got you.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I'm in the trenches, too.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I don't know why I'm...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Can you share a little bit about...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I don't know if it's now okay for you to share kind of like the amount of families that were able to get first, second, third pick, any of that data that you can share now.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I know it's recent, but for 3K and pre-K.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We got approval from our colleagues in the Office of Student Enrollment.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so one thing we are very proud about, we did it last year for this current school year, and we're doing it now for the upcoming school year, is any family who did apply by the application deadline received an offer.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So 100% offers were given to families who applied.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's great.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so across 3K and pre-K, there are about 100,000 applications with about 57,000.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Actually, let me take a step back.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There were about 100 applications, and then offers exceed the number of applicants because sometimes we manually place students.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so for the office of 3K, it was about 57,000, 56,838.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then for pre-K, we extended 43,083 offers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And of those, specifically for, I think this is 3K specifically, and I'll get back to you, Councilmember, on the first, second, third choice for pre-K.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But 3K, you know, we are trying to get to universal.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so those numbers around priority placement or placements based on the priority and preferences.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So 70% of families receive their top choice.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: 84% received one of their top three choices, and 88% received a choice on their application.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: 88% received a choice on the application.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And for the 12% that maybe received an offer that they've never heard of, what recourse do they have?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I know, you know, your office was super helpful.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: We encourage people to, like, you know, sign up to the waiting list, add your name.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: But what...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Kind of how are you moving along that 12%?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Because I think those parents are often the most vocal, right?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: They're like, I live in Crown Heights.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I don't know why I'm expected to go to, I'm making this up, but like Bushwick, let's just say.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: For whatever reason.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: How are you all kind of working through that and those placements?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So a few things.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so thanks to, you know, the motivating factor that you all have at just encouraging us and holding us accountable, the investments from the administration and the governor around just creating new seats, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so that's helpful in creating placements closer to home or work, wherever the preference is of that family.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we've gotten the distance down significantly for the upcoming year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: About 0.8 miles, we actually decreased the distance of placements.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I think that is worth celebrating.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Now.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This might be a provocative statement around the 12%.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There is no part of our system where 100% of families get their choice, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm a parent who just went through the high school application process, and I had to wipe some tears and give some hugs because she did not necessarily get into one of her top choices.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so I really want to be clear that we have to level set expectations.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But we always want families to be within a reasonable distance of travel.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so, again, it's going back to investing and placing seats and creating a more flexible system

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: where our community members, may they be CBO schools, family child care homes, can offer care based on community need and not be limited, families not be limited to where need is placed.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we are trying to infuse the system in ways that are thoughtful to create that flexibility.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it does take some investment, but it also takes some nimbleness in the contract.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Yeah, and I understand that.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think the instances that are being brought up to us and to my colleagues are ones where, you know, 3K and pre-K doesn't work the same way as K through 12, and we have zone schools, and we have schools that we know will take us.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: These are instances where parents are like, I don't, the school is a train right away, right?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: These are the extreme cases that I'm referring to.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And in those instances, no parent is not flexible because they realize they've engaged in a process that's, for the most part, still kind of up in the air.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: It's those specific instances that I'm referring to where people are being, you know, having to travel a lot further than point eight, which I think is wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So I want to give you all your flowers because I think that's important.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: But I'm talking about these extreme situations, which are common.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Gutierrez asks about the 2K pilot including home-based providers, family child care network costs, and whether unaffiliated providers can serve as their own networks. Hawkins explains the RFI process allowing group family daycare organizations to serve as networks, a new model for 2K.
chapters.title: CM Gutierrez on 2K pilot and home-based provider networks

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And then if we could move to 2K.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So exciting.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Applications went live, yes, last week.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: June 2nd.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Yeah, really exciting stuff.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Are you able to share at this point the proposed contractual seat cost for each 2K seat?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We can give that to the council afterwards.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It is a bit complex, and I don't want to take up too much of your time, but we can definitely share that afterwards.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And just to confirm, this 2K pilot applications went live last week.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: It's for enrollment for the fall.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: It's extended day, extended year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That is the primary seat model, expanded day, full year, but there are some providers who will be offering school day, school year as well.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, okay, excellent.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: That's exciting.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, I want to move to the home.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: home-based providers under this model.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: It does appear in the 2K pilot rollout that a large number of the home-based providers will be participating.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Can you share

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: What costs, what other costs do the family child care networks contracts cover at this point?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the family child care network structure, and just for everyone to have awareness, we contract with a network that then affiliates with a family child care home.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so through that cost per child rate,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: the network retains a portion to support that kind of administrative oversight and support.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So they provide coaching and training.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They have education specialists, as we call them, to provide that coaching and training in addition to what we provide within the Division of Early Childhood Education.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But they also support with the administrative oversight and support.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So they actually remit the payments.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To the family child care home and provide any other additional supports that they need to maintain compliance with the Office of Children and Family Services.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And do the contracts include any liability insurance costs, certification costs?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That is an allocable cost.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so they can, they as a network, can bill us for that insurance.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Is there any role that home-based providers who are not affiliated with a network currently play under this 2K rollout or will play to the best of your foreshadowing ability?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So one thing we were very thoughtful about is the placement.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We don't want to oversaturate communities because we know that some of our family child care partners want to remain independent, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And they're part of a healthy ecosystem of care that centers family and parent choice.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we did really look at expanded data set to determine their placement, and we worked with ACS on that really closely with them.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But what we hope to do is we hope they do consider partnering with us and affiliate.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And what we created as an opportunity in this RFI process that we released to encourage new 2K and 3K providers was that group family daycare organizations can actually serve as a network.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that's a new model.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so it's really exciting.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They are...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So one family, a home-based provider or family, yeah, sorry, just make sure I have the terminology correct, who is not within a network right now.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Can serve as their own network?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If they applied through the RFI and received, and they were selected, their proposal was selected.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: For this pilot?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: For this initial year, correct.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we plan to do that moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And is there, and we should, we will, I think, expand a little bit more because I think people are

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: are very curious and we want to be very careful.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I know you all share in that, that, you know, under the two administrations ago rollout of UPK, I think there were a lot of folks that ultimately had to close their doors because they were not in the process.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And we want to be really careful.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I don't want to be a part of something that's going to force these women, majority women, to shut down shop.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So for any of these home-based providers that are seeking to join a network or be their own network,

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: What is the process, right?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Let's say they're not one of these in the 2K pilot for this year, for this round.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: What is the best way for them to remain engaged?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: What is the best way for them to just not fall through the cracks?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So for the latter, if they wanted to operate specifically group family, not family daycare programs, if they wanted to operate as a network, that opportunity has since passed, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There was an RFI process.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They had to apply.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so I will say that if there are future opportunities, we will note that on our webpage.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But for those who are interested in affiliating for 2K or not 2K, right, we offer infant care, we offer 3K care through family child care.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They have the opportunity to reach out to us to determine if they want to affiliate with any of our existing about two dozen networks that already exist across about 48 contracts.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we have one in each, at least one in each borough.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so they can reach out to us.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There is a process to make sure it's a right fit, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We want the

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: affiliated provider to choose the network that they want to partner with.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Language is a real important aspect of that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Cultural responsiveness is at the core, but we also want the network to have some say in the affiliated provider.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And so- And I'm sorry to interrupt.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: No, it's okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Is every network that is affiliated

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: have the capacity to take on providers at this point?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That is a fantastic question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Not every single one of them have the capacity.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Some of them are at kind of their max target, but we are having conversations with those networks now and the UFT to determine if we can have some wiggle room in shifting that target threshold for them.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Yeah, I think that's a concern.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think especially the ones that have reached out to me have said, like, we've tried and they're...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: capacity.

chapters.summary: Gutierrez asks about the birth-to-two pilot, noting 160 seats are full and asking why the program isn't expanding to the original 240-seat target. Hawkins explains they focused on sustaining existing seats and converting some to 2K districts, with funding found through optimizing existing contracts.
chapters.title: CM Gutierrez on birth-to-two pilot program funding

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, I want to just switch gears to the birth to two pilot.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Sorry, Chancellor, I got you.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Don't worry about it.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I'm in the zone, but it's only a couple more, and then I'll ask you questions.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So the council was able to fund this $10 million, and thank you to the DOE for really kind of having it come to life and really encouraged

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: to hear that the administration is going to fund it to kind of roll it over into the next year, which is really exciting.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Can you share, are these the same providers from the last year?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, the same exact.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The same, the 160 seats.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And excitingly enough, all of those 160 seats are full.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And filled.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And this is just a new crop of two-year-olds with the same providers?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Or younger than.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Sorry, yeah, Earth 2.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Can you share why, is it, because I think originally it was, the allocation was for 240 seats.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And I know that there were some issues as to why it was not for this year, however, why are we sticking to the same 160 and not shooting for the 240?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I appreciate that question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so what we wanted to do was make sure that the existing seats that are already up and running can sustain for the upcoming school year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we focus.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's just funding on that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We also are focusing on making sure that those two-year-olds in the districts that was once a part of the Birth to Two initiative, but now part of a 2K district, will be converted to 2K and can support the 2K.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Is that a lot?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Do you know what the number is?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No, it's very, I don't have it, but I can get back to you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's very small.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Can we receive a list of the providers for FY27, including the school district and number of seats?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: For birth to two initiative?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Birth to two, excellent.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And then can you share why that funding was not in the executive plan?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So what we did was something I think is very creative.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You all know I used to work at OMB, and so math is my friend.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But we worked together to really determine through all of the shifts, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We got these investments for 2K, for 3K, and for pre-K.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We want to make sure that we take this opportunity to optimize, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so some of our existing partners, specifically in CBOs, decided to, like, age down or age up or move.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that allowed us to free up some city tax levy that allowed us to support the Birth to Two initiative for another year.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And do you know if there is, will it go beyond FY27?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I cannot speak to that right now, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Are you feeling good about it?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, yes.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: This mayor is always...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm always feeling good.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm feeling very hopeful about early care and education.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: No, I think it's...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I mean, I think, in all honesty, I think it's exciting.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And I think for, especially for communities that are not in this, like, 2K rollout, I think it's an opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: to expose them to like what it's like to contract with the city under this program.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And you know, there are rumors.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think I'm sure when the applications went live last week, I'm sure there were a number of parents who were like, oh man, I'm not in a zone, right?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And so I think this is, I think it would be great to continue it and would love to keep the conversation going about making that happen.

chapters.summary: Gutierrez asks about specific accommodations for 2K students with disabilities, noting families have trauma from past experiences. Hawkins commits to creating inclusive environments with specific supports like third teachers and sensory tools, and promises to update the website with details once finalized.
chapters.title: CM Gutierrez on 2K accommodations for children with disabilities

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, I want to just talk about my last question under early childhood, I promise, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: It's just the concern, I know you're like, I need a break.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I love the topic, it's fine by me.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think a lot of advocates and parents, to be completely honest, have

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: trauma from not this administration, but just trauma from, you know, not feeling like the 3K seats or pre-K seats were guaranteed for their child if their child had a disability.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And I don't mean, you know, the need for early intervention, but a disability.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: As you know, we fought tooth and nail to get funding to make sure that we were following the law even.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And so I think that there is this bad taste in the mouths of so many families.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And now with this 2K launch, again, which is very exciting, it would be really great.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: If there was something very explicit or something concrete that shared with these families

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: who are now looking for, you know, a seat for their two-year-old who has a disability, if there was something concrete that we could share that said, we have a plan, we are working on a plan.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I know the site says, you know, that there will be accommodations, but we don't know what those are.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And again, in a time when we have so much work to do to clean up, I think, from the past, what can you share today about the specificity of those

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: accommodations that are not going to meet the needs of kids who have disabilities that are not going to be resolved with early intervention resources?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And one thing I know we've heard our mayor say, we've heard our chancellor say, right, this is for all of our children, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And one thing we are not, I would say, belittling this process to is seat counts, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We are caring about the quality of seats and the care that each of the little will get in that seat.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so that means we have to be thoughtful about how we...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Create inclusive environments, but also ensure that we provide the right interventions and placements where appropriate.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so I cannot provide any specificity because what I never do, and I hope this is on brand, is over-promise and under-deliver.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But please know that this is something we are being very thoughtful about.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And once we have those specifics, and again, we're not doing this just within the doors of Tweed.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We are doing this with our partners at the health department because they too are a part of this and they are the experts as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we are creating what that array, that list will look like.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Maybe a third teacher, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Or specific sensory tools and materials.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Whatever it is, we are creating a very clear process to have like money in the coffers to provide that, a referral process.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: a place where folks can go, folks may be the family or the educator, where they can go and tap into that resource and make sure that that is clear and accessible.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we will be updating our website with that information once it is finalized.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And do you, thank you, thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And do you think that that will happen before the deadline to apply for 2K?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have currently on the website that it is open to all children with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I cannot commit to the accommodations details.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But again, it won't be an exhaustive list because the needs will vary, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so what we hope to be- You don't know what the need is yet.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Exactly.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: But I think that when we don't meet

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: the potential for a need is when parents start to feel like this isn't for me, so I'm not even going to bother.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I agree.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And again, like kind of undoing the trauma of years and budgets past, I think that we do have a responsibility.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think it's great.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I saw it on the website, and that's why I want to take the time today to kind of hear that from you, that you are committed to making these accommodations.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: We are absolutely committed, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And then I have one question for the Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I just want to second what my colleague, Shaker, Councilmember Chris Nunn had raised about AI in schools.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: We've gotten a ton of feedback from especially parents of K through 5 that are deeply concerned with the guidance.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So I know we're going to continue that conversation.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I know Chair Dinowitz is on top of that.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I wanted to ask about the funding for...

chapters.summary: Gutierrez asks about restorative justice funding, seeking clarification on whether there are cuts in the savings plan. Officials clarify the $6 million is not baselined and would need to be restored through the adopted budget process, with no specific savings cut to restorative justice.
chapters.title: CM Gutierrez on restorative justice funding clarification

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: For restorative justice.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: What can you share?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Is that you or not?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: That's you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No, no, no, yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I was expecting the AI question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's fine.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: No, that was it.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: We could be here all day with AI.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You gave me an okie-dokie there.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You're like, okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think I need to feel my concern, but I'm not going to ask about it right now.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, got you.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: For restorative justice, can you share a little bit about the...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: The issue with the vacancies?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Because as I understand it, that's where the cuts are being proposed is because there are vacancies or no?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Am I misunderstanding that?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, no.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So first of all, you know, again, we are committed to ensuring that we have high quality

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: programs when it comes to restorative practices is a huge part of what we do and our response to this discipline code and so I'm going to turn it over to chief to talk a little bit more but I'm not exactly sure the vacancy well just and I'm sorry I'm I

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: misspoke so it's cuts to RJ and then vacancies related to social services so two different things

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, so you want us to adjust the cuts to how the cuts to RJ?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Yeah, I want to understand why there are cuts to RJ, kind of what happened there.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: I don't see, I mean, the cuts, what cuts to show you first?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Was there no cuts to RJ training and proposed in the executive?

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: Cuts to the RJ training?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: When you say, I mean, the last council member, I apologize, I forgot the name, the question that she asked was about cohorts.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: Is that what you're referring to as well?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You're referring to...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the cut in the budgetary cut.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: In the savings.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: In an effort for all the savings, my understanding was that there was cuts to restorative justice training.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There was no cut in our savings that was submitted related to restorative justice.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think what folks have been talking about is the $6 million.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That is not in fiscal year 27 as of yet.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know that typically that...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The funding for that or the allocation for that happens through the adopted budget negotiation process.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I think that's what folks are referring to, but there's no specific cuts that we've taken for restorative justice.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And the six, thank you, thank you for clarifying.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And the six million that you're referring to that, you know, we will see at adoption, can you share, is that being invested in specific programs that you can highlight or is it any of the social services or that's separate?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I'll turn it over.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Mark, I don't know if you want to talk about specifics about restorative justice, but in terms of the work that we're doing now and that we hope to continue to do in fiscal year 27 once the funding is allocated.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: Yeah, once the funding is reallocated into the budget, that is the intention is to continue the work that we're doing around restorative.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: To expand it to more schools around the city.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, and there's nothing that you can say about, so, and the $6 million is a new allocation?

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: No, $6 million is not a new allocation.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: What's getting confused here is we're talking about $6 million that is not baselined.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: Right?

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: And that's what you're referring to.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: So without the baseline funding, obviously restorative is going to take some change in the way that it is modeled and offered to schools.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: So if, in fact, the $6 million is, in fact, restored, I'll say restored, we'll continue to be able to provide the existing schools.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: Yes, ma'am.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And is there any intention for...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Or is there any need, in your opinion, for additional funding to meet this need?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Because I think there's a lot of advocates that are saying they would love to see more investments in restorative justice, obviously.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So I think that is kind of where my line of questioning wants to get to.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I get $6 million is not nothing, but I think is there an opportunity or kind of what is the assessment on the need for more to expand on this, to reach more schools?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think at the prelim we had some feedback from council members who had engaged with their schools about specific programming.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Kind of where is the line of thinking or priority for that specific area where people want to see more of it?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Maybe stronger programs or expanded.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: So everyone has an opinion around what they would like to see as it relates to restorative.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: What we'd like to see in New York City Public School is a program that continues to evolve.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: Evolve in the sense that more people are trained, the mindset is shifting, right?

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: What we need to be able to do is continue to have more people invest in the overall work, right?

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: So we continue to offer cohorts where people are not showing.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: Right?

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: And so the real conversation is around getting more people to buy in to the notion of restorative and then create our internal muscle where our staff can train our staff and we can continue to grow and grow and grow.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: It's not necessarily about more money.

utterances.speaker: Mark
utterances.text: It's about more people investing in the notion of restorative practices.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you, chairs.

chapters.summary: Council Member Zhuang asks about state foundation aid ($18.7 million left to be allocated), ELL weight increases, foster care transportation funding, and 3K under-budgeting. CFO Scott confirms the $170 million cut was reversed in the preliminary plan and $35 million supports new 3K seats.
chapters.title: CM Zhuang on state foundation aid and 3K budgeting

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: So one quick question about the state funding, because I know that the state passed the budget, yay, for FY27, which I know we were waiting on for a while.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And they're going to provide $15.3 billion in school aid to New York City, including $11 billion in foundation aid.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: So just wanted to know how much additional foundation aid funding is left to be recognized at adoption over what was included in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yep, so we estimate about $18.7 million is left to be allocated in the adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And then can you also discuss the importance of getting the English language learner weight increased and a new weight for students in temporary housing and foster care, which I know we talked about?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We are extremely excited that those two things happened within the state budget, something that we have been advocating for for a very long time.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know that while we are happy and grateful that those things actually made it in, we're going to continue to advocate for regional cost index and increase funding for our students with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But happy to see that we're on the right track with having the foundation aid formula updated.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we will continue to partner with our city and state partners for more funding from the state to New York City.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And then given that, would you support the call for $3 million in funding to provide interim transportation for students in foster care that are awaiting school bus service?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I think that was the amount that we were looking at.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: 3 million from the funding that we received.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So unfortunately, any increases above 3% that we receive from state aid is actually geared towards or shifted to C4E, which is a restricted funding source.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And then just in terms of the 3K under budgeting, and then I promise I'll be done with my questions at least.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: In the preliminary plan, the administration addressed significant under budgeting in the 3K budget, including reversing the 170 million peg from the previous administration.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: So can you speak to the under budgeting in the 3K budget and what factors led to this issue?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I think I want to pivot to the fact that that funding was actually put up in the preliminary plan, which did also reverse the $170 million in cuts.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I think what this administration has come in and done is show the importance of investing in sustaining the system.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Again, prior to that, we had $170 million worth of cuts related to empty seats, but we never actually did anything with those seats.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So happy to see that that was restored and then the acknowledgement of our contractual obligations to our CBO.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So just again, want to point out that the preliminary budget, which is this admin's first budget, addressed all of those things.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Yeah, because it included the reversal of the $170 million peg, yes.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And then what was the gap in 3K funding in FY27 prior to the preliminary plan?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I can get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And then for the $205 million for 3K and $30 million for pre-K, how will you utilize the additional funds and recognize...

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Recognizing the preliminary plan from the state executive budget for FY27.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So those funds are doing two things specifically for 3K primarily is creating those new seats I mentioned earlier, but also sustaining that existing, the existing portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And then how much of this new state funding will be utilized for the 2000 new 3K seats?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So $73 million is dedicated to 2K.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And then what other programming has this funding been allocated towards?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: For early care and education specifically for New York City public schools, I believe you cover a day chair.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Are you ready or?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I'm always ready.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Go, yeah?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Yes.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about K-12 special education, noting the executive plan focuses on pre-K but does not expand AIMS, NEST, or Horizon. Deputy Chancellor Foti reports 80% offer acceptance rates, attendance 7 points above peers, and 9 out of 10 parent satisfaction, with 675 additional seats for FY27.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on K-12 special education program demand and outcomes

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What an intro.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Go.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: First, it was bugging me, D.C.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Hawkins.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I have to make a correction.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Math is not your friend.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Math is everyone's friend.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Oh, I said it's my friend.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's actually my bestie.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's your bestie.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Sorry, just wanted to clarify one thing.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: The 2,000 new 3K seats, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Oh, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it's a bit of a combination, but I think primarily $35 million is intended to support with the development of the new 3K seats.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Now I can go.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I go.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: K-12 special education.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As I mentioned in my opener, we're interested in discussing what the investments actually are, because it seems to be focused only on early childhood, or most on early childhood.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The executive plan includes additional funding for pre-K special education and IESP support at non-public schools.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This plan does not include an expansion of AIMS, NEST, Horizon, or other effective programs for students with disabilities in grades K-12.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: DC Foti, you've...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And Chancellor, you've both spoken about these programs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What metrics do you use to track the success of these programs that you could say they're wildly successful?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, you know, we are deeply committed to creating high-quality programs for families close to home.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: All of the programs you mentioned, one is that when we do open these programs, they are immensely popular and families really try to make use of them.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Can I pause you?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I just want to ask, like,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What does popular look like?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If you could say how many applications for these seats exist.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It means that, one, I think the last time I looked, 80% of families accepted the offer.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: to these programs.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then also, when you think of metrics, you know, we look at graduates and where we see significant levels, in fact, sometimes outperforming our students who

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: are in general education.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I'm going to turn it over to Christina to talk more about this.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But to be clear, this is a huge part of our strategy as we move forward in

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: in not only resolving our due process cases or getting them done, but investing in high-quality programs close to home.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So, Christina?

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: Thank you, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: I think a couple of other data points are in terms of attendance, we're seeing our students in our specialized programs coming to school more often than their peers.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: And in our AIMS program, it's 88%, which is at least seven points above their peers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: When you say their peers, you mean students with autism in a general community school?

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: Students with significant autism in District 75 or community school.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: Nest and Horizon, 92%.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: Again, that I think is about six percentage points higher than their peers in ICT or special classes.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: Nest are 50% more likely to be proficient, and Horizon are two times more likely to be proficient in math and ENLA.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: We also do parent satisfaction surveys where 9 and 10 parents say that they would recommend.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: their other families to their specialized programs.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: I think you also asked about seats, Chair, and just to the beginning of your question, you know, are we expanding K-12?

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: Yes, we are, and we are going to be

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: expanding our autism programs by more than 675 seats in FY27.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: That is an increase.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: We're going to be grading up our existing programs, which is exciting.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: I do want to say, yes, we've been focusing on the $67.5 million in terms of the early childhood investment.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: But we're six months into this administration, and we're seeing $67.5 million coming our way.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: We're seeing an additional 675 seats.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: And then the IESP investment is significant and another example of this administration's commitment in the past six months.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: That investment, if we are able to continue to hire those providers, right, we're all sharing the same resources.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: And so an investment in IESP work is an investment in the overall infrastructure of special education.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: It is hugely appreciative.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: I also just shared the last thing.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: I want to thank you for so deeply diving into this work.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: You know, this is the first time we've been asked to provide our counsel on a briefing on due process.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: It is a difficult topic, but you all have spent so much time digging in, and thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: And also the briefing requested on...

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: specialized programs.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: The fact that you all are able, I mean, your testimony, you did my job for me today, Chair, so thank you.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: And I really, all of us in the community recognize how much this matters, so thank you.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how many students were rejected from specialized programs. Foti reveals 2,070 applicants for 510 AIMS seats and 1,410 applications for 560 NEST/Horizon kindergarten seats. Lee presses on why there isn't greater investment given the moral and fiscal imperative.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on specialized program seat shortages and unmet demand

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And as I said, I think we all agree this is a moral imperative, but also a fiscal one.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I'm going to get to the IESP, and I want to thank you for that briefing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It was very informative.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As I said, you should be a teacher.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I mean, you're also a good lawyer, so you should do both.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The 675 seats that you cited, are those in District 75 schools, or are those part of Horizon, Nest, Ames programs?

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: Part of Horizon, Nest, and Ames programs were grading up.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: So the kids that are currently in the programs, we, of course, want them to continue next year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So this is not so much a...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I mean, you would define that as an expansion or you would define that as just keeping pace with the current class?

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: For K-12, the last thing we want to do is pull options from kids.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: And so it is expansion of existing program seats.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: Not a new program, but we're making sure that the program continues.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: So maybe expansion's not the word.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: Sustaining.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sustaining, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how many students were not offered a seat or rejected, whatever word you want to use, because there weren't enough seats?

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: I mean, those numbers are staggering, right?

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: So we have, I have to say, for our AIMS program, which we were on to something, we thought the AIMS program was exactly what our kids need.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: 510 seats, 510 kids in those seats, 2,070 applicants.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: For Horizon, we had a, currently we have an enrollment of 2,203 kids with a kindergarten,

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: with 1,410 applications to Nest and Horizon for kindergarten.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: In Nest and Horizon, those 1,410 applications were for 560 seats.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do you wildly popular, do you think?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's wildly popular, yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: High demand, do you think that that's an appropriate description?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we have here, and I want to highlight something you said, Chancellor, that this is part of, this is what you just testified, that these seats are part of resolving due process cases.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That it making the investment in our public system helps resolve the due process claims where families feel the need to seek out private school education.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I also just want to add that we see reduction in

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: the travel time that kids spend

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So this also solves the parts of the bus issue in some cases.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So what I'm trying to understand is why there isn't greater investment in something that is a moral obligation and a fiscal obligation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Where is the investment in this?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And to ask a specific question, what would it cost to provide a seat to every student who applied and was deemed eligible?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I think that's something I'd have to go back to, but I'm not sure if you have.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: Yes, we can definitely, we have the per pupil cost and we can definitely send those over to you.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: In terms of

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: The overall budget, I think, Chair, you know this is something that we continue to talk about, right?

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: We talked about a lot of priorities today, a lot of things that people want, a lot of things that kids need.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: One in four kids have a disability.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: We are seeing those numbers rise significantly and the needs of our students coming into the system rising significantly.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: That is true nationally, but we're very much, this is a local issue, of course.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: So the demand is going to continue to grow, but I would argue that every time we have an investment, we have shown enormous return on investment in terms of the outcomes you just have heard about.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: We also talked last hearing about the due process infrastructure and a lot of private benefit from public funds, and that is why we are glad to have the best lawyer in the city here helping us come up with a plan.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: With strategy.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So you agree with me.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: I agree with you on most things, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: I especially agree with Chair Lee on maybe, okay, I'll stop now.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: But most people do.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: The point is, though, we've had right since 2000, over a decade of $8.5 billion going to due process.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: And that is not just the cost of what it takes to educate the child alone.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: That is the entire economic infrastructure that is built around due process.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: Every dollar that goes to entities that are not the child themselves is a dollar that could have been invested in our public school systems to make our kids whole here.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Agreed.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we agree that these programs are popular, and in part because they're successful, you've demonstrated that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: With data, you agree that they are part of solving the due process crisis.

chapters.summary: Lee asks if DOE submitted a new needs request for specialized programs and how much was requested. Officials confirm the request was submitted to OMB but not included in the executive budget, with ongoing iterative conversations.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on DOE request to OMB for special ed program expansion

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Did the DOE submit a new needs request for these specialized programs?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how much funding would the DOE like to add for these programs in fiscal 2027?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, so the funding to support these programs are part of our conversations with OMB that have been submitted, and I feel optimistic that we'll continue to have good conversations.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As you noticed, the administration has made significant investments within special ed pre-K, recognizing that we need to capture students early.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so I think that that in itself shows that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The administration is fully invested in what the needs are for special education, you know, starting with pre-K, but we will continue to have those discussions around what the K-12 needs are.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I just want to make sure that I'm clearing the answer.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the investments in pre-K are wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I like how you stood up for that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Look at you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The investments in pre-K are wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I often say, you know, I wish so many more of my students had gone to pre-K or that they had been identified as having a need much earlier on.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think they would have had a much easier life, particularly in school.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And at the same time, we have the needs as kids are in K-12.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I just want to be clear in your testimony that you did submit the needs, but OMB, I guess, rejected the increase.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how much additional funding did you request from OMB?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I don't know if I'll necessarily categorize it as rejecting it, and I can get back to you the number.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm sorry I don't have it right in front of me, but I can let you know what it was.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Not to split hairs, but you made a request and OMB said...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sorry, so the reason I'm saying it's not, I'm not categorizing it as rejecting is because the budget process is iterative, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we have ongoing conversations with them throughout the year about what our needs are and are always hopeful that we will see those needs reflected in an upcoming budget.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So when, if you say rejected, that means it's rejected.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Never talk about it again.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's not the case, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we do, we have submitted it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We'll continue to, and we'll continue to have the conversations.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For this budget, for the executive budget, there is 675 additional seats to sustain the program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You've made a request to expand.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
