utterances.text: Nest aims horizon even more and OMB came back and said not right now It didn't make it into the executive.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It didn't make it into the executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so That that's what I'm asking.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the so the administration so the deal we is requesting more funding for this and the administration is

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: not putting it in the executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How can we support then?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I think there's wholesale agreement on the importance of these programs, morally and fiscally.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How can the council support the DOE in expanding these crucial and successful programs?

chapters.summary: General Counsel Vladek discusses the due process ecosystem, noting autism cases cost $145,000 per year, Carter schools lack accountability for outcomes, and the $1.5 billion in due process spending has no academic transparency. He calls it both a fiscal and moral crisis.
chapters.title: General Counsel Vladek on due process costs and Carter school accountability

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The joke is always that the lawyers are no good at math, but I'm going to give it a try.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But math is everyone's best friend.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Math is everyone's best friend.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So, you know, the last time we were here, we talked about how we've got to think of the ecosystem of special education as not just the DOE budget.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I think, you know, when DC Foti talks about the impact of her programs and when we talk about the comparative cost for her to develop more seats versus a due process case,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: it's really worth expanding on that a little.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We are in a bigger world.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Autism diagnoses are exploding all over the country.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I really highly recommend to everyone the New York Times has begun a series of coverage on the explosion in the provision of quote ABA services autism

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Behavioral, what's the A?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Analysis.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Applied behavioral analysis, thank you, ABA services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Because guess what?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: These services are Medicaid reimbursable.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so the New York Times has begun a series looking at how ABA services are a new cash cow for some well-intentioned people and plenty of bad actors.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Guess what else?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: More and more due process cases have autism components to them.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And when we talk about average cost per student of an IEP due process case is $105,000 a year, yeah, that's head and shoulders above what we're spending.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Except if we're talking about autism cases specifically, we're already up at $145,000 a year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And in addition to the other broad points that my colleagues have made, unlike DCFOD's programs,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: all of the Carter schools that receive this funding, $100,000, $150,000, $300,000 a year, there's no accountability.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They don't have to demonstrate to anyone what outcomes they're getting, because as you hear from our leadership and our chancellor first and foremost, we're not just talking about it is a fiscal crisis, it is a moral crisis,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: and the moral part of that is we want all of our students to be set up to succeed.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have no idea what's happening to students once they become harder cases.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There are certain legal eccentricities here that mean once they're a carter,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Carter case in kindergarten, first grade, second grade, they're never coming back to public school.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: More and more we have kids who are Carter cases who've never come to public school in the first place.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's an industry of folks who are, you know, living large on these dollars and there's no

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: set aside the lack of academic transparency and accountability.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's also no visibility into where the school's funding is coming from.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: How could they exist without Carter dollars?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What are they providing to students and families?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Is everybody certified?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the money that's moving around in this space

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: some of which gets chalked up to the due process budget, it's going somewhere.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And unlike the dollars in DCFOD's budget, we have no accounting for what that $1.5 billion is accomplishing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So it sounds like you're saying how the DOE can support, how the council can support the DOEs by holding accountable some of these institutions with transparency for outcomes?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we're not shirking our duty.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have, we're going to end the year with the number of due process cases down from 26,000 two years ago to under 16,000 this year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're going to end the year having gone from 18,000 IESP cases two years ago to under 7,000 cases.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: These are huge.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: huge changes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And those, they will generate savings.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Some of the strategies that we've used to bring those numbers down will generate further savings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can you talk about some of those strategies that you've used to bring the number of due process claims down?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I think first and foremost, what DC FODI referred to and what the mayor has clearly prioritized is making sure we're serving students with IESPs directly.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This is not a thing that used to exist, but now we have hundreds of staff who are devoted to, whether they're psychologists, social workers, teachers, administrators,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: providers, to engaging directly through our CSEs with families, ensuring that they have providers who are qualified to provide the services that they need,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: exercising some actual oversight so that we can be sure that the services are of an adequate quality.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've also been working to engage with schools, with private schools where students have IESPs because the law

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: does not envision school districts having the same extent of responsibility to families who opt out of public school as we have to students who want a public school education.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so in order to make those dollars make sense in IESP cases, we should be working with schools to determine where we can scale services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I wish we can scale services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're getting a better reception from those schools.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We are finding success.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have raised rates.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There was a valid complaint for a while that rates had not been raised in a long time.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We did a fair market rate study for set services because that's a unique service that only in New York City do we provide it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we had to really get into the weeds of...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What does it actually cost us to provide this?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that has helped us lower the cost of these services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And frankly, we've continued to, we have adopted and continued to implement and enforce fraud controls and strategic litigation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Meaning what?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Meaning, you know, all of these.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: 16,000 cases, most of them go to a hearing at an independent tribunal.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, we are cross-examining witnesses, we are demanding documents, we are insisting on a demonstration that parents actually know there's a case on their family's behalf,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: and we're starting to look at moving to recoup some of the funds that go out the door.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about strategies used to reduce due process cases. Vladek describes standing up a tribunal at OATH reducing case time from over a year to under 70 days, strategic litigation, fraud controls, rate studies, and direct service provision, reducing cases from 26,000 to under 16,000.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on strategies to reduce due process cases

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Because the legal process itself is not very well thought out, where we win the case in the end, but it's taken long enough to get there that we've made all these payments that we shouldn't have had to make.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So lots of things that we're doing that we're very optimistic about.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: At the last, the preliminary budget hearing, you went over

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: as a teacher would, the history of the due process claims

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: a change that happened under the de Blasio administration whereby the cases would be settled.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They would automatically be settled.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is that policy changing now and that you are contesting a lot of these due process claims?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That policy changed, we changed that policy several years ago, but we didn't do that in isolation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The first thing that we did was we said, okay, you know, we have IESP cases over here and tuition things over here and service gaps over here.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Put all of the pieces of machinery that due process requires in one place.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We rationalized it, we streamlined it, and we got strategic about how we manage the cases.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Meaning what?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Meaning we spent a lot of time, and thank you to Comptroller Lander and now Comptroller Levine, because they've helped us identify the cases that should be settled quickly.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Where we didn't make an offer of FAPE, and the student has a placement that works for them, and there's no need for us to fight it, and we can just get that off the docket for everybody's sake.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But we've also developed screening tools to identify what are the cases that need to be scrutinized much more closely.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we treat different kinds of cases differently, having stood up this tribunal, because it used to take more than a year to get a case from filing to completion.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So now that we've stood up this tribunal at oath, it's taking us, I think, under 70 days from start to finish.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So no longer...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: settling every case without distinction, yeah, we don't settle every case without distinction anymore.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We settle the cases that should be settled.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We litigate the cases that should be litigated.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about other agencies using the New Visions portal. Officials confirm ACS, DYCD, DHS, CUNY, and community school CBOs use the tool, with 1,542 schools logged on, 18,500 monthly users, and 4,600 users relying on it multiple times per week.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on New Visions portal interagency usage

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I want to turn over to the New Visions portal.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This is a small contract, but it was asked about before.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think you had testified that not many schools are using it, or only some people in the schools are using it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We were told 99% of schools used the New Vision portals this year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I don't think I said not a lot of schools.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I would more say it's used as more an administrative tool.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: A lot of schools do use the New Vision portal, but maybe not a lot of people in the schools are using it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So not necessarily a lot of teachers, but it is a widely used tool.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's a very popular tool from my perspective for the purpose that it serves.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we are continuing to talk to both our, and I think the opportunity that we see within the tool is also

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: across agency, which we haven't necessarily bolstered yet, but that's an opportunity that we're thinking about.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we're continuing to talk to our partners at New Visions about, you know, what are the options going forward and possibilities for not.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Disrupting the service.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you know how many schools are using the New Visions portal, even if it's the administration, how many schools are using it?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we've seen a logon, at least a logon for over 1,500 schools within the system.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the thought process behind potentially cutting the New Visions contract. The Chancellor explains the data exists elsewhere in DOE systems so it was viewed as duplicative, but acknowledges the portal's value as a one-stop shop and says conversations continue to avoid disruption.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on rationale for New Visions portal cut consideration

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How many schools are in the system?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: About 1,600.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So it's like almost all of them to some degree.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I have logged in or logged in monthly.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you have usage statistics?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have some.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I don't have.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can you share those?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, like do you have them now?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm sure I don't know.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, so I don't have them.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No, meaning like out of 1,500, like how many of them are using them once, twice, three times a month or using them in instruction or for whatever it's used for?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I can get you some of that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, well, why don't, I have other questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So maybe.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Go ahead, yeah, you can ask.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Someone else could get it and then they'll give you the little post-it note, like they always do.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And you had mentioned that other agencies use the tool as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What other agencies, to your knowledge, are using this tool?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: From my understanding, either are or wanting to use ACS and DYCD for sure.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I'm sorry, I don't know if you're...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Because DHS also uses the data.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So from my side of the work, we're working on building enhancement to the tools so that for ACS, they have access to data through agencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: DYCD typically uses that for their SYP.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: program which our colleague can speak to and then for the Department of Homeless Services is mostly leveraged around the shelter data supports.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And does CUNY use it as well?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: CUNY, your CBOs use it as well?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: CUNY, I believe so.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know definitely the community school CBOs, the community school directors leverage the tool as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So it just sounds to me, I mean, it's weird to quibble over like, you know, a little over $8 million, but it sounds like it's a tool that is used by almost all the schools, a tool that is used in an interagency fashion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can you explain the thought process behind the consideration of cutting this contract that seems to be used a lot?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, so first I'll say 1,542 schools have logged on this year, 18,500 monthly users.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: 9,000 weekly users.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Weekly users.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And 4,600 users rely on the YouthVisions portal multiple times per week.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And at a superintendent level, and certainly I use it significantly there, 249 superintendent team members logged on in 15 days this year on average for 40 days of usage.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I'll say...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In thinking about why this was proposed, all of the data that is in New Visions exists within DOE and other places.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so from that perspective, it was viewed as duplicative.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But I'll say that New Visions, for some schools and for some administrators, going to many different areas is difficult, often clunky.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so what New Vision does is has like a one-stop shop for a portal for many different, for like administrative users.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so I think, like I said, viewing it from that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Way I could see why it was viewed as potentially duplicative, but in terms of actually how the system works, I understand the value of the portal.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that's why we're continuing to have conversations.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, it just sounds like a lot of these

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You know, I say that in an almost $40 billion budget, small amount, although $8 million here, like $6 million is not a small amount of money.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But in the scheme of a $40 billion, almost $40 billion budget, you know, when you talk about $6 million restorative justice here,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 8.7 for a new visions portal, and these are actually tools that, according to your testimony, are used to a significant degree, and it's used in an interagency fashion, and it seems like it's a...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: A very necessary tool.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because if it goes away, if the contract ends, it goes away in July, it sounds like everyone from administrators to the Department of Social Services to CUNY to ACS to community-based organizations would be left in the lurch and wouldn't have the data that they need.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, there are other options.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And without having the program go away, this was a discussion of central spend.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's also having schools that use it, schools that have no whole harmless funds.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They can also pay for the service.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's schools for...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Only if it's less than $25,000.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There are other...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It was about five.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So there are other options within our system, and New Vision is a contract vendor as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So there are other options that we could talk about for how to get it rather than essential spend.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But like I said, I think we're having really meaningful conversations throughout the system as well as with other agencies about how to continue to not have interruptions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I mean, the worthwhile goal is to look at it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: every contract and to make sure everything from $1 million to $100 million contract to make sure that it's actually providing services to the students or to the teachers, to the faculty.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That is very important.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You've heard from this council when $12 billion of the dollars are spent on contracting.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We really do have to look at those contracts.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, and I think that's to the point of the cost savings team.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think that's what they did here.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: With the eye of looking at just contracts maybe objectively, you might not be able to see the way that it's used at a school level, but you could see, okay,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: the same data that the school has exists in other places, and there are teachers using other tools that they use a lot more.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so you might feel like, well, this program is potentially duplicative without seeing the context of, let's say, other agencies and so on.

chapters.summary: Lee urges that cost savings discussions include teachers and principals who actually use the tools. The Chancellor agrees and Scott confirms the next step in procurement reform is engaging school leaders about quality and service levels.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on including teachers in tool evaluation

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So again, we're having discussions to really think about what's the best way forward.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I hope, I'm going to assume you do, but I hope that these discussions you have with the cost savings team include teachers, include discussions with teachers and principals who are the ones actually using the tools.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You know, it's one thing to sit in an office and look at a spreadsheet and look at the one pager as to what, you know, different contracts do.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's another thing to go into the school and know what's actually happening in the classroom.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm going to chime in.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So as the Chancellor stated, we're having conversations with different program offices, specifically school-facing team.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we did a preliminary review of their contracts, and now the next step in that process will be for them to engage with school leaders and teachers to

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: have those conversations about quality and service levels.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Good.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And you know me, I think evaluating all of your tools is very important.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Evaluating your curricula, very important.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Evaluating everything is very important.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about NYC Reads and Solves funding levels. Officials confirm a $53 million baseline budget plus $17.3 million for expansion, and that the programs were previously self-funded without a dedicated budget line.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on NYC Reads and Solves funding levels

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I want to ask about New York City Reads and Solves.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The plan includes $17.3 million in fiscal 27 and the out years for Reads and Solves curricula.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Next school year, New York City Reads will expand to additional middle schools, and New York City Solves will expand to elementary schools for the first time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What's the current total funding levels for New York City Reads and Solves?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I mean, I'll jump in, but I would like your confirmation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So would I.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have a baseline budget for $53 million for reads and solves and an additional $17.3 million to support expansion.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Good?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, correct.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, you got it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Winning.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Good.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This is self-assessments.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This is great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's our understanding that both New York City Reads and New York City Solves have been self-funded and do not have a dedicated budget line.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can you confirm this?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Prior to us getting the dedicated funding for the expansion, we have been self-funding.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It was mentioned before about screen time and AI.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I do want to note that the AI hearing will be on June 24th.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So everyone mark your calendars.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Chair Lee is very excited.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: She's whatever this is, she's doing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I reference, because New York City solves, I will just note as a personal privilege, that I don't think most elementary school kids need calculators.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So do away with those calculators for those kids.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Let them use pen and paper, or pencil and paper, rather, for math.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Math is our friend, Chair.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about Student Success Centers facing a $3.3 million funding gap. Deputy Chancellor Gentes confirms 7-11% increases in post-secondary enrollment at schools with centers and says the next coalition meeting is June 16th, but funding is needed to begin RFP conversations.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on Student Success Centers funding gap

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The Student Success Centers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Funding for Student Success Centers has been added one year at a time since stimulus funding expired.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This programming is a unique peer support model for college and career readiness at high schools.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The executive plan does not include the $3.3 million gap in Student Success Center funding.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is DOE supportive of restoring and baselining the $3.3 million for student success centers?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, so I would say, obviously, student success centers are immensely valuable to us.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, you know, I'm going to turn it over to Jane to talk a little bit more about them, and as well as Sarita to add anything.

utterances.speaker: Jane
utterances.text: Hello again, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Jane
utterances.text: I would say unequivocally we are very, very supportive.

utterances.speaker: Jane
utterances.text: The SSC programs has shown that we have had 7 to 11 percent increases in post-secondary enrollment in the schools where we have had student success centers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and are you planning to release a new RFP for student success centers?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if so, what is the timeline and process for this RFP?

utterances.speaker: Jane
utterances.text: So we work in tandem with the coalition, and our next meeting is June 16th.

utterances.speaker: Jane
utterances.text: So we would really like to do that, but we actually really need the funding, the 3.3 funding that you all provided last year, again this year, in order to even begin those conversations.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to turn it over to, I believe, Councilmember Wong had some questions, so I will turn it over to him.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Wong questions why the hold harmless policy from COVID is still in place, asking about the cost and whether empty schools should be consolidated. The Chancellor says stability is important and removing hold harmless would cause whiplash, with the cost in the hundreds of millions.
chapters.title: CM Wong on hold harmless policy and empty school funding

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Just one question.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: One question.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Chancellor, in your statement, you mentioned that school will continue to be held harmless.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Now, as I recall, the whole harmless policy was introduced during COVID lockdown.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So because so many students left those public schools and instead of having the fundings leaving,

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: leaving the schools with the kids.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: They want

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: To keep the money and keep the funding in place.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But that was, the COVID lockdown has been lifted since 2023, February.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My question to you is why are we still holding schools harmless?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Why are we funding empty seats and even empty schools?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you answer that?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you for your question, Consul Memo.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think, you know, stability in schools is very important.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I think we need to ensure that our schools are well funded, despite the fact that, you know, we've seen enrollment declines.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If we do...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If we actually remove our own harmless funding, it would be a huge whiplash effect to schools

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: that would have to really contend with a lot of concerns at the budget.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And in some of our most vulnerable communities, they would feel the brunt of the issues.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so I think we are proud to, for this year, be able to hold our schools harmless to make sure that they are stable.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we will have an ongoing conversation about how we get to sustainability over time, including what we are able to do at the mid.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, I get that, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But what is the estimated cost of holding initial school budgets harmless?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And do you expect this additional funding to be reflected at adoption?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because I can see if we can free up so much funding and use it elsewhere rather than keeping schools open that are mostly empty.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Please answer that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we are doing our final quality assurance checks on the numbers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So once it's final, we can provide it to you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And yes, we can provide it to you once it's final.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I've visited some of the schools and I see that.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: We can definitely do something rather than keeping them empty.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My follow-up is, how can DOE not know the cost of this policy, especially given that 26, 27 enrollment projects have been available since early May?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Council Finance has estimated that this policy will cost at least $110 million, if not more.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Does this sound correct?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I wouldn't say we don't know the number.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Like I said, we're doing our final QA on the numbers, especially since school budgets are going to be released next week.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So as soon as that's final, we can provide the number.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I just don't want to give you something, and then it's going to change.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But yes, on par with over hundreds of millions, basically hundreds of millions of dollars.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And I have visited some of these schools, and common sense tells me that we can do a better job.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Just combine them and free up the funding.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That's where I'm coming from.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, you know, in the future, we'll be engaged in a conversation.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Shenzel.

chapters.summary: Zhuang asks questions on behalf of Council Member Brooks-Powers about the digital divide report due January 2027, charter school IEP transfer rights, and 2K expansion plans. Officials confirm 10,000 additional 2K seats planned for next year and commit to walking parents through transfer rights.
chapters.title: CM Zhuang for Brooks-Powers on digital divide, charter IEP, and 2K expansion

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: I just want to ask a couple questions on behalf of Councilmember Brooks Powers, because she couldn't stay earlier.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: So last year, the council passed her bill, Local Law 16, that requires the DOE to issue a biannual report on students'access to the Internet and electronic devices, as well as provide recommendations for how to close the digital divide.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: The first report is due by January 2027.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: What progress can the department report on this study, and can you confirm that you will be able to meet the January 2027 deadline?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We'll have to get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm not sure we have time right now.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And then her second question was, we continue to hear concerns from parents with special needs children about how their kids enroll in charter schools and are forced to transition to public school after one month of enrollment because their charter school cannot provide the relevant IEP services.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: What is DOE doing to ensure parents are aware of their rights regarding student transfers and IEP services?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, obviously we want to serve our students with disabilities in public schools, of course.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And when our charter school colleagues don't meet that, have concerns, we...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We do have a mechanism for ensuring charter schools are paid for students with IEPs that they serve, but I want to turn it over to Christina to talk.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: Yeah, so as the LEA, we are responsible for special education IEP provision.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: So when that is not happening at a school and the child, you know, the school is saying that the child can't remain or they can't meet the child's needs, first of all, we look to partner with the school to see how we can work together to get those needs met.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: And if the family would like to leave, we walk the parent individually through the process of offering a public school placement.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: So you guys would walk the parents through their rights.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And I'm assuming there's ample translation services and interpretation.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: And her last question was, School District 27 in Queens is among the first districts to receive free 2K seats, which she's very excited about.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: How does the administration plan to build on this pilot expanding access across more districts?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sorry, what was the pilot?

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Oh, for the 2K?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Oh, 2K.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Yeah, in her district, yes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: So how do you plan to build on the pilot, expanding access across more districts?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As you know, this is the beginning first year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have 2,000 seats, and we'll be expanding next year to 12.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so, Simone, Deputy Chancellor, would you like to share more?

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: That's precisely right.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: Thank you, Chancellor.

utterances.speaker: Christina
utterances.text: We will be adding another.

utterances.speaker: Simone
utterances.text: 10,000 seats next year.

utterances.speaker: Simone
utterances.text: We have not confirmed the where and the who will be providing those, but we are in active conversations on planning out those specifics.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the class size exemption deal with the UFT providing up to $8,500 differentials. Officials explain the differential applies to space and hard-to-staff exemptions needed to reach 70% compliance, with pro-rating based on the number of over-cap classes taught.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on class size exemption differentials

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, thank you, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Class size.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Our understanding is that the timeline extension includes a deal with the UFT, which will provide differential payments of up to $8,500 per teachers for those whose classes receive approved space or hard staff exemptions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can you provide more details on the deal?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, so let's specifically speak to what this is, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So, yes, there was an adjustment.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, for example, in September, the goal is now to get to at least 70%.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And now there's a two-year extension to the law.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so the process of exemptions now includes differentials specifically as they relate to any exemption that we have that gets us to 70.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it's not as though you're going to give everyone an exemption who is teaching in a class that is out of compliance.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It is only in the event that you need that exemption to get to 70%.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That is when the differential applies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so I want to turn it over to Aaron to add anything else in terms of details.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, I think the only thing we would add is we're working to develop guidance on the approach to implementation, and then we'll share more details when we can.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So there are currently four exemption types for class size.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: However, only two have been identified for differentials.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are teachers in schools defined as over-enrolled in economic distress or special classes also included in the policy?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, in terms of determining the exemptions, that's when we would apply the, we would prioritize students in schools with a high economic need, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So the way it works is, first you have to

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: decide on, and we do this collaboratively with our partners, which classes will be, which exemptions you're going to use.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then when you apply the exemptions, you apply them prioritizing our students who are in an economic need and so on.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then you get to apply the differential after that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we do have space exemptions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: several other types of exemptions.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, and so we have the space exemption and the over-enrolled exemption, which we had agreement with the unions and was published in our plan in FY26.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: The differential is applying to the space and hard to staff, which is the third.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: The economic distress exemption is something we haven't utilized and I think is more applicable not to the individual schools, but to the system at large, as we have discussed it.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: But again, the three that we have and are talking about are the space, the hard to staff, and the over-enrolled.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And this is an exemption of up to $8,500.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So what's going to determine how much the differential is actually for?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The differential, if you're teaching, for example, for the entire year, you would get to 8,500 if you teach, let's say, in a class for half of that year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then as you reprogram and it's different, you would get half of the differential.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: And I think this is where we need to look a little bit more deeply and go into the data.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: And I think conceptually we're looking at it applying to the over-enrolled classes that the teacher is teaching or the classes above the statutory caps that the teacher is teaching.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: So at a high school level, it could be, say, two out of the five.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: And then there's a pro, we would do pro-rating at that point.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the SEED sensory program facing discontinuation without continued funding. Deputy Chancellor Foti reports 3,450 students currently enrolled, with 15,000 served over five years, and says DOE will pursue every avenue including the adopted budget process.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on SEED sensory program funding crisis

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It was asked for about the SEED, the sensory program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think you had testified 15,000 students use this weekend program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's been funded through a state grant for the past few years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In your written response to our questions following the preliminary budget, you had said, quote, without continued investment, the SEED program will be discontinued

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: in the 26-27 school year, and the loss of SEED would significantly impact students with intensive sensory needs and their families.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What would be the programmatic impact if this funding is not restored in fiscal 27?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And just to clarify, it's over the past five years, the 15,000 number.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So 15,000 kids have been served over the past five years.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I just want to correct for our audience.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you for the clarification.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, and currently 3,450 are enrolled as of today.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're going to pursue every avenue to try to get this funding.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, we've had initially this was funded through COVID stimulus funds, and then we were able to get the state grant for the past couple of years.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But we're still in the process of having conversations and obviously want to sustain the program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And you've made the request with OMB as well?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I believe those conversations are going to happen through the adopted budget process.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so similar to before, you've made the request and OMB has, well, I'll use the word rejected for now.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They said no, and until they say yes, the answer is no.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think I'm right.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the JFK campus Student Success Center at risk of losing funding, noting the campus experienced a student murder and the first ICE detention of a student. Gentes says principals opted out but DOE will extend for one more year, and funds can transfer to the Walton campus.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on JFK campus Student Success Center

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The JFK campus is a little low.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm now local council member Eric Dinowitz.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: JFK campus is at risk of losing their student success centers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I was asking about our student success centers before.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Now to remind you, JFK campus is one where a young boy was murdered just a few short months ago.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They faced the first

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The student at one of their schools on the JFK campus was the first one to be detained by ICE, and the Student Success Center funding is critical, and they are at risk of losing that funding.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What assurances can DOE provide that the Student Success Center at JFK will not be cut?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Dinowitz.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As you know, the Student Success Centers are really a partnership between our office at the Office of Student Pathways, the Student Success Coalition, but particularly

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: with our principals and school leaders.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so at the Ellis campus, which is one of the four at Kennedy, the principal actually made the decision that they did not think the Student Success Center was effective, and so we have been following their lead.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: However, and given that the other principals also did not want to continue with the program, that is the reason why it was going to leave that campus.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But given the late timing and when it's been happening, we are going to extend it for one other year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But the principals there, because this is a program that is really driven by school leaders, have actually said that it's not something that they're interested in keeping.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we should continue in having conversations with them at that particular campus.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The good news is that the funding actually does not go away for the CBO that's involved.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So as you know, it's Kingsbridge Center.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That the funds that Kennedy doesn't use can actually be transferred over to the Walton campus where the Kingsbridge folks also work with.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Two of my favorite schools.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In our favorite district.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Our favorite, that's right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I hear they have good people who used to work on those campuses.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I would note the information, it was me, I would note the information that I have is that it is not all of the principals who want to discontinue that, to the principals who have the programming due, but that there is a cost.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So this is a financial cost.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But we will continue the conversation, but just know that this was the organization that was there for the students after the murder of that young boy and that they were there

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: to organize the town hall around school safety where we had a hearing here about, and they're there for our students.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I would like to continue that conversation, but this is of importance to the students, and my understanding is that it is of importance to the principals on the campus.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I visited that campus, both when that incident happened and also visited.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: With the return of Dylan, I would say we'll look into it and see what we can do.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: One moment.

chapters.summary: Lee and chairs thank the Chancellor and team for staying beyond schedule to answer all questions. The Chancellor thanks the Council for partnership and notes orange and blue skies outside.
chapters.title: Closing remarks and thanks for DOE hearing

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, so I think that's it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I still can see, clearly, sort of, somewhat.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I just want to thank you all for spending, I know we went over time, but we really appreciate the fact that you guys stayed and answered all of our questions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So really, thank you so much for being such great partners with us.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Well, thank you very much for having us.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think we all can agree that there are orange and blue skies outside.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Go Knicks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you all so much.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Holy smokes.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: There's a whole crew back here.

summary: Joint FY27 Executive Budget Hearing covering CUNY and DOE, with Chairs Lee, Joseph, and Dinowitz questioning Chancellors Matos Rodriguez and Samuels on budget priorities, program funding, and agency operations.

CUNY portion focused on enrollment growth, student success programs (ACE, ASAP, Reconnect), capital needs, baselining requests, and federal research cut impacts across approximately 90 minutes of testimony and Q&A.

DOE portion covered procurement transparency disputes, $922M savings plan, special education program shortages, restorative justice cuts, class size compliance, early childhood expansion including 2K launch, and due process cost reduction strategies over approximately 4 hours of testimony and Q&A.
title: Committee on Education
